## 2.1 The Four Mandatory Properties of Any Valid Consensus Protocol

A protocol is *not* a consensus mechanism unless it satisfies all four of these properties simultaneously. Missing even one disqualifies it. KTU examiners consistently test these four properties (typical 7-mark question framing).

### Property 1 — **Agreement (Consistency)**
*No two honest nodes decide on different values.*

$$\forall i, j \in \text{Honest Nodes}: \text{decided}_i = \text{decided}_j$$

If Node A and Node B are both honest, after consensus completes they must hold the *exact same* block as the next ledger entry.

### Property 2 — **Validity (Non-Triviality)**
*The value decided upon must be a value actually proposed by some honest node.*

$$\text{decided} \in \{\text{proposed}_1, \text{proposed}_2, \dots, \text{proposed}_n\}$$

The protocol cannot magically invent a block; it can only choose among blocks that honest nodes have proposed. This prevents a "phantom block" attack.

### Property 3 — **Termination (Liveness)**
*Every honest node eventually decides on a value, in bounded time.*

$$\forall i \in \text{Honest Nodes}: \exists \, t_{\text{max}} \text{ such that } \text{decided}_i \text{ at time} \leq t_{\text{max}}$$

The protocol cannot hang forever. There must be a *worst-case* time bound (in PoW this is probabilistic; in PBFT it is deterministic).

### Property 4 — **Integrity (Byzantine Fault Tolerance)**
*If all honest nodes propose the same value, then that value is the one decided upon.*

$$\left(\forall i \in \text{Honest}: \text{proposed}_i = v\right) \implies \text{decided} = v$$

Combined with the BGP inequality, this gives the *fault tolerance threshold* of the system.

## 2.2 The FLP Impossibility Result (1985)

A critical theoretical result that every KTU 2024 student must memorize:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fischer, Lynch, and Paterson (FLP) Impossibility (1985):**
> In a *purely asynchronous* distributed system, **no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee all four properties** (Agreement, Validity, Termination) with even a *single* faulty (crash-failure) node.

**Implication for blockchain:** This is why *real* blockchain consensus protocols either (a) use *synchrony assumptions* (PoW, PoS), (b) use *randomization* (Algorand's cryptographic sortition), or (c) use *partial synchrony* (PBFT, Tendermint). They *circumvent* FLP, they do not *solve* it.

## 2.3 The CAP Theorem Context

For distributed ledgers, the **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000)** states that only **two of three** properties can be guaranteed simultaneously:

| Property | Meaning in Blockchain |
|---|---|
| **C**onsistency | Every node sees the same ledger at the same time |
| **A**vailability | Every request eventually receives a response |
| **P**artition Tolerance | System operates despite network splits |

Bitcoin chooses **AP** (eventual consistency, high availability). PBFT-based chains choose **CP** (strict consistency, may halt under partition). This tradeoff is exam-favorite.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **ALL formulas, inequalities, and bounds you need for a 14-mark ESE question on Consensus Mechanisms.** Tables below use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown rendering.

| # | Formula / Inequality | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | $n$ = total nodes, $f$ = Byzantine (malicious) nodes | Minimum node count for BFT |
| 2 | $f < n/3$ | Fault tolerance threshold | Validates BGP guarantee |
| 3 | $t_{\text{block}} \approx \dfrac{D}{\text{Hashrate}_{\text{network}}}$ | Block time ≈ difficulty / network hash rate | PoW scaling analysis |
| 4 | $\text{Safety} = \Pr[\text{fork accepted}] \propto e^{-c \cdot n \cdot q}$ | $q$ = attacker's hash fraction, $n$ = confirmations | 51% attack probability |
| 5 | $\text{Slashing Penalty}_{\text{PoS}} = \alpha \cdot \text{Stake}$ | $\alpha$ = penalty multiplier (e.g., 1.0x to 3.0x) | PoS economic security |
| 6 | $\text{Honest Majority} \Leftrightarrow \sum \text{Stake}_{\text{honest}} > \sum \text{Stake}_{\text{malicious}}$ | Stake-weighted voting | PoS safety bound |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tx}_{\text{block}}}{\text{Block Time}}$ | Transactions per second (TPS) | Layer-1 scaling |
| 8 | $\lambda_{\text{fork}} = q^2 \cdot \Delta t$ | Fork rate under selfish mining | Nakamoto consensus analysis |
| 9 | $\text{Energy}_{\text{PoW}} = \text{Hashrate} \times t \times E_{\text{per\,hash}}$ | Energy cost of PoW | Sustainability critique |
| 10 | $\text{Finality}_{\text{PBFT}} = 2 \cdot \Delta_{\text{network}} + 1 \text{ RTT}$ | Deterministic finality latency | BFT-class protocols |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Automotive Domain

For **PEAUT751** specifically, consensus serves three concrete automotive engineering purposes:

1. **Tamper-Proof Telemetry:** Autonomous vehicles log **~1 TB/day** of sensor data. Consensus on a permissioned blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) ensures that no OEM, insurer, or court can forge post-hoc odometer, brake, or ADAS data — critical for **product liability litigation** under emerging autonomous-vehicle laws (e.g., UN Regulation 157).

2. **OTA Update Provenance:** Over-the-air firmware updates touch millions of ECUs. A consensus-recorded hash of each firmware version, signed by the OEM and verified by a BFT quorum of service-center nodes, prevents **supply-chain ECU malware injection** (a documented attack vector in the 2015 Chrysler Jeep Cherokee recall).

3. **V2X Trust:** In Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication, a consensus layer (e.g., IOTA's Tangle, or a permissioned BFT chain) prevents a single compromised Road-Side Unit (RSU) from broadcasting fake "green light" or "collision imminent" messages to nearby vehicles.

## 2.6 The Taxonomy of Consensus Families (Survey Level)

For Module 1, KTU expects familiarity with the following four families. Each will be developed in later modules.

| Family | Representative Algorithm | Fault Model | Energy | Finality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Proof-based (Nakamoto)** | PoW (Bitcoin), PoS (Ethereum 2.0) | Crash + Sybil | High (PoW) / Low (PoS) | Probabilistic |
| **Classical BFT** | PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff | Byzantine (≤ f < n/3) | Low | Deterministic |
| **Delegated / Identity-based** | DPoS, PoA (VeChain, Hyperledger) | Honest majority of delegates | Very Low | Fast deterministic |
| **DAG-based** | Hashgraph, Tangle (IOTA) | Asynchronous BFT | Very Low | Transactional |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Byzantine Fault Tolerance Bound $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$

This is the **single most important derivation** in consensus theory. KTU examiners frequently award 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question for deriving or explaining this bound.

### Step 1 — State the Setup
We have a network of $n$ nodes, of which $f$ are **Byzantine (malicious / traitorous)** and the remaining $n - f$ are **honest**. The goal is for all honest nodes to reach identical decisions.

### Step 2 — The First Bound: The Honest Majority
For any meaningful consensus, we need strictly more honest than Byzantine:

$$n - f > f \quad \Rightarrow \quad n > 2f \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \geq 2f + 1$$

This is the *crash-fault* tolerance. A *crash-fault* node simply halts; it does not lie. So $2f+1$ is enough to tolerate $f$ *crashes*.

### Step 3 — The Second Bound: The Traitor's Worst-Case Message Confusion
Byzantine nodes are *worse* than crashed nodes — they can send **conflicting messages to different honest nodes simultaneously**. Consider the worst case: the Byzantine nodes coordinate to send **"vote YES"** to one group of honest nodes and **"vote NO"** to another group.

For the honest nodes to break this coordination, the two groups of honest nodes must each independently have *more* honest messages than Byzantine messages. The largest possible "confused group" has size $f + k$ (where $k$ is the count of honest nodes in one group), and the *other* group has size $n - (f + k)$. The honest count in the first group is $k$, the Byzantine count is $f$. The honest count in the second group is $n - (f + k)$, the Byzantine count is $f$.

### Step 4 — The Two Inequalities
For the *first* group to outvote the traitors:

$$k > f \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \geq f + 1$$

For the *second* group to outvote the traitors:

$$n - (f + k) > f \quad \Rightarrow \quad n - f - k > f \quad \Rightarrow \quad n > 2f + k$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Inequalities
Substitute the *worst-case* $k = f + 1$ into the second inequality:

$$n > 2f + (f + 1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad n > 3f + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \geq 3f + 1$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$\boxed{n_{\min} = 3f + 1}$$

This is the **minimum total number of nodes** required to tolerate $f$ Byzantine failures while guaranteeing all four consensus properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** To tolerate **f = 1** traitor, you need **n = 4** nodes. To tolerate **f = 10** traitors, you need **n = 31** nodes. Hyperledger Fabric's default ordering service typically uses 4–7 nodes, tolerating f = 1 or f = 2. This is why most enterprise BFT deployments are *small, permissioned, identity-verified* clusters — not public networks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bitcoin's Probabilistic Finality (Confirmations vs. Attack Probability)

In Nakamoto consensus (Bitcoin, Module 2 preview), finality is *probabilistic*, not deterministic. Each new block on top of a transaction *quadratically* reduces the probability of a successful double-spend fork.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables
- $q$ = fraction of total network hash power controlled by the **attacker**.
- $q_z$ = probability that the attacker catches up from $z$ blocks behind.
- $z$ = number of confirmations (blocks built on top of the transaction's block).

### Step 2 — The Attacker Catch-Up Probability
Following Nakamoto's 2008 whitepaper derivation:

$$q_z = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q > 0.5 \\[6pt] \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{q}{1-q}\right)^{z} \cdot \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{q}{1-q}\right)^{z_{\text{total}}}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{q}{1-q}\right)} & \text{if } q < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

Wait — the **corrected closed form** is:

$$q_z = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z + k - 1}{k} \cdot (1 - q)^z \cdot q^k$$

For large $z$ and $q \ll 0.5$, this approximates to:

$$q_z \approx \left(\frac{q}{1-q}\right)^{z}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
Suppose $q = 0.10$ (attacker has 10% of hash power), $z = 6$ confirmations (Bitcoin's default):

$$\frac{q}{1-q} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} \approx 0.1111$$

$$q_z \approx (0.1111)^{6} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000177\%$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion
Six confirmations under a 10% attacker reduce double-spend success probability to **~1.77 in a million**. This is the *practical* meaning of "6 confirmations = safe" in Bitcoin.

> [!NOTE]
> **For automotive use:** A 10-minute-block chain (Bitcoin-like) is far too slow for a vehicle collision-record. Layered architectures (e.g., Hyperledger Sawtooth for high-throughput vehicle telemetry + periodic anchoring to a PoW chain) combine deterministic BFT finality for individual events with global timestamping for forensic immutability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CAP Trade-off and the Consensus Latency Bound

In a *partially synchronous* BFT protocol (PBFT, Tendermint), the *latency to finality* is bounded by:

$$\text{Latency}_{\text{finality}} \geq 2 \cdot \Delta_{\text{network}} + \frac{T_{\text{propose}}}{n - f}$$

Where:
- $\Delta_{\text{network}}$ = worst-case network delay bound (partial synchrony assumption)
- $T_{\text{propose}}$ = time for the leader to propose a value
- $n - f$ = the honest quorum (since any $f$ may be silent)

**Why the factor of 2?** Consensus requires *one round* for the leader to propose and *one round* for all honest nodes to vote and acknowledge. Under asynchrony, you cannot shortcut this.

**Automotive implication:** For a 100-ms-latency V2X network, $\Delta_{\text{network}} \leq 20$ ms, so the minimum PBFT finality is **≥ 40 ms + proposal time**. This is feasible for collision warnings but tight for emergency braking.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Consensus Mechanism Selection for Automotive Use-Cases

| Automotive Use-Case | Latency Budget | Trust Model | Recommended Consensus | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OTA firmware provenance | Hours-Days | Permissioned (OEM + Tier-1 + Regulators) | **PBFT / Raft** (Fabric, Sawtooth) | Deterministic finality, low TPS ok |
| V2X collision warning | < 100 ms | Permissioned (RSU consortium) | **Hashgraph / DAG BFT** | High TPS, low finality |
| Insurance claim records | Days | Permissioned + Public anchor | **PoA (VeChainThor)** + periodic ETH anchor | Cheap, regulatory audit trail |
| Autonomous driving event log | Minutes | Hybrid (Vehicle OEM + Insurer) | **IOTA Tangle** | Zero-fee, parallel DAG |
| Vehicle identity (DID) | Seconds | Public-permissioned | **PoS (Ethereum 2.0)** | Decentralized trust for public identity |
| Car-sharing access control | Seconds | Permissioned (Fleet operator) | **PoA** | Identity-based, fast |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — The General Consensus Workflow in Blockchain

This Mermaid flow shows the *generic* lifecycle of a transaction from submission to finality through any consensus mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Transaction TX_i] --> B[Broadcast to P2P Network]
    B --> C[Each Node Validates TX_i Independently]
    C --> D{Valid Signature and State?}
    D -- No --> E[Drop TX_i - Reject]
    D -- Yes --> F[Add to Mempool - Pending Block Candidate]
    F --> G[Consensus Round Begins]
    G --> H[Leader Proposes Block B_k]
    H --> I[All Nodes Execute Consensus Algorithm]
    I --> J{Byzantine Quorum Reached?}
    J -- No --> K[Round Fails - Leader Rotation]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> L[Block B_k Committed to Local Chain]
    L --> M[State Update Propagated to Peers]
    M --> N[Finality Achieved - TX_i Immutable]
    N --> O[Smart Contract / Application Triggers]

    stepALabel[Transaction Initiation]:::infoStyle
    stepBLabel[Network Propagation]:::infoStyle
    stepCLabel[Independent Validation]:::infoStyle
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style G fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style J fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F
    style L fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style N fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    classDef infoStyle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The blue node (A) is the *entry point* — the user's signed transaction.
- The orange node (G) is where **consensus begins** — this is the focal point of Module 1.
- The red node (J) is the *Byzantine quorum check* — must reach $\geq 2f+1$ agreement.
- The green node (N) is the *finality* — the transaction is now cryptographically and economically irreversible.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Consensus Layer (Automotive Permissioned Blockchain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Application Layer (Off-Chain)"
        OBD[OBD-II Telemetry Reader]
        ECU[ECU State Logger]
        V2X[V2X Message Handler]
    end

    subgraph "Smart Contract Layer (On-Chain)"
        SC1[Vehicle Identity Registry]
        SC2[Firmware Provenance Contract]
        SC3[Collision Event Recorder]
    end

    subgraph "Consensus Layer (Module 1 Focus)"
        L1[Transaction Pool Mempool]
        L2[Block Proposer Leader]
        L3[Consensus Engine PBFT or PoA]
        L4[Fork Resolver]
        L5[Finality Gadget]
    end

    subgraph "Data Layer"
        D1[Distributed Ledger Chain]
        D2[World State Merkle Patricia Trie]
        D3[Block Index and Hash Chain]
    end

    subgraph "Network Layer P2P"
        N1[Gossip Protocol Engine]
        N2[TLS Encrypted Mesh]
        N3[Node Discovery and Membership]
    end

    OBD --> L1
    ECU --> L1
    V2X --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    N1 --> L1
    N2 --> N1
    N3 --> N1
    SC1 --> L1
    SC2 --> L1
    SC3 --> L1
```

**Interpretation for PEAUT751:**
- The **Consensus Layer** (highlighted central column) is the *sole arbiter* of what becomes a permanent record.
- Data flows **upward** from sensors → mempool → consensus → ledger.
- The **Mempool** is unordered; the **Block Proposer** orders it; the **Consensus Engine** validates and commits; the **Finality Gadget** declares the block irreversible.

---

## 4.3 Figure 3 — The Four Properties of Consensus as a State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Proposed: Client submits TX
    Proposed --> Validated: Honest nodes check signatures and state
    Validated --> Voted: Each honest node casts vote
    Voted --> QuorumReached: At least 2f plus 1 votes agree
    Voted --> RoundFailed: Less than 2f plus 1 agreement
    QuorumReached --> Committed: Block written to local chain
    Committed --> Finalized: Finality gadget confirms
    Finalized --> [*]: TX is immutable
    RoundFailed --> Proposed: New round with new leader
```

**Reading:** This is the *state diagram* of a single transaction's journey through consensus. The two absorbing "good" states are **Committed** and **Finalized**. The one non-absorbing "bad" state is **RoundFailed**, which loops back to **Proposed** for a fresh round (this is the *retry* mechanism that gives BFT its *liveness*).

---

## 4.4 Figure 4 — The Fault Tolerance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What fault model does your automotive blockchain face?] --> B{Network permissioned?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Maximum suspected malicious nodes}
    B -- No --> D{Public, trustless, open-membership}

    C -- Less than one-third --> E[Use Classical BFT - PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff]
    C -- Unknown or unbounded --> F[Use PoA with identity vetting and slashing]

    D -- Energy tolerable --> G[Use PoW - Nakamoto consensus, 51 percent resistant]
    D -- Energy not tolerable --> H[Use PoS - Ethereum 2.0, Cardano Ouroboros]

    E --> I[Finality: deterministic in 2 to 4 seconds]
    F --> J[Finality: deterministic in 1 to 2 seconds]
    G --> K[Finality: probabilistic, 6 blocks for safety]
    H --> L[Finality: economic, 12 to 64 epochs]
```

**Reading:** This is the *engineering selection guide* for choosing the right consensus mechanism for a given automotive use-case. KTU 2024 may ask students to *justify* a choice — this tree provides the logical structure of any valid justification.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Part A questions test *recall* and *definition*. Answers should be 4–6 sentences. Use the **exact syllabus terminology** wherever possible. Diagrams earn bonus marks.

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**
**Q: Define the term "consensus mechanism" in the context of blockchain technology. State any two purposes it serves.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
A consensus mechanism is a fault-tolerant protocol that enables all honest nodes in a decentralized peer-to-peer blockchain network to agree on a single, canonical state of the distributed ledger, even in the presence of faulty or malicious participants. The mechanism is enforced by mathematical rules rather than a central authority, making the system *trust-minimized*. It serves the following purposes: **(1) Decentralized Agreement:** it replaces a central authority by ensuring all honest nodes converge on the same version of the ledger, eliminating the single point of failure. **(2) Sybil Resistance:** it makes it economically or computationally infeasible for a single attacker to masquerade as thousands of nodes and outvote the honest network. *(Acceptable also: Fork Resolution, Byzantine Fault Tolerance, Incentive Compatibility, Finality — any two valid.)* **\[3 marks\]**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Adaptation]**
**Q: What is the Byzantine Generals Problem? How does it relate to blockchain consensus?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
The Byzantine Generals Problem (BGP), formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in 1982, is a logical dilemma that asks: *how can a group of distributed generals, communicating only by messengers, agree on a common plan of action when some of the generals may be traitors sending deliberately conflicting messages?* It is the foundational theoretical problem that blockchain consensus mechanisms are designed to solve. The "generals" represent network nodes, the "messengers" represent network packets, the "traitors" represent malicious or compromised nodes, and the "common plan of action" represents the next block to be appended. The BGP formally proves that consensus with Byzantine (malicious) failures is achievable only when the number of traitor nodes $f$ is strictly less than **one-third** of the total nodes $n$, i.e., the well-known $n \geq 3f + 1$ bound. **\[3 marks\]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR one 7-mark sub-part and one 7-mark sub-part. The internal choice offers *complete* alternative questions. Both alternatives are presented below.

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted for PEAUT751]**

> **Q: (a)** Explain the **four mandatory properties** that any valid blockchain consensus protocol must satisfy. State the formal mathematical condition for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** With a neat diagram, explain how the **Byzantine Generals Problem (BGP)** is solved using a $n = 3f+1$ node configuration. Derive the bound. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The four mandatory properties are:

1. **Agreement (Consistency):** All honest nodes must decide on the *same* value.
$$\forall i, j \in \text{Honest}: \text{decided}_i = \text{decided}_j$$
In blockchain terms, no two honest nodes may accept different forks of the chain.

2. **Validity (Non-Triviality):** The value decided must be one that was actually proposed by some honest node.
$$\text{decided} \in \{\text{proposed}_k \,\vert\, k \in \text{Honest}\}$$
This prevents a Byzantine leader from fabricating a phantom block.

3. **Termination (Liveness):** Every honest node must eventually decide, within a bounded time $t_{\max}$. The protocol cannot loop indefinitely. This is the property that FLP proved *impossible* in pure asynchrony.

4. **Integrity (BFT):** If all honest nodes propose the same value $v$, then the final decision must be $v$. This property, combined with the BFT threshold, ensures the protocol is fault-tolerant.

These four properties collectively define a *consensus protocol*; absence of any one disqualifies the algorithm. **\[7 marks: 1 mark per property + 3 marks for formal conditions / 1 mark for BFT context\]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Diagram (simplified, drawn on paper):** Show 4 generals — G1, G2, G3 (honest) and T (traitor). Each general sends a value to every other. Show the conflicting messages T sends (e.g., "ATTACK" to G1/G2, "RETREAT" to G3). Show that the 3 honest generals can each compute the same *majority* value (ATTACK: 2 votes) despite T's lies, and so all reach the same decision.

**Derivation of the bound $n \geq 3f + 1$:**

Step 1: For honest majority, $n - f > f \Rightarrow n \geq 2f + 1$. This handles *crash faults*.

Step 2: For *Byzantine* (adversarial) faults, the traitor can send *different* messages to different honest nodes. The worst case partitions the honest nodes into two confused groups. The largest possible "confused group" includes all $f$ traitors plus $k$ honest nodes; the *other* group has $n - (f+k)$ honest nodes.

Step 3: For each honest group to outvote the traitors locally: $k > f$ and $n - f - k > f$.

Step 4: Setting $k = f + 1$ (worst case) into the second inequality:

$$n > 2f + k = 2f + (f+1) = 3f + 1$$

$$\boxed{n \geq 3f + 1}$$

**Interpretation:** With $n=4$ nodes and $f=1$ traitor, all 3 honest nodes can reach the same decision by majority voting, regardless of the traitor's conflicting messages. **\[7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 4 marks for derivation + 1 mark for interpretation\]**

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q: (a)** Discuss the **role of consensus in automotive blockchain applications**. Identify three concrete use-cases and justify the choice of consensus mechanism for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** Explain the **FLP Impossibility Result** and the **CAP Theorem**. How do real-world blockchain consensus mechanisms *circumvent* FLP while operating under CAP constraints? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Consensus is the *sine qua non* of automotive blockchain applications because no single entity (OEM, insurer, regulator, court) can be allowed to unilaterally alter vehicle data. Three concrete use-cases:

1. **OTA Firmware Provenance (Over-the-Air Updates):** Millions of ECUs receive software updates. A malicious or buggy firmware version, once deployed, could endanger lives. The OEM, Tier-1 suppliers, and regulators run a **permissioned BFT chain** (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric with PBFT). Each firmware artifact's cryptographic hash is recorded as a transaction; BFT consensus with $f < n/3$ tolerance ensures that no single rogue party (not even a compromised Tier-1) can repudiate a published firmware. **Justification:** deterministic finality in seconds, identity-based trust, low TPS requirement.

2. **V2X Collision Avoidance:** Vehicles exchange safety-critical messages (e.g., hard-braking warnings) with other vehicles and Road-Side Units. A false "no obstacle" message could cause a fatal accident. **DAG-based consensus (Hashgraph, IOTA Tangle)** is preferred because it provides high TPS, low latency, and *asynchronous* BFT — the exact circumvention of FLP. **Justification:** sub-100ms latency requirement, parallel structure suits many concurrent vehicles, fee-less for micro-messages.

3. **Insurance Claim Records (Accident Liability):** When a collision occurs, immutable evidence of vehicle speed, brake application, steering angle, and timestamp must be recorded for liability disputes. **Proof-of-Authority (PoA)** chains (e.g., VeChainThor) are run by a consortium of insurers, OEMs, and police. PoA provides fast deterministic finality with low energy and is identity-vetted, so no anonymous Sybil attack is possible. **Justification:** low TPS, regulated trust model, energy-efficient. **\[7 marks: 1 mark for role-of-consensus intro + 2 marks per use-case\]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**FLP Impossibility (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson — 1985):**
In a *purely asynchronous* distributed system (where message delays are unbounded), **no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee all three properties** of Agreement, Validity, and Termination simultaneously — even with a *single* crash-failure node. The proof constructs a configuration in which the protocol is forced to either (a) violate termination by looping forever, or (b) violate agreement by splitting. FLP is a *negative* result that defines a hard theoretical limit.

**CAP Theorem (Brewer — 2000):**
In any distributed data store subject to network partition (P), the system can guarantee only **two** of the three properties: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)**. Since partitions are inevitable in real networks, every blockchain effectively chooses between **CP** (strict consistency, may halt) and **AP** (eventual consistency, always available).

**How real consensus protocols circumvent FLP + respect CAP:**

| Circumvention Strategy | Example Algorithm | CAP Choice | How FLP is Bypassed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weak synchrony assumption (bounded delay) | PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff | **CP** | Assumes partial synchrony → bounded rounds → termination |
| Randomization + cryptographic sortition | Algorand, Ouroboros (PoS) | **AP** | Random leader selection → probabilistic termination |
| Computational puzzle (PoW) | Bitcoin, Ethereum 1.0 | **AP** | Probabilistic finality, liveness from block production |
| Identity-based + Slashing | PoA, DPoS | **CP** | Synchronous committee, immediate finality |

In short, real protocols *weaken* one of FLP's assumptions (pure asynchrony or deterministic outcome) to escape the impossibility. CAP forces a tradeoff that every blockchain designer must explicitly choose. **\[7 marks: 2 marks FLP + 2 marks CAP + 3 marks circumvention table\]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Consensus Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "consensus mechanism" with "consensus algorithm":** In KTU 2024, *mechanism* refers to the *class* (PoW, PoS, BFT), while *algorithm* refers to a *specific instance* (Bitcoin's Nakamoto, Ethereum's Casper). Using them interchangeably loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting the FLP caveat:** Stating "consensus is always possible" without acknowledging FLP and the synchrony assumption loses 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Skipping the $n \geq 3f+1$ derivation steps:** Examiners award only 1 mark for the *statement* of the bound but 3–4 marks for the *derivation*. Memorize the four algebraic steps.
> 4. **Confusing BFT with crash fault tolerance:** Crash fault tolerance needs only $n \geq 2f+1$. Byzantine fault tolerance needs $n \geq 3f+1$. Mixing these up is a very common and *heavily penalized* error.
> 5. **Ignoring automotive context in PEAUT751:** Even in a theoretical question, mentioning *one* concrete automotive application (OTA, V2X, insurance) earns a bonus mark. The examiner is testing both blockchain knowledge *and* automotive relevance.
> 6. **Stating "consensus = voting":** Voting alone is not consensus. You must add the *fault tolerance threshold* and the *finality property*. Voting without these is incomplete.
> 7. **Saying "51% attack" when meaning "Byzantine majority":** A 51% attack is a *specific* PoW term. BFT requires 2/3 honest supermajority. Use the correct vocabulary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Consensus Mechanisms — Purpose of Consensus in Blockchain"**

- [ ] **Core definition:** Consensus mechanism = fault-tolerant protocol that lets *all honest* nodes agree on the *single* canonical ledger state, *without* a central authority.
- [ ] **Four mandatory properties:** **A**greement, **V**alidity, **T**ermination, **I**ntegrity (mnemonic: **AVTI**).
- [ ] **Byzantine Generals Problem (BGP, 1982):** foundational theoretical problem. Lamport, Shostak, Pease. Traitor generals sending conflicting messages.
- [ ] **BFT Bound:** $n \geq 3f + 1$, i.e., $f < n/3$. **Crash fault tolerance** is $n \geq 2f+1$ (different bound — do not confuse).
- [ ] **FLP Impossibility (1985):** Pure asynchrony + deterministic + even 1 crash fault = no consensus. Real protocols *weaken* asynchrony via (a) partial synchrony, (b) randomization, (c) probabilistic finality.
- [ ] **CAP Theorem:** Of C, A, P — only 2 of 3. Bitcoin = AP. PBFT chains = CP. DAGs typically = AP.
- [ ] **Five purposes of consensus:** Decentralized Agreement, Fork Resolution, BFT, Sybil Resistance, Incentive Compatibility.
- [ ] **Nakamoto (PoW) finality is probabilistic:** catch-up probability $q_z \approx (q/(1-q))^z$ for attacker fraction $q$, confirmations $z$. 6 confirmations at $q=0.10$ ≈ $1.77 \times 10^{-6}$ risk.
- [ ] **BFT (PBFT) finality is deterministic:** in $2\Delta_{\text{network}} + 1$ RTT. Used in Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint, HotStuff.
- [ ] **Automotive use-cases:** OTA firmware (BFT/PoA), V2X collision avoidance (DAG/Hashgraph), insurance liability (PoA consortium), vehicle DID (PoS public).
- [ ] **Sybil Resistance methods:** PoW = energy cost; PoS = stake cost; PoA = identity verification; DPoS = delegation with reputation.
- [ ] **Energy metric:** PoW energy = Hashrate × time × energy-per-hash. Often cited as the *Achilles heel* of PoW for automotive/IoT contexts.
- [ ] **Incentive compatibility:** Honest mining = reward. Dishonest mining = slashing (PoS) or wasted energy (PoW). Game theory aligns rational self-interest with network honesty.
- [ ] **Difference between consensus and atomic commit:** Consensus = agreement in *Byzantine* setting with arbitrary failures. Atomic commit (2PC, 3PC) = agreement in *crash-fail* setting. The latter is *easier* but insufficient for trustless blockchains.
- [ ] **Finality types:** *Probabilistic* (Nakamoto), *Deterministic instant* (PBFT), *Economic* (PoS with slashing), *Absolute* (Tendermint single-round).
- [ ] **Threshold signatures vs. voting:** Modern BFT (HotStuff, Tendermint) uses threshold BLS signatures to collapse voting overhead — an exam bonus point.
- [ ] **For PEAUT751 specifically:** Always link consensus properties to *automotive* requirements (latency, throughput, trust model, regulatory audit) — never give a generic blockchain answer in this course.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Consensus Mechanisms: Purpose of Consensus in Blockchain**
> *Next Module Preview: Module 2 — Detailed study of Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), and Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Consensus Mechanisms (Basic Ideas Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Consensus Mechanisms (Basic Ideas Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Consensus Mechanism** is a fault-tolerant protocol used in distributed blockchain networks to achieve agreement on a single data value (such as the next block to be appended) among a group of distributed, potentially untrusted nodes. It is the procedural backbone that ensures all participating nodes maintain an identical, tamper-proof copy of the distributed ledger.

In the context of **PEAUT751 – Block Chain in Automotive Industry**, consensus mechanisms determine *which node* gets the right to write the next block containing vehicle telemetry, Over-The-Air (OTA) updates, smart contract executions for autonomous vehicles, or supply-chain provenance data for spare parts.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Village Council"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a village of 500 households that must collectively decide whether to repair the road. No single household is trusted more than the others. So the village adopts a rule:
> - **Option A (PoW):** The person who solves a complex math puzzle first gets to propose the decision.
> - **Option B (PoS):** The person who has staked (deposited) the most money gets to propose, but loses the deposit if they lie.
> - **Option C (PoA):** A pre-approved set of village elders (known identities) votes on the decision.
> - **Option D (PBFT):** The proposal is discussed in rounds, and if 2/3 of the village agrees, the decision is final.

Replace the village with a **blockchain network of vehicles, OEMs, dealers, and regulators** and the road-repair with **validating a new block** — that is exactly what a consensus mechanism does.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in the Automotive Domain

| Automotive Use-Case | Required Consensus Property |
|---|---|
| OTA firmware updates across millions of ECUs | High throughput, low latency |
| Vehicle identity & V2X communication | Fast finality, energy efficiency |
| Spare-parts provenance (anti-counterfeit) | Permissioned, identity-based trust |
| Autonomous vehicle decision logging | Byzantine fault tolerance |
| Carbon-credit & emission data | Immutability & auditability |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Public blockchains (Bitcoin, Ethereum) favor **Proof of Work / Proof of Stake** for maximum decentralization. **Automotive consortium blockchains** (e.g., MOBI, IOTA, Hyperledger Fabric) favor **Proof of Authority, PBFT, or Raft** for speed and known participants.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Consensus Mechanisms

```
                        CONSENSUS MECHANISMS
                                 |
        ┌────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
        |                        |                        |
   PUBLIC/PERMISSIONLESS   CONSORTIUM/PERMISSIONED   HYBRID
        |                        |                        |
   • PoW                  • PBFT                  • DPoS
   • PoS                  • PoA                   • PoH
   • PoH (Proof of History)• Raft                 
```

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Type

#### A. Proof of Work (PoW) — *Used in: Bitcoin, Ethereum (pre-Merge)*
- **Idea:** Miners compete to solve a computationally hard cryptographic puzzle (finding a nonce such that `Hash(Block Header) < Target`).
- **"Why it works":** Solving the puzzle costs real electricity & hardware, so cheating is economically irrational.
- **"How it works":**
  1. Miners collect pending transactions into a candidate block.
  2. They vary a counter called the **nonce**.
  3. They hash the block header repeatedly until the hash falls below a target value (difficulty).
  4. The first miner to succeed broadcasts the block; others verify and append.
- **Automotive suitability:** ❌ Low — energy-intensive and slow (~7 TPS in Bitcoin).

#### B. Proof of Stake (PoS) — *Used in: Ethereum 2.0, Cardano, Polkadot*
- **Idea:** Validators are *selected* to propose blocks in proportion to the number of coins they have **staked (locked)** as collateral.
- **"Why it works":** A validator that approves a fraudulent block loses its stake — a financial penalty called **"slashing."**
- **"How it works":**
  1. Validators lock up tokens (e.g., 32 ETH on Ethereum 2.0).
  2. A pseudo-random selection algorithm picks one validator per slot.
  3. Other validators *attest* to the block's validity.
  4. If 2/3 attest, the block is finalized.
- **Automotive suitability:** ⚠️ Medium — better energy profile, but still permissionless.

#### C. Proof of Authority (PoA) — *Used in: VeChain, POA Network, some MOBI pilots*
- **Idea:** Block validators are **pre-approved, known, reputable identities** (e.g., OEMs, Tier-1 suppliers, regulators). Identity IS the stake.
- **"Why it works":** Identities are public, so misbehaviour ruins reputation — a powerful economic deterrent.
- **"How it works":**
  1. A governance council approves a fixed set of **authorities** (typically 5–25).
  2. Authorities take turns producing blocks in a round-robin / leader-election manner.
  3. Other authorities verify and sign.
  4. If an authority signs a bad block, it is removed (and possibly legally liable).
- **Automotive suitability:** ✅ **Excellent** — high throughput, low latency, ideal for consortium of OEM + suppliers + regulators.

#### D. Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — *Used in: EOS, Tron, BitShares*
- **Idea:** Token holders **vote** to elect a small fixed number of *delegates* (witnesses/producers) who run consensus on their behalf.
- **"Why it works":** Voting creates a competitive marketplace; bad delegates are voted out.
- **"How it works":**
  1. Stake-holders cast votes proportional to their token weight.
  2. Top N (e.g., N = 21 in EOS) become block producers.
  3. Producers take turns in fixed round-robin order.
  4. If a producer misses their slot, they are skipped and may be voted out.
- **Automotive suitability:** ⚠️ Medium — fast but semi-centralized.

#### E. Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) — *Used in: Hyperledger Fabric, Stellar, Zilliqa*
- **Idea:** Tolerate up to `f` malicious (Byzantine) nodes out of a total of `3f + 1` nodes while still reaching agreement.
- **"Why it works":** Through multi-round voting, honest nodes converge on the same decision even if some lie.
- **"How it works":**
  1. A **client** sends a request to a **primary (leader)** node.
  2. The primary **pre-prepares** a message and broadcasts.
  3. **Backup** nodes **prepare** by broadcasting their agreement.
  4. Once a node receives `2f + 1` prepare messages, it sends a **commit** message.
  5. After `2f + 1` commit messages, the request is executed and the reply sent to the client.
- **Formula:** Network tolerates `f` faulty nodes if total nodes `N = 3f + 1`.
- **Automotive suitability:** ✅ **Excellent** — deterministic finality, used in Hyperledger-based vehicle supply chains.

#### F. Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET) — *Used in: Hyperledger Sawtooth (Intel SGX-based)*
- **Idea:** Each validator waits a *random* amount of time inside a trusted hardware enclave (Intel SGX); the one whose timer expires first wins the block.
- **"Why it works":** The random wait is cryptographically verified by the hardware, making it both fair and energy-efficient.
- **Automotive suitability:** ⚠️ Medium — requires trusted hardware (SGX/TPM) inside vehicles.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Mechanism | Selection Criterion | Reward / Penalty | Energy Use | Finality | Fault Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PoW | Highest hash power | Block reward | Very High | Probabilistic | < 50% hash |
| PoS | Stake weight (random) | Tx fees / **Slashing** | Low | Probabilistic / Economic | < 33% stake |
| PoA | Pre-approved identity | Reputation loss | Very Low | Instant | < N/2 |
| DPoS | Stake-weighted vote | Block reward / Removal | Low | Instant | < N/2 |
| PBFT | Leader + quorum vote | View-change | Very Low | **Deterministic** | `f < N/3` |
| PoET | Random trusted timer | Block reward | Very Low | Probabilistic | < N/2 |

**Key Mathematical Relationship for PBFT:**

$$
N_{min} = 3f + 1
$$

Where $N_{min}$ is the minimum number of nodes and $f$ is the number of tolerated Byzantine (faulty/malicious) nodes.

**Difficulty Check for PoW (target conversion):**

$$
\text{Target} = \frac{\text{MaxTarget}}{\text{Difficulty}}
$$

Where `MaxTarget` is a fixed constant (e.g., `0x00000000FFFF0000...` in Bitcoin), and `Difficulty` adjusts every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) to keep block time near **600 seconds**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hyperledger Fabric (PBFT/BFT variants):** Deployed by **BMW, Ford, and Renault** in the **MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative)** for vehicle identity and supply-chain traceability.
- **IOTA (Tangle / Coordicide):** DAG-based feeless consensus for V2X data exchange between vehicles.
- **Ethereum PoS:** Tested for **electric vehicle charging settlements** between vehicles, charging stations, and grid operators.
- **VeChain (PoA):** Used by **BMW (VerifyCar)** for odometer-fraud prevention across European used-car markets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following analytical reasoning develops the **PBFT fault-tolerance bound** and the **PoW difficulty-target relationship** — the two most commonly asked derivations in KTU valuation scripts for this module.

### 3.1 Derivation: PBFT Minimum Node Count for `f` Faulty Nodes

**Given:**
- A distributed network of $N$ nodes.
- Up to $f$ nodes may behave maliciously (Byzantine fault).
- The remaining $N - f$ nodes are honest.
- Honest nodes must still reach agreement on the next block.

**Step 1 — Quorum requirement:**

For two messages to be proven identical, the intersection of any two quorums must contain at least one honest node. This is the *Quorum Intersection Property* of Byzantine systems.

$$
Q_1 \cap Q_2 \ge 1 \quad \text{(honest node in common)}
$$

**Step 2 — Size of each quorum:**

For safety, the system waits for $2f + 1$ matching responses before committing (overlap = $2f + 1 - f = f + 1 \ge 1$ honest node).

$$
Q = 2f + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Total nodes required:**

Since there are $f$ faulty nodes that may *not* respond honestly, the network must have at least:

$$
N \ge Q + f = (2f + 1) + f
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
N \ge 3f + 1
$$

**Step 5 — Practical example:**

For $f = 1$ faulty node (single vehicle acting maliciously), the minimum network size is:

$$
N = 3(1) + 1 = 4 \text{ nodes}
$$

For $f = 3$ faulty nodes, the minimum is:

$$
N = 3(3) + 1 = 10 \text{ nodes}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{min} = 3f + 1}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Bitcoin's PoW Difficulty Adjustment

**Given (Bitcoin protocol rules):**
- Target block time: `T = 600 seconds` (10 minutes).
- Adjustment period: `P = 2016` blocks.
- Maximum target (easiest): `MaxTarget = 0x00000000FFFF0000...` (a 256-bit number).
- Actual time taken for the last 2016 blocks: `ActualTime`.

**Step 1 — Calculate actual time elapsed:**

$$
\text{ActualTime} = \text{TimeStamp}(Block_{2016}) - \text{TimeStamp}(Block_0)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute adjustment ratio:**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{ActualTime}}{T \times P}
$$

**Step 3 — Clamp ratio to prevent extreme jumps (max 4× change):**

$$
\text{Ratio}_{clamped} = \max(0.25, \min(4, \text{Ratio}))
$$

**Step 4 — New target value:**

$$
\text{NewTarget} = \frac{\text{OldTarget}}{\text{Ratio}_{clamped}}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent difficulty:**

$$
\text{NewDifficulty} = \frac{\text{MaxTarget}}{\text{NewTarget}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NewTarget} = \frac{\text{OldTarget} \times T \times P}{\text{ActualTime}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** If miners collectively find blocks *faster* than 10 min, `ActualTime < T × P`, so `NewTarget` shrinks → difficulty rises. If blocks are *slower*, difficulty falls. This self-regulation is the essence of PoW stability.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | PoW | PoS | PoA | PBFT | DPoS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Node Identity | Anonymous | Pseudo-anonymous | **Known & KYC'd** | **Known & KYC'd** | Pseudo-anonymous |
| Throughput (TPS) | 7 (BTC) | 30–1000 (ETH2) | 1000+ | 1000–10 000 | 1000–4000 |
| Energy per tx | ~700 kWh | ~0.03 kWh | Negligible | Negligible | Negligible |
| Finality | ~6 blocks (~60 min) | ~12 min | Instant | **Deterministic (< 1 s)** | ~3 s |
| Sybil Resistance | Computational cost | Economic cost | **Identity cost** | Membership control | Vote + stake |
| Best for Automotive | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Consensus Flow (Block-Acceptance Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pending Transactions Pool] --> B[Validator/ Miner Selection]
    B --> C[Block Construction]
    C --> D[Consensus Protocol Execution]
    D --> E{2/3 Agreement Reached?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Block Added to Chain]
    E -- No --> G[Block Rejected / View Change]
    F --> H[Broadcast to All Nodes]
    H --> I[State Update in Each Node]
    I --> J[Transaction Confirmed]
```

### 4.2 PBFT Round-by-Round Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Primary as Primary Node
    participant B1 as Backup 1
    participant B2 as Backup 2
    participant B3 as Backup 3

    Client->>Primary: Request
    Primary->>B1: Pre-Prepare
    Primary->>B2: Pre-Prepare
    Primary->>B3: Pre-Prepare
    B1->>Primary: Prepare
    B2->>Primary: Prepare
    B3->>Primary: Prepare
    Primary->>B1: Commit
    Primary->>B2: Commit
    Primary->>B3: Commit
    B1->>Client: Reply
    B2->>Client: Reply
    B3->>Client: Reply
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Automotive Consortium Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Consortium_Layer
        OEM[OEM Authority Node]
        SUP[Supplier Authority Node]
        REG[Regulator Authority Node]
        INS[Insurance Authority Node]
    end

    subgraph Consensus_Engine
        SEL[Leader Election - PoA Round Robin]
        VAL[Block Validation - PBFT Quorum Check]
        VOTE[Commit Voting - 2f+1 Threshold]
    end

    subgraph Ledger_Layer
        LEDGER[Permissioned Distributed Ledger]
    end

    OEM --> SEL
    SUP --> SEL
    REG --> SEL
    INS --> SEL
    SEL --> VAL
    VAL --> VOTE
    VOTE --> LEDGER
    LEDGER --> OEM
    LEDGER --> SUP
    LEDGER --> REG
    LEDGER --> INS
```

### 4.4 Comparative Decision Tree — Which Consensus for Which Use-Case?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Blockchain Project] --> Q1{Network Open or Closed?}
    Q1 -- Public --> Q2{Need Maximum Decentralization?}
    Q1 -- Consortium / Permissioned --> Q3{Need Instant Finality?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PoW[Use PoW or PoS]
    Q2 -- No --> PoS2[Use PoS or DPoS]
    Q3 -- Yes --> PBFT2[Use PBFT or PoA]
    Q3 -- No --> PoET2[Use PoET or PoA]
    PoW --> App1[Bitcoin, Public Crypto]
    PoS2 --> App2[Ethereum 2.0, Cardano]
    PBFT2 --> App3[Hyperledger Fabric, MOBI Vehicle Chain]
    PoET2 --> App4[Supply Chain Traceability, Sawtooth]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** In valuation, always pair a written answer with a labelled diagram of the consensus flow. Examiners allocate **1–2 extra marks** (out of 14) for clear, well-labelled block diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
**Define the term "consensus mechanism" in the context of blockchain technology. Why is it necessary in a distributed automotive ledger?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *consensus mechanism* is a fault-tolerant protocol that enables all nodes in a distributed blockchain network to agree on the validity and order of transactions, ensuring a single, consistent state of the ledger (1 mark). In automotive applications, vehicles, OEMs, suppliers, and regulators operate as separate, untrusted nodes (1 mark). Without consensus, malicious or faulty nodes could inject false telemetry, forged service records, or fraudulent OTA updates. Consensus provides **Byzantine fault tolerance, immutability, and trustless agreement** even when some participants act maliciously (1 mark).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`**
**List any three types of consensus mechanisms and state one automotive-relevant application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Proof of Authority (PoA)** → Used in consortium vehicle-identity blockchains where known OEMs and regulators act as authorities. (1 mark)
2. **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** → Deployed in Hyperledger Fabric for tracking spare-parts provenance across multi-tier supplier networks. (1 mark)
3. **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** → Suitable for electric-vehicle charging networks where charging stations vote to elect validators for settlement. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Part B typically allows an *internal choice* — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for parallel practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **Proof of Work (PoW)** consensus mechanism with a neat block diagram. Discuss why it is **not preferred** for automotive IoT applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Working of PoW (5 marks for explanation + 1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for automotive critique):*

1. **Transaction Pooling:** Pending vehicle transactions (e.g., toll payments, telemetry) are gathered by miner nodes into a candidate block. (1 mark)
2. **Hash Puzzle Construction:** Each miner constructs a block header containing the previous block's hash, a Merkle root of transactions, a timestamp, and a variable counter called the *nonce*. (1 mark)
3. **Iterative Hashing:** Miners repeatedly hash the block header (typically with SHA-256 double-hashing in Bitcoin) by varying the nonce, searching for a hash value less than the current network target. (1 mark)
4. **Block Broadcast:** The first miner to find a valid hash broadcasts the block to the network. (1 mark)
5. **Verification & Append:** Other nodes verify the hash trivially (one hash call) and append the block to their copy of the chain; the winning miner receives a block reward. (1 mark)

*Why PoW is unsuitable for automotive IoT (2 marks):*
- **High energy consumption** makes it impractical for resource-constrained ECUs and battery-powered vehicles. (1 mark)
- **Low throughput** (~7 TPS in Bitcoin, ~15–30 TPS in pre-Merge Ethereum) cannot support the high-frequency V2X and telemetry data generated by modern vehicles. (1 mark)
- (Diagrammatic representation: Hash-puzzle loop with nonce variation, broadcast, verification, reward — 1 mark.)

---

**(b)** Describe the **Proof of Stake (PoS)** mechanism. Compare it with PoW on the basis of energy consumption, attack cost, and finality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Proof of Stake Explanation (4 marks):*
1. Validators lock up a stake (e.g., 32 ETH on Ethereum 2.0) in a smart contract as collateral. (1 mark)
2. A pseudo-random selection algorithm (often using VRF — Verifiable Random Function) picks one validator per slot to propose the next block. (1 mark)
3. Other validators *attest* to the block's correctness by signing it. (1 mark)
4. If 2/3 of the total staked amount attests, the block is *justified* and eventually *finalized* after two epochs (~12.8 minutes on Ethereum). A validator that signs contradictory blocks is **slashed** (loses part or all of their stake). (1 mark)

*Comparison Table (3 marks — 1 each):*

| Parameter | PoW | PoS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Consumption | Extremely high (entire mining farms) | ~99.95% lower (no mining, only signatures) |
| Attack Cost | Need > 51% of global hash power (~$10 B+ hardware) | Need > 51% of total staked value (slashed if caught) |
| Finality | Probabilistic (~6 confirmations ≈ 60 min) | Economic finality (~12.8 min, deterministic after finality) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 Choice Pattern]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)** consensus mechanism. Derive the relation `N = 3f + 1` for the minimum number of nodes required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*PBFT Phases (4 marks — 1 per phase):*
1. **Request:** A client sends the request to the *primary* (leader) node. (1 mark)
2. **Pre-Prepare:** The primary assigns a sequence number and broadcasts a *pre-prepare* message to all backups. (1 mark)
3. **Prepare:** Each backup verifies and broadcasts a *prepare* message to all nodes. (1 mark)
4. **Commit:** When a node receives `2f + 1` matching prepare messages, it broadcasts a *commit*. After `2f + 1` commits, the request is executed and a reply is sent to the client. (1 mark)

*Derivation of `N = 3f + 1` (3 marks):*
- **Quorum intersection requirement:** Two majorities must intersect in at least one honest node → each quorum must have at least `2f + 1` nodes. (1 mark)
- **Total required:** `N ≥ (2f + 1) + f = 3f + 1`. (1 mark)
- **Example:** For `f = 1` malicious node, minimum nodes `N = 4`; honest nodes = 3 (which is `2f + 1 = 3` → majority). (1 mark)

(Diagrammatic representation of PBFT phases: 1 mark for labelled sequence diagram in the answer script.)

---

**(b)** Explain the **Proof of Authority (PoA)** and **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** mechanisms. Why is PoA considered well-suited for automotive consortium blockchains? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Proof of Authority (3 marks):*
1. A governing council pre-approves a set of *authorities* whose real-world identities are publicly known and KYC-verified (e.g., OEM representatives, Tier-1 suppliers, regulators). (1 mark)
2. Authorities take turns producing blocks in a deterministic round-robin order. (1 mark)
3. Misbehaviour (signing invalid blocks, double-signing) results in the authority being removed from the validator set and losing its hard-earned reputation. (1 mark)

*Delegated Proof of Stake (2 marks):*
1. Token holders cast stake-weighted votes to elect a small set of *delegates* (e.g., 21 in EOS, 27 in Tron). (1 mark)
2. The elected delegates produce blocks in round-robin; if a delegate misses a slot, they are skipped and may be voted out in the next cycle. (1 mark)

*Why PoA suits automotive consortium chains (2 marks):*
1. **Known, trusted participants** — OEMs, suppliers, and regulators are pre-vetted, eliminating the need for energy-intensive mining or large stake pools. (1 mark)
2. **High throughput and instant finality** — ideal for high-frequency vehicle data such as OTA updates, telemetry logging, and supply-chain events. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (as flagged by KTU senior examiners):**
> 1. **Confusing PoW with PoS** — Many students incorrectly state that PoS *also* involves solving puzzles. It does NOT; it uses stake-weighted random selection. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Skipping the derivation** — In PBFT/PoW numericals, writing the final formula `N = 3f + 1` without showing the quorum-intersection step leads to **partial marking** only. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the automotive angle** — Generic blockchain answers (Bitcoin, Ethereum) score *less* than answers that tie mechanisms to **vehicle data, OTA, or supply-chain** use-cases. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 4. **No diagram / unclear diagram** — A 14-mark Part B answer without a labelled block/sequence diagram typically loses 1–2 marks even if the text is correct.
> 5. **Mixing up "deterministic" vs "probabilistic" finality** — PBFT and PoA give *deterministic* finality; PoW and PoS give *probabilistic/economic* finality. Examiners treat this distinction as a critical concept.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Consensus Mechanisms (Basic Ideas):**
> - A **consensus mechanism** is a fault-tolerant agreement protocol for distributed nodes.
> - **PoW** = computational puzzle; high security, very high energy; used in Bitcoin.
> - **PoS** = stake-weighted selection + slashing; energy-efficient; used in Ethereum 2.0.
> - **PoA** = known-identity validators; ideal for **automotive consortium** chains (e.g., MOBI).
> - **DPoS** = stake-weighted voting elects a small set of delegates; fast but semi-centralized.
> - **PBFT** = multi-round voting; **deterministic** finality; `N_min = 3f + 1`; used in Hyperledger Fabric.
> - **PoET** = random trusted-hardware timer; energy-efficient; requires Intel SGX/TPM.
> - **Difficulty in PoW** adjusts every 2016 blocks to maintain ~10 min block time.
> - **Finality types:** *Probabilistic* (PoW, PoS) vs *Deterministic* (PBFT, PoA).
> - **Automotive use-case matrix:**
>   - Vehicle identity / V2X → PBFT or PoA
>   - OTA firmware updates → PoA
>   - Spare-parts provenance → PBFT (Hyperledger)
>   - Public charging settlement → DPoS or PoS
> - **Formula to memorize cold:** $N_{min} = 3f + 1$ and $\text{NewTarget} = \text{OldTarget} \times \frac{T \times P}{\text{ActualTime}}$.
> - Always **pair** your answer with a **labelled diagram** for full marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Blockchains: Public, Private, and Consortium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Blockchains: Public, Private, and Consortium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **blockchain** is a distributed, append-only data structure composed of cryptographically linked blocks, maintained by a peer-to-peer network that reaches agreement (consensus) without requiring a central trusted authority. Based on the access, participation, and governance model, blockchains are broadly classified into three primary types:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> 1. **Public Blockchain (Permissionless)** – An open, fully decentralized ledger where any participant can join, read, write, and validate transactions without prior authorization.
> 2. **Private Blockchain (Permissioned)** – A restricted ledger controlled by a single organization where participation, reading, and validation rights are gated through identity verification.
> 3. **Consortium Blockchain (Federated / Permissioned)** – A semi-decentralized ledger governed by a pre-selected group of organizations sharing the responsibility of validation and governance.

The **core differentiator** between these three types is the **degree of decentralization**, the **identity model** (permissionless vs. permissioned), and the **consensus mechanism** employed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Blockchain Type | Real-World Analogy | Key Takeaway |
|---|---|---|
| **Public** | A public city bulletin board – anyone can post a notice, anyone can read it, and the notice stays permanently. | Permissionless, transparent, trustless. |
| **Private** | A company's internal notice board inside a secured office – only employees with keycards can post or read. | Controlled, faster, limited transparency. |
| **Consortium** | A shared notice board in an apartment complex managed by a few elected resident representatives. | Semi-trusted, multi-organizational, partially open. |

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the Automotive Industry

In the automotive domain, blockchain choice is dictated by the use case:

- **Public chains** are used for vehicle identity, decentralized vehicle financing, and tokenized carbon-credit tracking.
- **Private chains** suit internal OEM manufacturing traceability and proprietary R&D audit trails.
- **Consortium chains** are ideal for **multi-OEM supply chain traceability, V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) data exchange, and shared warranty/recall registries**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners often frame questions around "which blockchain type is best suited for a given automotive scenario." Always justify your answer with three criteria: **throughput, identity control, and trust assumption**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture & Operational Analysis

### A. Public Blockchain

- **Access Model:** Permissionless. No identity verification required.
- **Read/Write Privileges:** Open to all (read + write).
- **Consensus:** Resource-intensive, e.g., **Proof of Work (PoW)**, **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, **Delegated PoS**.
- **Incentive:** Token-based economic rewards (e.g., block reward + transaction fees).
- **Throughput:** Limited. Bitcoin ≈ **7 TPS**, Ethereum ≈ **15–30 TPS**.
- **Immutability:** Strongest, since thousands of independent nodes validate.
- **Examples:** Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, Polkadot, BNB Chain.

### B. Private Blockchain

- **Access Model:** Permissioned. Strict identity verification (KYC / certificates).
- **Read/Write Privileges:** Restricted; controlled by a single governing organization.
- **Consensus:** Lightweight, e.g., **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)**, **Raft**, **Quorum-based**.
- **Incentive:** No native cryptocurrency. Incentive is operational efficiency.
- **Throughput:** High. Hyperledger Fabric can achieve **3,000+ TPS** in benchmark tests.
- **Immutability:** Weaker – governed entity can rewrite history through administrative overrides.
- **Examples:** Hyperledger Besu (private mode), Quorum (private deployment), Multichain.

### C. Consortium Blockchain

- **Access Model:** Permissioned. Governed by a pre-selected group of trusted nodes.
- **Read/Write Privileges:** Public read, restricted write (or fully restricted depending on policy).
- **Consensus:** Pre-agreed protocol among member nodes (PBFT, Federated BFT, Raft).
- **Incentive:** Shared business value, not crypto-tokens.
- **Throughput:** Moderate to high. **1,000+ TPS** depending on validator count.
- **Immutability:** Stronger than private, weaker than public – trust distributed across consortium members.
- **Examples:** Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, Quorum, Energy Web Chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common KTU question asks to differentiate between "fully decentralized" and "semi-decentralized." The correct answer pattern is: *Public = fully decentralized; Consortium = semi-decentralized (some centralization among validators); Private = centralized governance with distributed ledger infrastructure.*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Public Blockchain | Private Blockchain | Consortium Blockchain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Control** | Permissionless | Permissioned (Single Org) | Permissioned (Group of Orgs) |
| **Participants** | Anonymous, open | Known, vetted | Known, pre-selected |
| **Consensus Mechanism** | PoW, PoS, DPoS | PBFT, Raft, Quorum | PBFT, FBFT, Raft |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | Low (7–3,000) | High (1,000–20,000) | Moderate–High (1,000–5,000) |
| **Energy Consumption** | High (PoW) | Low | Low |
| **Native Token** | Yes (mandatory) | No | Optional |
| **Immutability Strength** | Very High | Moderate | High |
| **Cost per Transaction** | Variable (gas fees) | Low / Near zero | Low / Shared |
| **Transparency** | Full | Limited | Partial |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Tolerates $\leq 51\%$ attack | Tolerates $\leq 33\%$ (BFT) | Tolerates $\leq 33\%$ (BFT) |
| **Governance** | Community / Foundation | Single Authority | Consortium Council |
| **Best Suited For** | Crypto, public registries | Enterprise internal use | B2B, supply chain, banking |
| **Automotive Use Case** | Vehicle NFT identity, crypto tolls | OEM factory audit, recall mgmt | Multi-OEM parts traceability, V2X |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

In an automotive blockchain implementation, the **transaction throughput requirement** drives the choice:

$$T_{required} = N_{vehicles} \times f_{tx,vehicle}$$

where $N_{vehicles}$ is the fleet size and $f_{tx,vehicle}$ is the per-vehicle transaction frequency (e.g., 1 tx/min for telemetry). If $T_{required} > 1000$ TPS, **public chains become infeasible** without Layer-2 scaling, making **private or consortium** chains the practical choice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Block Validation Throughput Derivation

To compare blockchain types quantitatively, we use the throughput equation for BFT-based consensus:

$$TPS = \frac{B \times f_{block}}{T_{latency} \times n_{tx,per\_block}}$$

where:
- $B$ = Number of blocks committed per consensus round
- $f_{block}$ = Block frequency (blocks/sec)
- $T_{latency}$ = End-to-end consensus latency
- $n_{tx,per\_block}$ = Average transactions packed per block

### Worked Example: Comparing Public vs Consortium for an Automotive Telemetry Use Case

**Given:**
- $B = 1$ block per round
- $T_{latency}^{public} = 600$ sec (Bitcoin block time)
- $T_{latency}^{consortium} = 1$ sec (PBFT)
- $n_{tx,per\_block} = 2{,}000$ for public, $500$ for consortium

**Step 1:** Public blockchain throughput

$$TPS_{public} = \frac{1 \times \frac{1}{600}}{2000} \times \frac{1}{1} = \frac{1}{600 \times 2000}$$

$$TPS_{public} = 8.33 \times 10^{-7} \text{ blocks} \times \text{tx}^{-1} \times \text{sec}^{-1}$$

Converting correctly:

$$TPS_{public} = \frac{1}{600} \times 2000 = 3333 \text{ sec}^{-1} \cdot \text{tx} \times \text{wait...}$$

Corrected formulation:

$$TPS_{public} = \frac{n_{tx,per\_block}}{T_{latency}} = \frac{2000}{600} = 3.33 \text{ TPS}$$

**Step 2:** Consortium blockchain throughput

$$TPS_{consortium} = \frac{n_{tx,per\_block}}{T_{latency}} = \frac{500}{1} = 500 \text{ TPS}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{TPS_{consortium}}{TPS_{public}} = \frac{500}{3.33} \approx 150 \times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For high-frequency automotive telemetry, consortium chains deliver **~150× higher throughput** than a public chain like Bitcoin, justifying the choice of permissioned ledgers for V2X and fleet management.

## 3.2 Security / Fault Tolerance Derivation

For BFT-based consensus, a system of $n$ validators can tolerate up to $f$ faulty nodes:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

### Example: Consortium with 10 OEMs

To find the maximum tolerable faulty nodes:

$$10 \geq 3f + 1$$

$$3f \leq 9$$

$$f \leq 3$$

So the system tolerates up to **3 faulty nodes** out of 10. If we instead used a public chain with PoW, an attacker would need:

$$\text{Hash Power}_{attacker} > 51\%$$

of the total network. This is far more secure in *theory* but practically infeasible for high TPS automotive workloads.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix for Automotive Use Cases

| Automotive Use Case | Required TPS | Trust Model | Recommended Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle NFT identity | $<10$ | Trustless | **Public** |
| OEM internal recall logs | $<100$ | Single org | **Private** |
| Multi-OEM parts traceability | $100$–$1000$ | Multi-party | **Consortium** |
| V2X real-time messaging | $>1000$ | Trusted validators | **Consortium** |
| Decentralized car-sharing | $10$–$50$ | Trustless | **Public** |
| In-factory IoT audit | $>5000$ | Single org | **Private** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Public Blockchain – Fully Decentralized Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Node A - Miner]:::publicNode
    A2[Node B - Miner]:::publicNode
    A3[Node C - Miner]:::publicNode
    A4[Node D - Miner]:::publicNode
    A5[Node E - Miner]:::publicNode
    A6[Node F - Miner]:::publicNode
    P1[Public Ledger Copy 1]:::ledger
    P2[Public Ledger Copy 2]:::ledger
    P3[Public Ledger Copy 3]:::ledger
    P4[Public Ledger Copy 4]:::ledger

    A1 --- A2
    A1 --- A3
    A1 --- A4
    A2 --- A5
    A3 --- A6
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6

    A1 -.replicates.-> P1
    A2 -.replicates.-> P2
    A3 -.replicates.-> P3
    A4 -.replicates.-> P4
    A5 -.replicates.-> P1
    A6 -.replicates.-> P2

    classDef publicNode fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ledger fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every node is identical in privilege (peers), and each maintains a full copy of the global ledger. There is no administrative gatekeeper.

## 4.2 Private Blockchain – Centralized Governance Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    ADM[Central Admin Authority]:::admin
    V1[Validator Node 1]:::validator
    V2[Validator Node 2]:::validator
    V3[Validator Node 3]:::validator
    R1[Read-Only Client 1]:::reader
    R2[Read-Only Client 2]:::reader
    R3[Read-Only Client 3]:::reader
    L1[Private Ledger]:::ledgerPriv

    ADM --> V1
    ADM --> V2
    ADM --> V3
    ADM -.approves.-> R1
    ADM -.approves.-> R2
    ADM -.approves.-> R3

    V1 --- V2
    V2 --- V3
    V1 --- V3

    V1 -.writes.-> L1
    V2 -.writes.-> L1
    V3 -.writes.-> L1

    classDef admin fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef validator fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reader fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ledgerPriv fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single administrative authority controls validator selection and reader admission. Validators form the consensus group; clients are read-only by default.

## 4.3 Consortium Blockchain – Federated Governance Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    O1[OEM 1 - Validator]:::consortium
    O2[OEM 2 - Validator]:::consortium
    O3[OEM 3 - Validator]:::consortium
    O4[OEM 4 - Validator]:::consortium
    O5[Supplier 1 - Non-Validator]:::supplier
    O6[Supplier 2 - Non-Validator]:::supplier
    S1[Shared Consortium Ledger]:::ledgerCons

    O1 --- O2
    O2 --- O3
    O3 --- O4
    O1 --- O4

    O5 -.submits tx.-> O1
    O6 -.submits tx.-> O3

    O1 -.validates.-> S1
    O2 -.validates.-> S1
    O3 -.validates.-> S1
    O4 -.validates.-> S1

    classDef consortium fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef supplier fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ledgerCons fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A pre-selected group of OEMs acts as validators, forming a peer consortium. External suppliers submit transactions but do not validate. The ledger is shared among all consortium members.

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PUB[Public Chain Flow]
        P1[Anyone Submits TX] --> P2[All Nodes Validate via PoW PoS]
        P2 --> P3[Global Ledger Updated]
    end

    subgraph PRI[Private Chain Flow]
        Q1[Admin Approves Participant] --> Q2[Approved Node Submits TX]
        Q2 --> Q3[Internal Validators Reach BFT Consensus]
        Q3 --> Q4[Internal Ledger Updated]
    end

    subgraph CON[Consortium Chain Flow]
        R1[Member Org Submits TX] --> R2[Pre-Selected Validators Vote]
        R2 --> R3[Shared Ledger Updated Across Orgs]
    end

    classDef pubStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef priStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef conStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
*Differentiate between a public blockchain and a private blockchain with two distinguishing points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Access Model:** A *public blockchain* is **permissionless** – any anonymous participant can join, validate, and transact (e.g., Bitcoin). A *private blockchain* is **permissioned** – only pre-approved, identity-verified participants can join (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric in private mode). **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Consensus & Performance:** Public chains typically use resource-intensive consensus (PoW/PoS) resulting in low TPS (≈7–30 TPS), whereas private chains use lightweight BFT-based consensus achieving **1,000+ TPS**. **(1.5 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
*List any three characteristics of a consortium blockchain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Semi-decentralized governance** – controlled by a pre-selected group of organizations rather than a single entity or open public.
2. **Permissioned access** – validators are pre-approved; reading and writing rights can be selectively granted.
3. **Higher throughput than public chains** – typically 1,000+ TPS, with consensus restricted to a small validator set.
4. *(Optional 4th)* Shared trust model with no single point of failure among consortium members.
**(1 mark each)**

---

## Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B must be provided.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) – CHOICE 1

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the architectural differences between **public, private, and consortium blockchains** with suitable diagrams. Highlight the differences in **access control, consensus, and governance**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consortium of **5 automotive OEMs** wants to share a tamper-proof parts traceability ledger. The expected workload is **800 transactions per second**, and at most **one OEM may act maliciously**. Determine whether PBFT consensus is feasible, and justify your choice with a fault-tolerance calculation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architectural Differences (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Public | Private | Consortium |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Open / Anonymous | Single org controls | Group of orgs controls |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS | PBFT / Raft | PBFT / Federated BFT |
| **Validator Count** | Thousands | Few (single org) | Pre-selected (e.g., 5–50) |
| **Governance** | Distributed community | Centralized | Federated council |
| **Immutability** | Strongest | Weakest (admin override possible) | Strong (multi-party trust) |
| **Throughput** | Low | High | High |

- A textual + diagram explanation (as given in Section 4) should accompany the answer.
- *Examiner expects at least 3 distinguishing parameters with examples (Bitcoin, Hyperledger, R3 Corda).* **(7 marks)**

**(b) PBFT Feasibility Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the fault tolerance requirement. The problem states *"at most one OEM may act maliciously"*, so $f = 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply the BFT condition:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

$$n \geq 3(1) + 1 = 4$$

**Step 3:** Since the consortium has $n = 5$ OEMs, and $5 \geq 4$, **PBFT is feasible**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4:** Throughput check. With PBFT, 5 validators can achieve ~1,000+ TPS, easily satisfying 800 TPS. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** Conclusion: A **consortium blockchain with PBFT** is the correct choice – meets fault tolerance, throughput, and shared governance requirements. **(2 marks)**

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) – CHOICE 2 (Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **public, private, and consortium blockchains** across at least **six parameters**, and identify the most suitable type for **(i) vehicle NFT identity** and **(ii) multi-OEM supply chain traceability**. Justify with reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block latency of **0.5 seconds** and **300 transactions per block** in a consortium chain, calculate the achievable **TPS**. If the workload is increased to **2,000 TPS**, what architectural changes would you recommend? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Six-Parameter Comparison + Use Case Mapping (7 marks):**

Comparison table across: *access control, consensus, throughput, immutability, governance, token requirement*. (3 marks for table)

- **(i) Vehicle NFT identity** → **Public Blockchain.** Justification: Trustless, no central authority needed; immutability of ownership records is critical; transaction volume is low. (2 marks)
- **(ii) Multi-OEM supply chain traceability** → **Consortium Blockchain.** Justification: Multiple trusted OEMs must share data; throughput is high; semi-decentralized governance is fair. (2 marks)

**(b) TPS Calculation + Scaling (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Use the throughput formula:

$$TPS = \frac{n_{tx,per\_block}}{T_{latency}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$TPS = \frac{300}{0.5} = 600 \text{ TPS}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 3:** For 2,000 TPS workload:

$$TPS_{required} = 2000 > TPS_{current} = 600$$

**Step 4:** Recommended architectural changes (4 marks):
1. **Sharding** – partition the ledger into parallel sub-chains.
2. **Layer-2 off-chaining** – process high-frequency micro-transactions off-chain and settle on-chain in batches.
3. **Parallel PBFT pipelines** – increase validator parallelism.
4. **Block size optimization** – increase $n_{tx,per\_block}$ or reduce $T_{latency}$ via optimized networking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "decentralized infrastructure" with "decentralized governance."** A private blockchain has distributed nodes but centralized governance – losing this distinction costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT claim consortium = fully decentralized.** It is *semi-decentralized*. This is a frequent mark-loser.
> 3. **Always back your answer with at least one example** (Bitcoin, Hyperledger, Corda). Marks are awarded for application, not just definition.
