> 4. **In numeric problems, always show the substitution step** – writing only the final answer forfeits 2 marks.
> 5. **Avoid using "permissioned" and "private" as synonyms** in consortium questions – they are related but not interchangeable.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Public** = Permissionless + PoW/PoS + Anonymous + Low TPS + Strongest immutability. (Bitcoin, Ethereum)
- **Private** = Permissioned (single org) + BFT/Raft + Known identities + High TPS + Admin-overridable. (Hyperledger, Quorum)
- **Consortium** = Permissioned (group of orgs) + Federated BFT + Pre-selected validators + High TPS + Shared trust. (R3 Corda, Hyperledger Fabric)
- **BFT fault tolerance formula:** $n \geq 3f + 1$.
- **Public chain attack threshold:** $> 51\%$ hash power (PoW) or $> 51\%$ stake (PoS).
- **TPS formula:** $TPS = n_{tx,per\_block} / T_{latency}$.
- **Automotive recommendation rule of thumb:**
  - Trustless, low-frequency, public-facing → **Public**.
  - Single OEM internal audit → **Private**.
  - Multi-party B2B with shared governance → **Consortium**.
- **Key terms to define in exam answers:** *Permissionless, Permissioned, Semi-decentralized, Validator, Consensus, BFT, TPS, Immutability, Governance, Federation.*
- **Avoid jargon-only answers** – every claim should be tied to a property (throughput, trust, identity, cost).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Cryptocurrencies and Their Relation to Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Cryptocurrencies and Their Relation to Blockchain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cryptocurrency

A **cryptocurrency** is a digital or virtual form of currency that is secured by cryptographic techniques, operates independently of any central monetary authority (such as a central bank or government), and is built upon a distributed ledger technology — most commonly a **blockchain**. The cryptographic primitives ensure transactional integrity, non-repudiation, and resistance to double-spending.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Cryptocurrency is a medium of exchange, created and stored electronically in the blockchain, using cryptographic protocols to secure transactions, control the creation of additional monetary units, and verify the transfer of assets.*

**Key descriptive properties of a cryptocurrency:**
- **Decentralized** — No single point of control.
- **Cryptographic** — Uses hash functions, public-key cryptography, and digital signatures.
- **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** — Direct transfer between participants without intermediaries.
- **Immutable** — Once confirmed, transactions cannot be retroactively altered.
- **Pseudonymous** — Users are identified by wallet addresses rather than real-world identities.
- **Scarcity-bound** — Most cryptocurrencies (e.g., **Bitcoin's 21 million cap**) have a mathematically enforced finite supply.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Blockchain (in the context of cryptocurrencies)

A **blockchain** is a distributed, append-only data structure composed of a chronologically linked chain of **blocks**, where each block contains a batch of validated transactions. Blocks are cryptographically linked using **hash pointers**, and consensus among network participants ensures a single, consistent state of the ledger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Relationship (KTU High-Yield Statement)**
> The **blockchain is the underlying infrastructure** (the data layer + consensus layer), while the **cryptocurrency is the application layer asset** that is transferred, tracked, and secured by that infrastructure. Without blockchain, decentralized cryptocurrency is impossible; without cryptocurrency, blockchain reduces to a generic distributed ledger.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Classroom Notebook

Imagine a classroom of **100 students** keeping financial IOU records.

- **Traditional banking (Centralized):** The class teacher (the bank) keeps the only notebook. If the teacher is bribed, makes an error, or is absent, the system fails.
- **Cryptocurrency (Decentralized):** *Every* student has an identical copy of the notebook. When Alice pays Bob ₹10, she announces it aloud. Every student verifies the transaction (does Alice have enough balance? Has she signed it with her unique seal?) and updates their own copy simultaneously.
- **Blockchain (The Notebook's Rules):** The notebook is organized into **pages (blocks)**. Each new page references the previous page's unique fingerprint (**hash pointer**). To tamper with page 50, you would have to rewrite pages 51–100 in every student's notebook — practically impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for the automotive industry (KTU Context)**
> In a connected vehicle ecosystem, just as no single student controls the ledger, no single OEM or service provider controls the vehicle data record. Cryptocurrency-style distributed consensus models are being explored for in-vehicle micro-payments (charging, tolls, parking) and tamper-proof odometer/usage records.

## 1.4 Why Cryptocurrencies Need Blockchain

| Problem in Digital Cash | Blockchain Solution |
|---|---|
| Double-spending (spending the same coin twice) | Distributed consensus rejects conflicting transactions |
| Trust without a central bank | Cryptographic proof replaces institutional trust |
| Tampering with transaction history | Hash-linked blocks + replication make alterations detectable |
| Censorship / arbitrary account freezing | No central authority can block valid transactions |
| Counterfeiting | Cryptographic signatures verify coin ownership |

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants (Syllabus Highlights)

- **Bitcoin block time:** ~**10 minutes**
- **Ethereum block time:** ~**12–15 seconds**
- **Bitcoin total supply cap:** **21,000,000 BTC**
- **Bitcoin block reward halving interval:** **Every 210,000 blocks** (≈4 years)
- **Hash function output length (SHA-256):** **256 bits**
- **Public-key cryptography used:** **ECDSA over secp256k1 curve (Bitcoin)**, **Ed25519 (some altcoins)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of a Cryptocurrency

A cryptocurrency system can be deconstructed into three interdependent layers:

### Layer 1 — The Blockchain (Data Layer)
- Maintains the **distributed ledger** of all confirmed transactions.
- Each block **Bₙ** contains:
  - Block header (previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, nonce, difficulty target)
  - Block body (ordered list of transactions)
- Blocks are linked by a **hash pointer** $H(B_{n-1})$ embedded in block $B_n$.

### Layer 2 — The Consensus Protocol (Agreement Layer)
- Ensures all honest nodes agree on a single chain state.
- Common mechanisms:
  - **Proof of Work (PoW)** — Bitcoin
  - **Proof of Stake (PoS)** — Ethereum (post-Merge)
  - **Delegated PoS, Byzantine Fault Tolerant variants** — used in automotive consortium chains.

### Layer 3 — The Currency / Token (Application Layer)
- The **asset** being transferred (e.g., BTC, ETH, custom automotive tokens).
- Defined by a set of protocol rules: supply schedule, divisibility, transfer logic.
- Often governed by **smart contracts** (programmable money).

## 2.2 Cryptographic Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three cryptographic primitives underpin every cryptocurrency:**

1. **Cryptographic Hash Function** $H(\cdot)$
   - Deterministic, one-way, collision-resistant.
   - Standard: **SHA-256** in Bitcoin, **Keccak-256** in Ethereum.

2. **Public-Key Cryptography (Asymmetric)**
   - Each user holds a **key pair** $(sk, pk)$ where $sk$ is the secret key and $pk$ is the public key.
   - The wallet address is derived: $\text{Address} = H(pk)$.
   - Standard: **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm).

3. **Digital Signatures**
   - A user signs transaction $T$ as: $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(T))$.
   - Verification: $\text{Verify}(pk, H(T), \sigma) \in \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$.

## 2.3 The Transaction Lifecycle (Detailed Steps)

A cryptocurrency transaction goes through the following pipeline:

1. **Initiation** — Sender Alice constructs a transaction $T$ specifying: inputs (UTXOs or account nonce), outputs (Bob's address + amount), and optional data field.
2. **Signing** — Alice computes $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk_A, H(T))$ and broadcasts $\{T, \sigma\}$ to the P2P network.
3. **Propagation** — Nodes validate $\text{Verify}(pk_A, H(T), \sigma)$ and relay the transaction across the gossip network.
4. **Mempool** — Valid transactions sit in each node's memory pool awaiting inclusion in a block.
5. **Block Inclusion (Mining/Validation)** — A miner/validator selects transactions, forms a candidate block, and executes the consensus protocol.
6. **Block Confirmation** — The new block is appended to the chain; the transaction is now considered confirmed (probability of reversal decreases with each subsequent block).
7. **Finality** — After $k$ confirmations (typically $k = 6$ for Bitcoin), the transaction is considered economically final.

## 2.4 Proof of Work — Mathematical Skeleton

In PoW, miners search for a **nonce** $n$ such that the block hash is below a target threshold $T$:

$$H(B_n) \;=\; \text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_n) \;<\; T$$

The target is related to the required difficulty $D$ by:

$$T \;=\; \frac{T_{\max}}{D}$$

where $T_{\max}$ is the maximum possible hash value for the given hash function. Bitcoin adjusts $D$ every **2016 blocks** (≈2 weeks) to maintain a 10-minute average block interval.

## 2.5 Merkle Tree — Efficient Transaction Commitment

Each block summarizes all its transactions via a **Merkle root** $R$:

$$R \;=\; H(H(T_1 \parallel T_2) \;\parallel\; H(T_3 \parallel T_4))$$

for 4 transactions, generalized recursively for any power-of-2 count. This allows **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — a light client can verify a transaction's inclusion using only $O(\log n)$ hashes instead of downloading the full block.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Block hash link | $H_n = H(B_n \parallel H_{n-1})$ | 256-bit output (SHA-256) |
| PoW condition | $H(\text{Header} \parallel n) < T$ | Brute-force search over $n$ |
| Difficulty–target relation | $T = T_{\max} / D$ | $D$ adjusted per epoch |
| ECDSA signature | $\sigma = (r, s)$ on curve secp256k1 | 512 bits total (two 256-bit ints) |
| Wallet address | $\text{Addr} = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(pk))$ | 160-bit (Bitcoin) |
| Merkle root (4 leaves) | $R = H(H(T_1\parallel T_2) \parallel H(T_3\parallel T_4))$ | Enables $O(\log n)$ SPV |
| Block reward (Bitcoin) | $R(t) = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ BTC | $h$ = block height |
| Total supply cap | $S = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 50 \cdot 2^{-i} \cdot 210000 = 21{,}000{,}000$ | Bitcoin |
| Gas fee (Ethereum) | $\text{Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$ | Wei denomination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the pipe character `|`, in any LaTeX table cell — to keep markdown tables from breaking.**

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering (Automotive Focus)

- **In-vehicle micropayments:** Pay-per-use insurance, dynamic road tolls, EV charging.
- **Tamper-proof odometer:** Vehicle mileage recorded on-chain prevents odometer fraud in the used-car market.
- **Provenance of parts:** Each auto-component's manufacturing history signed and stored on-chain.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** Electric vehicles earning tokens for discharging energy back to the grid.
- **Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS):** Unified token-based payment across buses, taxis, ride-shares.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bitcoin's 21 Million Supply Cap

The total supply of Bitcoin is bounded because the block reward is halved every 210,000 blocks. We derive the closed-form sum.

**Step 1 — Define the reward schedule.**

Let the block height be $h$ and the epoch number be $e = \lfloor h / 210000 \rfloor$. The reward at height $h$ is:

$$R(h) \;=\; 50 \cdot 2^{-e} \quad \text{[BTC per block]}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total reward emitted in epoch $e$.**

Each epoch contains exactly $N = 210{,}000$ blocks, so the BTC emitted in epoch $e$ is:

$$S_e \;=\; N \cdot R(e) \;=\; 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-e}$$

**Step 3 — Sum across all epochs.**

The total supply $S$ is the geometric series:

$$S \;=\; \sum_{e=0}^{\infty} S_e \;=\; \sum_{e=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-e}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out constants.**

$$S \;=\; (210000 \times 50) \cdot \sum_{e=0}^{\infty} 2^{-e}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the geometric series formula.**

The series $\sum_{e=0}^{\infty} 2^{-e} = \sum_{e=0}^{\infty} (1/2)^e$ is a convergent geometric series with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $r = 1/2$:

$$\sum_{e=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{e} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} \;=\; 2$$

**Step 6 — Final evaluation.**

$$S \;=\; 210000 \times 50 \times 2 \;=\; 21{,}000{,}000 \;\text{BTC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The cap of **21 million BTC** is not a policy choice — it is a *mathematical inevitability* emerging from the 210,000-block halving interval and the initial 50 BTC reward. Even if the parameters changed, the geometric convergence guarantees a finite supply.

## 3.2 Derivation: Merkle Root for $n$ Transactions

Suppose a block contains $n = 2^k$ transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{2^k}$.

**Step 1 — Hash the leaves (level 0).**

$$h_i^{(0)} \;=\; H(T_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^k$$

**Step 2 — Build level 1 (pairs of leaves).**

$$h_i^{(1)} \;=\; H\!\left(h_{2i-1}^{(0)} \;\parallel\; h_{2i}^{(0)}\right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{k-1}$$

**Step 3 — Induct upward to the root.**

At level $\ell$, with $2^{k-\ell}$ nodes:

$$h_i^{(\ell)} \;=\; H\!\left(h_{2i-1}^{(\ell-1)} \;\parallel\; h_{2i}^{(\ell-1)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — The Merkle root is the single node at level $k$.**

$$R \;=\; h_1^{(k)}$$

**Step 5 — Computational complexity.**

The total number of hash operations is:

$$C(n) \;=\; n + \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{4} + \cdots + 1 \;=\; 2n - 1 \;\;=\;\; O(n)$$

**Step 6 — SPV verification cost.**

To prove $T_j$ is in the block, a light client needs only the **Merkle path** of length $k$:

$$\text{SPV cost} \;=\; O(\log_2 n)$$

This is what allows mobile wallets to verify payments without downloading the entire blockchain.

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Block Time in PoW

We derive why Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment maintains a 10-minute block time.

**Step 1 — Define hash rate.**

Let $H$ = total network hash rate (hashes per second) and $p$ = probability that a single random hash attempt satisfies the PoW condition. Then:

$$p \;=\; \frac{T}{T_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{1}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Expected time to find a block.**

The number of attempts follows a geometric distribution with success probability $p$. Expected attempts:

$$E[\text{attempts}] \;=\; \frac{1}{p} \;=\; D$$

**Step 3 — Convert to time.**

$$\bar{t} \;=\; \frac{D}{H}$$

**Step 4 — Difficulty adjustment rule.**

Bitcoin targets $\bar{t} = 600$ seconds. Solving for $D$:

$$D_{\text{new}} \;=\; D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{2016 \cdot 600}$$

This recalibration happens every 2016 blocks, ensuring long-run stability of the block interval.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cryptocurrencies

| Property | Bitcoin (BTC) | Ethereum (ETH) | Automotive Token (Hypothetical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consensus | PoW (SHA-256) | PoS (Casper) | PoA / BFT (consortium) |
| Block time | ~10 min | ~12 s | ~2 s (private chain) |
| Supply cap | 21 M | No hard cap | Configurable (e.g., 1 B) |
| Smart contracts | Limited (Script) | Turing-complete (Solidity) | Yes (Solidity / Hyperledger) |
| Primary use | Store of value | DApps & DeFi | Vehicle data + payments |
| Privacy | Pseudonymous | Pseudonymous | Permissioned (KYC-bound) |

## 3.5 Worked Example — End-to-End Transaction (Pedagogical)

Suppose Alice (UTXO set balance: 1.0 BTC) wants to send 0.4 BTC to Bob.

**Step 1** — Alice's wallet selects UTXOs summing $\geq 0.4$ BTC. Assume she uses $U_1 = 0.5$ BTC.

**Step 2** — Construct transaction:

$$T = \{ \text{Input}: U_1, \text{Outputs}: (0.4 \to pk_B, \; 0.099 \to pk_A), \text{Fee}: 0.001 \}$$

The 0.001 BTC difference goes to the miner as a fee.

**Step 3** — Sign: $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk_A, H(T))$.

**Step 4** — Bob verifies and waits for 6 confirmations. The probability of a successful double-spend attack after $z$ confirmations is:

$$P_{\text{ds}}(z) \;=\; 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$

where $\lambda = z \cdot (q/p)$ with $p$ = honest hash power fraction and $q = 1 - p$ = attacker fraction. For $q < 0.1$ and $z = 6$, $P_{\text{ds}} < 0.001$ — economically negligible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cryptocurrency Transaction Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alice initiates transaction T] --> B[Alice signs T with secret key sk_A]
    B --> C[Broadcast T and signature sigma to P2P network]
    C --> D[Each node verifies Verify pk_A H T sigma]
    D --> E{Valid signature and unspent inputs?}
    E -->|No| F[Reject and drop transaction]
    E -->|Yes| G[Add to node mempool]
    G --> H[Validator or miner selects transactions for candidate block]
    H --> I[Run consensus protocol PoW PoS or PoA]
    I --> J{Block accepted by network?}
    J -->|No| K[Abandon block, gossip failure message]
    J -->|Yes| L[Append block to chain, propagate to all peers]
    L --> M[Bob waits for k confirmations]
    M --> N[Transaction is final and immutable]
```

## 4.2 Blockchain Block Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BlockNMinus1[Block n minus 1]
        H1[Block n minus 1 Header]
        B1[Block n minus 1 Body Transactions]
    end
    subgraph BlockN[Block n]
        H2[Block n Header]
        B2[Block n Body Transactions]
    end
    subgraph BlockNPlus1[Block n plus 1]
        H3[Block n plus 1 Header]
        B3[Block n plus 1 Body Transactions]
    end
    H1 -->|SHA256 hash pointer| H2
    H2 -->|SHA256 hash pointer| H3
    B2 -->|Merkle root| H2
    B3 -->|Merkle root| H3
```

## 4.3 Three-Layer Cryptocurrency Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer: Cryptocurrency Tokens and Smart Contracts]
    L2[Consensus Layer: PoW PoS or BFT Protocols]
    L3[Data Layer: Blockchain Distributed Ledger]
    L2 --> L1
    L3 --> L2
    L3 --> L1
```

## 4.4 Merkle Tree Construction (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Merkle Root R]
    N12[H of H T1 T2 and H T3 T4]
    N34[H of H T5 T6 and H T7 T8]
    N1[H T1]
    N2[H T2]
    N3[H T3]
    N4[H T4]
    N5[H T5]
    N6[H T6]
    N7[H T7]
    N8[H T8]
    R --> N12
    R --> N34
    N12 --> N1
    N12 --> N2
    N12 --> N3
    N12 --> N4
    N34 --> N5
    N34 --> N6
    N34 --> N7
    N34 --> N8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Merkle root $R$ at the top is a single 256-bit commitment to *all* transactions in the block. A light client can prove $T_5$ is included by presenting only $\{T_5, H(T_6 \parallel T_7 \parallel T_8), H(T_1 \parallel T_2 \parallel T_3 \parallel T_4)\}$ — just 2 hashes instead of 8.

## 4.5 PoW Mining Loop (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start assemble candidate block] --> N[Set nonce n to 0]
    N --> C[Compute H Header parallel n]
    C --> Q{H less than target T}
    Q -->|No| I[Increment n by 1]
    I --> C
    Q -->|Yes| X[Broadcast block to network]
    X --> V{Other nodes validate}
    V -->|Valid| A[Append to local chain]
    V -->|Invalid| D[Discard and continue mining on previous block]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Explain how a cryptocurrency differs from a traditional fiat currency. List any four distinguishing properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A cryptocurrency is a digital currency built on cryptographic principles and operates on a decentralized blockchain network, unlike fiat currency which is issued and controlled by a central authority such as a central bank.
**(1 mark)** Four distinguishing properties:
- **Decentralization** — No central authority controls issuance or transactions. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Cryptographic Security** — Transactions are secured using public-key cryptography and hash functions. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Transparency & Immutability** — All confirmed transactions are recorded on a public ledger and cannot be altered. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Global & Permissionless** — Anyone with an internet connection can transact, irrespective of geography. **(0.5 mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**With a neat diagram, describe the role of a Merkle root in a blockchain block.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Merkle root is a single 256-bit hash value stored in the block header that acts as a compact cryptographic commitment to **all** transactions in the block. **(1 mark)** It is computed by recursively hashing pairs of transaction hashes in a binary tree structure until a single root hash remains. **(1 mark)** Its role: enables **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — light clients can prove a transaction's inclusion with only $O(\log n)$ hashes, without downloading the full block. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Each Part B question carries two sub-parts, **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**, as per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Define a cryptocurrency. Discuss in detail the three cryptographic primitives that secure any cryptocurrency. How does each primitive contribute to the system's overall security?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A cryptocurrency is a digital medium of exchange that uses cryptographic techniques to secure transactions, controls the creation of new units, and operates on a decentralized blockchain network without the need for a central issuing authority.

**The three cryptographic primitives (5 marks):**

1. **Hash Functions (1.5 marks):**
   - One-way function: given $H(m)$, infeasible to recover $m$.
   - Collision-resistant: hard to find $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
   - Contribution: ensures block integrity and creates the chain linking blocks via $H_{n-1} \to B_n$.

2. **Public-Key Cryptography (1.5 marks):**
   - Each user holds $(sk, pk)$. Standard: ECDSA on secp256k1.
   - Contribution: enables wallet address generation, identity binding, and key-based authentication.

3. **Digital Signatures (2 marks):**
   - Sign: $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(m))$. Verify: $\text{Verify}(pk, H(m), \sigma)$.
   - Contribution: provides **non-repudiation** and **authenticity** — Alice cannot deny sending a transaction she signed.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the structure of a blockchain block. With a labelled diagram, show how blocks are cryptographically linked. What happens if a malicious node tries to alter a transaction in block $n-1$?

**Model Solution:**

**Block structure (2 marks):** A block consists of a **header** (containing version, previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, and nonce) and a **body** (containing the ordered list of transactions, typically structured as a Merkle tree).

**Cryptographic linking (2 marks):** Each block header contains $H(B_{n-1})$, the SHA-256 hash of the previous block's header. This hash pointer is what makes the chain tamper-evident — the link is cryptographically enforced.

**Tampering consequence (3 marks):** If a malicious node alters any transaction in $B_{n-1}$:
- The Merkle root of $B_{n-1}$ changes (Merkle trees are collision-resistant).
- The block hash $H(B_{n-1})$ changes.
- The pointer stored in $B_n$'s header no longer matches.
- All honest nodes detect the mismatch and **reject the tampered chain**.
- The attacker would need to recompute PoW for $B_{n-1}, B_n, B_{n+1}, \ldots, B_{\text{tip}}$ — economically infeasible if the honest network controls $>50\%$ hash power.

**Diagram (would be similar to Section 4.2):** Show three blocks connected by hash pointers with the Merkle root path.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern, Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the Merkle tree structure in a blockchain block. Derive the number of hash operations required to construct the Merkle root for $n$ transactions.

**Model Solution:**

**Merkle tree structure (3 marks):** A Merkle tree is a binary tree of hash values. Leaves are $H(T_i)$ for each transaction $T_i$. Internal nodes are $H(\text{left} \parallel \text{right})$. The root is a single hash that uniquely commits to the entire set of transactions. (See Section 4.4 diagram.)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- Level 0 has $n$ nodes (leaf hashes).
- Level 1 has $n/2$ nodes.
- Level $\ell$ has $n/2^\ell$ nodes.
- Total hash operations:

$$C(n) = n + \frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{4} + \cdots + 1 = n \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n}\right)$$

$$C(n) = 2n - 1 \quad \text{(for } n = 2^k\text{)}$$

Hence, **building the Merkle root requires $O(n)$ hash operations**, while **verifying inclusion of one transaction requires only $O(\log_2 n)$ hashes** (the Merkle path length).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the role of Proof of Work (PoW) in a cryptocurrency like Bitcoin. Derive the expected block time and explain why the difficulty parameter $D$ must be adjusted periodically.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of PoW (2 marks):** PoW serves three purposes: (i) Sybil resistance — making it computationally expensive to create identities, (ii) block-selection mechanism — probabilistic proportional to hash power, and (iii) chain-finality — exponentially increasing the cost of rewriting history with each added block.

**Expected block time derivation (3 marks):**
- Each hash attempt has success probability $p = 1/D$ (where $D$ is the difficulty).
- Expected number of attempts before success: $E[\text{attempts}] = 1/p = D$.
- Expected time: $\bar{t} = D / H$, where $H$ is the total hash rate.
- Bitcoin targets $\bar{t} = 600$ s.

**Why periodic adjustment (2 marks):** As mining hardware improves and the hash rate $H$ grows, $\bar{t}$ would shrink below 10 minutes, accelerating BTC issuance. To maintain a stable block interval, the difficulty is recalibrated every **2016 blocks** (≈2 weeks) as:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{t_{\text{actual,2016}}}{2016 \times 600}$$

This ensures long-run monetary stability and predictable issuance schedule.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing blockchain with Bitcoin** — Blockchain is the *infrastructure*; Bitcoin/cryptocurrency is the *asset*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Writing "cryptocurrency is anonymous"** — Incorrect. It is *pseudonymous*. Real identity may be inferred via chain analytics. **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting units / bit-lengths** — SHA-256 produces 256-bit output, not "a hash." Always state bit-length for full marks. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the derivation in Part B** — Part B (a) sub-questions often *require* a step-by-step derivation. Writing the final answer without intermediate steps results in partial marking. **(Lose up to 50% of sub-part marks)**
> 5. **Ignoring the automotive context** — When a question is set in the automotive context (e.g., vehicle micropayments, odometer fraud), always tie your answer back to the **application domain** for the application-component marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cryptocurrency = application-layer asset.** **Blockchain = infrastructure layer.**
- A cryptocurrency needs **three** cryptographic primitives: **hash function, public-key cryptography, digital signatures**.
- **Bitcoin's 21 million cap** is mathematically derived from the halving schedule — it is not arbitrary.
- **SHA-256** is the standard hash in Bitcoin; **Keccak-256** in Ethereum; both produce 256-bit outputs.
- **ECDSA on secp256k1** is the signature algorithm in Bitcoin.
- **Merkle root** enables $O(\log n)$ SPV — light-client-friendly verification.
- **Proof of Work** equation: $H(\text{Header} \parallel n) < T$, with $T = T_{\max} / D$.
- **Difficulty adjustment** happens every **2016 blocks** in Bitcoin (≈2 weeks) to maintain a 10-minute target.
- **Block reward halving** happens every **210,000 blocks** (≈4 years).
- **Transaction finality** in Bitcoin is probabilistic — after **6 confirmations**, reversal probability is <0.1% if attacker has <10% hash power.
- **Double-spending prevention** is the central problem solved by decentralized consensus.
- **UTXO model (Bitcoin)** vs **Account model (Ethereum)** — know the difference; it's a frequent KTU question.
- **Automotive relevance** — Always frame your answer in the in-vehicle / V2G / odometer-fraud / MaaS context when the question is set in PEAUT751.
- **Pseudonymous ≠ Anonymous** — state this distinction explicitly in any exam answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Cases of Blockchain in Automotive Supply Chain Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use Cases of Blockchain in Automotive Supply Chain Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Supply Chain Management (ASCM)** refers to the coordinated, end-to-end orchestration of the flow of raw materials, sub-assemblies, components, finished vehicles, and associated information flows — from Tier-N raw material suppliers → Tier-1 system integrators → Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) → dealerships → end customers, including reverse logistics for recalls and end-of-life recycling.

**Blockchain-Based ASCM** is the application of Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) to ASCM, wherein every transaction — such as the transfer of a part, certification of origin, customs clearance, quality inspection, or payment settlement — is recorded as an immutable, cryptographically signed, time-stamped block on a shared, permissioned ledger (typically Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum, or Corda in industrial deployments).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** The term "Use Cases" implies a **descriptive/applied** cognitive level (Bloom's L3 — *Apply*). Students are expected to enumerate, differentiate, and critically analyze the various blockchain applications in the automotive supply chain — *not* to re-derive the cryptography or consensus mechanisms (covered in Module 1).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Notary That Travels With the Part"

Imagine a **luxury spare part** — say, a Bosch fuel injector — moving from a steel mill in Pune, to a Tier-2 forging shop in Coimbatore, to a Tier-1 Bosch plant, to a Maruti Suzuki OEM assembly line in Gurugram, and finally to a service centre in Kochi.

In the **legacy system** (ERP + spreadsheets + paper bills of lading), every time this part changes hands, a *new* paper/digital record is created in a *different* system. If any of these systems is hacked, lost, or deliberately falsified, the chain of custody is broken. Tracing a counterfeit injector back to its source is, in practice, nearly impossible.

Now imagine that **a tiny digital "passport" is welded to that injector at the steel mill** — and this passport is automatically updated, cryptographically signed, and distributed to *every* stakeholder (mill, forger, OEM, dealer, regulator) the moment any event occurs. **Nobody can forge, delete, or alter any page of this passport without everyone else noticing instantly.** That digital passport *is* the blockchain — a decentralized, tamper-evident, append-only ledger that travels with the physical part.

## 1.3 The 9 Canonical Use Cases (KTU High-Yield Map)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups blockchain applications in the automotive supply chain into **nine canonical use cases**. Memorizing this taxonomy is critical for Part A and Part B answers.

| # | Use Case | Core Problem Solved |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Parts Provenance & Traceability** | "Where did this part come from?" |
| 2 | **Anti-Counterfeit Authentication** | "Is this a genuine OEM part?" |
| 3 | **Recall Management** | "Which vehicles are affected by this defect?" |
| 4 | **Tier-N Supplier Visibility** | "Who made the raw material inside this part?" |
| 5 | **Logistics & Shipment Tracking** | "Where is my container, and is its condition intact?" |
| 6 | **Smart Contract-Based Payments** | "Has the milestone been met? Pay automatically." |
| 7 | **Warranty & Service History** | "Has this car's service record been tampered with?" |
| 8 | **Compliance & Sustainability (ESG)** | "Is this cobalt conflict-free? Is the CO₂ claim real?" |
| 9 | **Inventory & Spare Parts Optimization** | "Is this SKU real, and is the stock count trustworthy?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Statistic (Frequently Quoted in KTU Papers):** The World Economic Forum estimates that blockchain-based traceability could **reduce counterfeit parts in the automotive aftermarket by up to 40%** and **cut recall-related losses by ~$1 billion annually** for a single major OEM. These numbers are illustrative benchmarks — cite them in 14-mark answers to demonstrate industry awareness.

## 1.4 Why Blockchain? — The Five Inherent Properties

A blockchain is uniquely suited to ASCM because it provides — natively, without trusted intermediaries — the following five guarantees:

1. **Immutability** — Once a block is committed (e.g., "Part #INV-9981 left Plant A at 09:42"), it cannot be retroactively edited.
2. **Provenance** — Every block is cryptographically chained to its predecessor, giving a verifiable lineage.
3. **Transparency (Permissioned)** — Pre-approved industry participants (OEM, suppliers, regulators) can view the same single source of truth.
4. **Automation via Smart Contracts** — Self-executing code triggers payment, alerts, or recalls when on-chain conditions are met.
5. **Disintermediation** — Eliminates the need for a central ERP system to be the "single point of trust," reducing single-point-of-failure risk.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why use blockchain instead of a traditional database in the automotive supply chain?"* — your answer **must** invoke the *combination* of multi-party trust + immutability + disintermediation. A centralized database already provides immutability (via backups) — the *unique* value-add of blockchain is **trustless consensus across competing organizations** (OEM, supplier, regulator, insurer).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Use Case 1 — Parts Provenance & Traceability

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Minting:** At the Tier-N raw material supplier (e.g., a steel mill), each batch of steel is assigned a **Digital Twin ID** (typically a GS1 EPCIS-compliant identifier or a hash of a serialized QR code). This ID is written to the blockchain as the *genesis* transaction.
- **Step 2 — Transformation Events:** As the steel is forged → machined → assembled into a sub-component → integrated into a vehicle, each transformation event is signed by the responsible party's private key and appended to the chain.
- **Step 3 — Anchoring:** The blockchain stores *only the cryptographic hash* of supporting documents (mill test certificates, CMVR approvals, quality inspection reports) — the documents themselves live in off-chain IPFS or enterprise databases. This keeps the chain lean.
- **Step 4 — Query:** When a dealer or regulator queries "Show me the full lineage of fuel injector X," the smart contract traverses the Merkle tree of hashes and reconstructs the lineage in O(log n) time.

### 2.1.2 Why It Matters

- A **BMW study (2019)** demonstrated that for a single vehicle, the supply chain spans **~25,000 parts from ~2,300 suppliers across 70+ countries**. Without blockchain, full provenance is computationally and organizationally infeasible.
- Provenance directly enables **use case 2, 3, 4, and 8** — it is the *foundational* use case on which others are layered.

## 2.2 Use Case 2 — Anti-Counterfeit Authentication

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Tagging:** Every genuine OEM part is laser-marked with a tamper-evident QR/NFC tag whose secret key is registered on-chain at production.
- **Step 2 — Field Verification:** A mechanic at a service centre scans the tag. The smartphone app queries the blockchain; the smart contract returns a boolean: `isGenuine = (hashOnTag == hashOnChain) AND (partStatus != "RECALLED") AND (partStatus != "DECOMMISSIONED")`.
- **Step 3 — Counterfeit Response:** If the verification fails, the part is flagged, and the event is logged for the OEM's fraud analytics dashboard.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Deployment Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Renault's Xcevia Initiative (Mentioned in KTU Reference Material):** Renault, in partnership with Microsoft and VISEO, deployed a blockchain-based "Xcevia" certificate-of-authenticity system for ~1 million vehicle documents in 2018 — directly applicable to anti-counterfeit workflows for parts and vehicles.

## 2.3 Use Case 3 — Recall Management

### 2.3.1 The Legacy Problem

In a traditional system, when an OEM issues a recall (e.g., "Takata airbags, 2010–2017 models"), it must:
1. Manually cross-reference the defective batch's serial numbers with VINs registered at dealerships.
2. Notify each dealer individually.
3. Hope the dealer actually checks VINs before servicing.

This process averages **6–18 months** and recovers only **~60–70%** of affected vehicles globally.

### 2.3.2 The Blockchain Solution

- **Step 1:** When a part is found defective post-sale, the OEM writes a `RECALL_EVENT` to the chain, referencing the batch ID.
- **Step 2:** A smart contract automatically broadcasts a notification to **every dealership, insurer, and registered service centre** whose public keys are listed as downstream custodians of that batch.
- **Step 3:** Customers can self-verify against the chain via a public portal — no need to wait for postal mail.
- **Step 4:** As affected parts are replaced, the chain records the remediation, providing the OEM with **real-time recall completion analytics** (a regulatory requirement in the EU under the **General Safety Regulation 2019/2144**).

## 2.4 Use Case 4 — Tier-N Supplier Visibility

The modern automotive value chain is a **multi-tier graph**:

$$
\text{Tier-N (mines)} \rightarrow \text{Tier-(N-1) (refineries)} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Tier-2 (forgings)} \rightarrow \text{Tier-1 (Bosch, ZF)} \rightarrow \text{OEM}
$$

In legacy ERP, OEMs typically have visibility only into **Tier-1** direct suppliers. **Blockchain enables Tier-N visibility** because each Tier-1 is contractually required (via a smart contract clause) to push its Tier-2 / Tier-3 provenance hashes up the chain.

This is critical for:
- **Conflict minerals compliance** (Section 1502 of the U.S. Dodd-Frank Act, EU Conflict Minerals Regulation 2017/821).
- **Carbon footprint accounting** (Scope-3 emissions per the GHG Protocol).

## 2.5 Use Case 5 — Logistics & Shipment Tracking

### 2.5.1 IoT-Blockchain Coupling

Each shipping container is fitted with IoT sensors (GPS, temperature, humidity, shock). The sensor data is written to the chain via an **oracle** (e.g., Chainlink, Hyperledger Oracle). The smart contract can:
- Release a payment to the logistics provider when GPS confirms port-to-port transit.
- Trigger an **insurance claim automatically** if the temperature excursion exceeds the contractual threshold (e.g., for battery cells).
- Notify the OEM if the shock sensor exceeds a g-force limit, flagging potential part damage.

## 2.6 Use Case 6 — Smart Contract-Based Payments (Pay-Per-Milestone)

### 2.6.1 The "Letter of Credit 2.0"

Traditional trade finance uses paper Letters of Credit (LC), which take 5–10 days to clear. A blockchain-based **smart LC** triggers payment the instant the on-chain condition is met:

$$
\text{IF } (\text{partQualityCertHash} = \text{expectedHash}) \text{ AND } (\text{GPS.recipientLocation} = \text{expectedLocation}) \text{ THEN } \text{transfer}(\text{supplierWallet}, \text{amount})
$$

This reduces settlement time from days to **seconds** and eliminates reconciliation disputes.

## 2.7 Use Case 7 — Warranty & Service History

### 2.7.1 The Odometer Fraud Problem

Odometer fraud in the used-car market costs consumers **~$1 billion annually in the EU alone** (Europol estimate). Blockchain-based service logs — written by authorized service centres — make odometer rollback **cryptographically infeasible** because each service event includes a hash of `(VIN, odometerReading, timestamp, serviceCentrePubKey)`.

## 2.8 Use Case 8 — Compliance & Sustainability (ESG Tracking)

For Electric Vehicles (EVs), the **battery passport** (mandated by the EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542, effective Feb 2027) requires:
- Provenance of cobalt, lithium, nickel.
- Carbon footprint of manufacturing (kg CO₂eq/kWh).
- Recycling content percentage.

A blockchain-based **battery passport** is the only technically feasible way to maintain this multi-stakeholder, multi-jurisdiction, multi-decade record (an EV battery is warrantied for ~10 years and recycled over 20+ years).

## 2.9 Use Case 9 — Inventory & Spare Parts Optimization

Discrepancies between physical inventory and ERP inventory (called **"phantom inventory"** or **"shrinkage"**) cost the automotive aftermarket **~$5–8 billion/year** globally. Blockchain-recorded movements between warehouse and dealer create a **single source of truth** that can be audited in real time, dramatically reducing shrinkage.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Use Case | Blockchain Property Leveraged | Key Benefit | Real-World Reference |
|----------|-------------------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Provenance | Immutability, Chained Hashes | End-to-end lineage of 25,000+ parts | BMW "PartChain" (2019) |
| Anti-Counterfeit | Asymmetric Cryptography | Genuine vs. fake verification | Renault Xcevia |
| Recall Management | Smart Contracts, Multi-Party Notifications | Real-time, automated recall propagation | GM + IBM (pilot) |
| Tier-N Visibility | Permissioned Ledger | Visibility beyond Tier-1 | RCS Global (cobalt) |
| Logistics Tracking | IoT Oracles | Tamper-evident sensor data | Maersk TradeLens |
| Smart Payments | Smart Contracts | T+0 settlement vs. T+5 days | Marco Polo Network |
| Warranty/Service | Immutability | Anti-odometer-fraud | Volkswagen VerifyCar |
| ESG/Battery Passport | Multi-Decade Immutability | Regulatory compliance | EU Battery Reg. 2023/1542 |
| Inventory | Single Source of Truth | Reduced phantom inventory | PartStack pilots |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse *permissionless* public blockchains (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum mainnet) with *permissioned* consortia chains (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) when answering KTU questions. **Automotive supply chain deployments are 99% permissioned** because competitors (OEMs, suppliers) must be able to see data without allowing public read access.

## 2.11 Engineering Trade-Offs (For 14-Mark Critical Analysis Answers)

| Advantage | Disadvantage / Open Challenge |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Tamper-evidence | **Energy consumption** (for PoW chains — but Hyperledger uses PBFT, which is energy-efficient) |
| Multi-party trust | **Throughput bottleneck** — Hyperledger Fabric: ~3,000 TPS vs. required ~10,000+ TPS for global auto industry |
| 24/7 transparency | **Scalability of on-chain storage** — full chain grows ~1 TB/year for a mid-size OEM |
| Smart automation | **Smart contract bugs are immutable** — the 2016 DAO hack cost $50M; automotive SCs must be formally verified |
| Disintermediation | **Regulatory uncertainty** — legal status of smart contracts varies by jurisdiction (India: pending under IT Act amendments) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Use Case Mapping Matrix

## 3.1 The KTU "Problem → Solution" Analytical Framework

For any 14-mark question asking *"Explain the use cases of blockchain in automotive supply chain management,"* the KTU examiner expects a **structured 4-layer analysis** for *each* major use case. Use this template:

### Layer 1 — The Stakeholder Pain Point (1–2 marks)
Who is suffering, and what is the quantifiable cost?

### Layer 2 — Why Legacy IT Fails (2 marks)
What does the existing ERP/SAP/EDI system fail to do?

### Layer 3 — The Blockchain Mechanism (3–4 marks)
What specific blockchain feature (hash chain, smart contract, consensus) solves the problem?

### Layer 4 — Tangible Outcome (1–2 marks)
What is the measured or projected improvement?

## 3.2 Worked Example — Applying the Framework to *Anti-Counterfeit Authentication*

> **Question (Internal Choice — 14 Marks):** *"With a neat block-level functional architecture, explain how blockchain enables anti-counterfeit part authentication in the automotive aftermarket. Justify why a traditional centralized database is insufficient."*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Stakeholder Pain Point:**
The global automotive counterfeit parts market is estimated at **USD 30–45 billion annually** (Source: OECD/EUIPO, 2019). Counterfeit brake pads, airbags, and oil filters directly cause **~10% of road accidents in developing markets**, and OEMs lose brand equity and warranty revenue.

**Step 2 — Why Legacy IT Fails:**
- Centralized OEM databases are **single points of failure** and **single points of trust**.
- A malicious dealer in another country can collude with a database admin to **mark a counterfeit part as genuine**.
- Cross-border verification (e.g., a Korean OEM verifying a part in Vietnam) is impossible without API integrations, which are expensive and insecure.

**Step 3 — Blockchain Mechanism:**

The architecture has **five layers**:

$$
\text{Layer 5: Field Verification} \rightarrow \text{Layer 4: Smart Contract} \rightarrow \text{Layer 3: Blockchain Ledger} \rightarrow \text{Layer 2: IoT/QR Tag} \rightarrow \text{Layer 1: OEM Minting}
$$

- **Layer 1 (Minting):** At OEM production, each genuine part's unique ID and its secret cryptographic commitment are written to the chain via a transaction signed by the OEM's private key.
- **Layer 2 (Tagging):** A tamper-evident NFC chip is physically bonded to the part. The chip stores a hash `h = SHA-256(partID || secretKey)`.
- **Layer 3 (Ledger):** The on-chain record stores the mapping `(partID, h, status, OEMpubKey)`.
- **Layer 4 (Smart Contract):** A query function `verifyPart(partID, presentedHash)` returns `true` if and only if the presented hash matches the on-chain hash, the part is not in `RECALLED` or `DECOMMISSIONED` state, and the OEM's digital signature is valid.
- **Layer 5 (Verification):** A mechanic at a service centre taps an NFC-enabled phone, which calls the smart contract via a thin-client app. The result — "Genuine" / "Suspect" — is returned in <500 ms.

**Step 4 — Tangible Outcome:**
- **~40% reduction** in counterfeit infiltration (industry-projected benchmark).
- **Elimination of odometer and warranty fraud** (secondary effect).
- **No central server** can be compromised to mark a fake as genuine — attacker would need to corrupt 2/3 of the consensus nodes (typically 10–20 OEM/government/insurer nodes).

## 3.3 Use Case × Property × Stakeholder Mapping Matrix

This is the **single most important table** for KTU 14-mark answers. Memorize the logic.

| Use Case | Blockchain Property | Primary Stakeholder(s) | Secondary Stakeholder(s) |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|
| Provenance | Hash Chain | OEM, Tier-1 Supplier | Regulator, End Customer |
| Anti-Counterfeit | Asymmetric Crypto | OEM, Service Centre | Insurance, Customer |
| Recall Management | Smart Contract | OEM, Regulator | Customer, Dealer |
| Tier-N Visibility | Permissioned Ledger | OEM | NGO, Compliance Auditor |
| Logistics Tracking | IoT Oracle | Logistics Provider, OEM | Insurer, Customs |
| Smart Payments | Smart Contract (Escrow) | Supplier, OEM | Bank, Trade-Finance Body |
| Warranty/Service | Immutability | Customer, Insurer | Dealer, Resale Platform |
| ESG / Battery Passport | Long-Term Immutability | OEM, Battery Maker | EU Regulator, Recycler |
| Inventory Optimization | Single Source of Truth | Warehouse, Dealer | OEM Finance |

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis — Blockchain vs. Centralized Database

This comparison is **frequently asked in 7-mark sub-questions**.

| Dimension | Centralized Database (e.g., Oracle, SAP) | Blockchain (Permissioned) |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Trust Model** | Trust the central admin | Trust the consensus of N independent parties |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes — crash = data unavailability | No — distributed replicas |
| **Tamper Resistance** | Admin can modify any row | Cryptographically infeasible to modify past blocks |
| **Cross-Org Data Sharing** | Requires brittle API integration | Native — same ledger, same view |
| **Performance (TPS)** | 10,000+ | 1,000–3,000 (Hyperledger) |
| **Cost** | Low (mature tech) | Medium (consensus overhead) |
| **Best For** | Single-company, internal data | Multi-party, low-trust, audit-heavy workflows |

## 3.5 The Smart Contract Execution Lifecycle (For the Pay-Per-Milestone Use Case)

This 6-step lifecycle is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in KTU papers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Negotiation} &\rightarrow \text{OEM \& Supplier agree to a smart contract (Solidity/Go code)} \\
\text{2. Deployment} &\rightarrow \text{Contract is deployed to the permissioned chain; both parties sign the deployment} \\
\text{3. Monitoring} &\rightarrow \text{IoT oracle pushes GPS / temperature / quality data to the chain} \\
\text{4. Evaluation} &\rightarrow \text{Contract logic evaluates: IF conditions met THEN execute} \\
\text{5. Execution} &\rightarrow \text{Tokenized payment (CBDC or stablecoin) transferred atomically} \\
\text{6. Logging} &\rightarrow \text{The execution itself is logged on-chain, creating a permanent audit trail}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** When explaining smart contracts in a KTU answer, always emphasize the **`IF-THEN` atomicity** and the **oracle problem** (How does a blockchain, which is deterministic, learn about real-world events? Answer: via trusted oracles like Chainlink, or trusted IoT gateways). The oracle problem is a 2024-scheme favourite.

## 3.6 Quantitative Impact Summary (For "Discuss the Economic Impact" Sub-Questions)

| KPI | Legacy (No Blockchain) | With Blockchain | Δ Improvement |
|-----|------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Recall identification time | 6–18 months | <72 hours | ~99% faster |
| Counterfeit parts in aftermarket | 8–10% of total | 2–4% (projected) | ~60% reduction |
| Trade-finance settlement (LC) | T+5 days | T+0 (real-time) | ~100% faster |
| Odometer fraud cases (EU) | ~400,000/year | Near zero | ~100% reduction |
| Supply-chain audit cost (per OEM) | ~$50M/year | ~$15M/year | ~70% reduction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
