# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"List any three use cases of blockchain technology in automotive supply chain management."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per use case with a one-line justification):**

1. **Parts Provenance & Traceability** — Enables end-to-end tracking of every component from raw material to end customer, providing immutable lineage.
2. **Anti-Counterfeit Authentication** — Uses cryptographic hashes stored on-chain to verify whether a part is genuine OEM-issued or counterfeit.
3. **Recall Management** — Smart contracts automatically notify all affected dealers, insurers, and customers in real time when a defect is identified.

> (Alternative acceptable answers: Smart-Contract Payments, Tier-N Supplier Visibility, Warranty/Service History, ESG/Battery Passport, Logistics Tracking, Inventory Optimization.)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between a permissioned and a permissionless blockchain. Which type is preferred for automotive supply chain applications, and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Permissionless (e.g., Bitcoin) | Permissioned (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Access | Anyone can join & validate | Pre-approved participants only |
| Consensus | Proof of Work / PoS | PBFT, Raft |
| Throughput | Low (~7 TPS for BTC) | High (~3,000 TPS) |
| Privacy | Fully public | Confidential channels |

**Preferred for automotive supply chain: Permissioned.**
**Reason (1 mark):** Automotive data is commercially sensitive and competitors (OEMs, Tier-1 suppliers) cannot operate on a fully public ledger. Permissioned chains also deliver the throughput (~3,000 TPS) needed for industrial-scale transactions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Internal Choice Q1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block-level diagram, explain how blockchain enables **real-time recall management** in the automotive industry. Compare its performance with the traditional ERP-based recall process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **smart contracts** in automating **pay-per-milestone payments** in the automotive supply chain. What is the "oracle problem" and how is it addressed in such deployments?

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Recall management refers to the process of identifying, notifying, and remediating vehicles affected by a manufacturing defect. A typical OEM recall affects millions of vehicles across 50+ countries and historically takes 6–18 months to complete.

**Legacy ERP-Based Recall Process (2 marks):**
- OEM identifies defect → manually extracts batch serial numbers from MES (Manufacturing Execution System).
- Cross-references batch IDs with VIN database (often incomplete across geographies).
- Sends postal/email notifications to each dealer individually.
- Dealer checks VIN at service intake (often skipped).
- Average recovery: 60–70% of affected vehicles; average time: 6–18 months.

**Blockchain-Enabled Recall Process (3 marks):**
1. **Defect Logging:** OEM writes a `RECALL_EVENT` transaction to the permissioned chain, signed with OEM's private key, referencing the defective batch ID.
2. **Smart Contract Propagation:** The contract scans all downstream custodian records (dealers, insurers, service centres) associated with that batch and automatically triggers a multi-party notification.
3. **Customer Self-Verification:** End customers can verify via a public portal/app — no need to wait for postal mail.
4. **Real-Time Remediation Tracking:** As parts are replaced, the new replacement part ID is logged on-chain, giving OEM a live dashboard of recall completion percentage.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Legacy ERP | Blockchain |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Time to notify all parties | 2–8 weeks | Seconds |
| Recall completion visibility | Lagging (months) | Real-time |
| Cross-border coordination | Manual | Automated |
| Audit trail | Fragile | Immutable |

---

### Model Solution to Question B-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Smart Contracts in Pay-Per-Milestone (3 marks):**
A smart contract is a self-executing program deployed on the blockchain that runs `IF-THEN` logic deterministically. In the automotive supply chain, a typical milestone is "100 brake calipers delivered to OEM Plant A, Gurugram, with quality certificate X." The contract holds payment in escrow; upon verified delivery, it atomically releases the payment to the supplier's wallet. This reduces settlement from T+5 days (paper LC) to T+0 seconds, eliminates reconciliation disputes, and removes the need for a bank as a trusted intermediary.

**The Oracle Problem (3 marks):**
A blockchain is a **closed, deterministic system** — it cannot natively access external (off-chain) data such as GPS coordinates, temperature readings, or quality certificate hashes. This is called the **oracle problem**.

**Solution:** Trusted oracles (e.g., Chainlink, Hyperledger Oracles, enterprise IoT gateways) act as secure bridges. In an automotive deployment, an IoT sensor on a shipping container cryptographically signs a temperature reading and pushes it to the oracle, which then writes the data on-chain. The smart contract can now safely read this data and trigger the payment.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Smart contracts with oracle integration transform the automotive supply chain from a "trust-the-bank" model to a "trust-the-code" model, with measurable improvements in speed, cost, and dispute resolution.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Internal Choice Q2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Parts Provenance and Traceability** as a use case of blockchain in the automotive industry. How does it differ from a traditional ERP-based tracking system?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the use of blockchain in creating an **EV Battery Passport** for compliance with the EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542. What supply chain data points must be recorded on-chain?

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Parts provenance refers to the ability to trace every component in a vehicle back to its raw material origin through every transformation event (forging → machining → assembly → integration).

**Architecture (3 marks):**
- At the Tier-N supplier (e.g., steel mill), each batch is assigned a **Digital Twin ID** and a cryptographic hash is written to the chain.
- As the part moves through Tier-(N-1), Tier-2, Tier-1, and OEM, each transformation event is signed and appended.
- The blockchain stores only hashes of supporting documents (mill certificates, quality reports); full documents live in off-chain IPFS or enterprise databases.
- Querying is done via smart contract that traverses the Merkle tree of hashes in O(log n).

**Difference from ERP (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ERP | Blockchain |
|--------|-----|------------|
| Multi-party trust | Requires API integration between competitors | Native, single source of truth |
| Tamper resistance | Admin can edit | Cryptographically infeasible |
| Lifetime | ~10 years | Permanent (decades — needed for warranty, recycling) |
| Cross-border visibility | Brittle | Seamless |

---

### Model Solution to Question B-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Regulatory Context (2 marks):** The EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542 mandates a **digital battery passport** for all EV batteries sold in the EU from **February 2027**. The passport must accompany the battery through its entire lifecycle (manufacturing → first use → second life → recycling) — a span of 20+ years.

**Data Points to be Recorded On-Chain (3 marks):**
1. **Provenance data** — Origin of cobalt, lithium, nickel, graphite (for conflict-mineral compliance).
2. **Carbon footprint** — kg CO₂eq per kWh of capacity (manufacturing + raw materials).
3. **Recycled content** — Percentage of recycled cobalt, lithium, nickel, lead.
4. **Performance & durability** — Capacity fade over cycles, state of health.
5. **Compliance certificates** — UN 38.3 transport test, IEC 62133 safety, REACH chemical compliance.

**Why Blockchain is the Only Viable Technology (2 marks):**
- The data must persist **20+ years** — longer than most companies' ERP lifecycles. A blockchain's append-only, distributed nature survives corporate mergers, bankruptcies, and software obsolescence.
- Multiple independent parties (miner, refiner, cell maker, OEM, recycler, EU regulator) must write to and read from the same data set **without trusting a single central administrator**.
- Smart contracts can automatically enforce compliance (e.g., revoking the passport if a battery is shipped to a non-compliant recycler).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Module 1 (Blockchain fundamentals — hashing, consensus) with Module 2 (Use cases).** The question is about *application*, not re-deriving SHA-256. Spend ≤20% of your answer on blockchain basics; ≥80% on the automotive use case.
> 2. **Do NOT list use cases without explaining "Why blockchain and not a database?"** — This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Do NOT cite made-up project names or statistics.** If you cite Renault Xcevia, BMW PartChain, Maersk TradeLens — these are real. If you invent a project, you will be marked down.
> 4. **Permissionless vs. Permissioned confusion** — Automotive uses **permissioned** (Hyperledger) chains. Citing Bitcoin/Ethereum as the deployment is a factual error.
> 5. **The oracle problem is the most-missed concept** in smart-contract answers. If your answer to a smart-contract question omits the oracle problem, expect a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memorization Block — Read this 3 times before the exam.**

- [ ] **9 Canonical Use Cases:** Provenance, Anti-Counterfeit, Recall Mgmt, Tier-N Visibility, Logistics Tracking, Smart Payments, Warranty/Service, ESG/Battery Passport, Inventory Optimization.
- [ ] **5 Blockchain Properties for ASCM:** Immutability, Provenance, Transparency, Smart-Contract Automation, Disintermediation.
- [ ] **Why blockchain, not database?** Multi-party trust + tamper-evidence + cross-border auditability + decades-long persistence.
- [ ] **Permissioned > Permissionless** for automotive. Cite Hyperledger Fabric / Quorum / Corda.
- [ ] **The Oracle Problem** is mandatory in any smart-contract answer.
- [ ] **Smart Contract Lifecycle (6 steps):** Negotiation → Deployment → Monitoring → Evaluation → Execution → Logging.
- [ ] **Key Real-World References:** BMW PartChain, Renault Xcevia, Maersk TradeLens, Volkswagen VerifyCar, EU Battery Reg. 2023/1542, EU General Safety Reg. 2019/2144.
- [ ] **Key Industry Numbers (memorize as ballparks):** ~25,000 parts, ~2,300 suppliers, 70+ countries per vehicle; ~$30–45B counterfeit market; 6–18 month legacy recall time → <72 hours with blockchain.
- [ ] **Bloom's Level:** This is an L3 (Apply) topic. Your answer must *apply* blockchain concepts to the automotive domain — not re-define blockchain.
- [ ] **Mandatory Diagram Component:** Always include a Mermaid or hand-drawn block diagram in a 14-mark answer. Examiners allocate ~2 marks for the visual.
- [ ] **Mandatory Closing Statement:** End every 14-mark answer with a 2-line summary that addresses **"Why this use case is strategically critical for the future of the automotive industry."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain for Vehicle Manufacturing and Assembly Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blockchain for Vehicle Manufacturing and Assembly Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockchain for Vehicle Manufacturing and Assembly Processes** is defined as the application of distributed ledger technology (DLT) to create an immutable, cryptographically-secured, and consensus-validated digital record of every event, transaction, and state-change occurring during the design, sourcing, fabrication, sub-assembly, final assembly, and quality verification of a motor vehicle. Each component, sub-system, and the complete vehicle is assigned a **unique cryptographic identity (Vehicle Identification Number / VIN-linked hash)** that is anchored to an on-chain record, enabling end-to-end provenance tracking, tamper-proof bill-of-materials (BOM) management, automated quality assurance via smart contracts, and rapid root-cause analysis in the event of a recall.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic sits at the intersection of **Industry 4.0**, **Cyber-Physical Production Systems (CPPS)**, and **Distributed Systems**, treating the modern automotive factory floor as a trustless, multi-stakeholder environment where OEMs, Tier-1/Tier-2 suppliers, logistics providers, regulators, and dealers all require a single source of truth.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vehicle's Digital Passport"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Car's "Lifetime Passport"**
> Imagine every car that rolls off the assembly line is issued a **tamper-proof passport** at birth (the stamping of the VIN on the frame). Every station it passes through — engine mating, painting, wheel alignment, ECU flashing, quality inspection, dealer handover — **stamps that passport with a date, a signature, and a photo of the work done**. No mechanic, no dealer, no regulator can later erase or alter a stamp. If a bolt was over-torqued at station 14 on Tuesday, that fact is sealed forever, and a recall notice can be sent to **every passport** affected by that exact batch of bolts in minutes — not weeks.

This is precisely the role of blockchain in manufacturing: it converts the historically *opaque, paper-based, siloed* factory data into a **transparent, append-only, cryptographically verifiable** historical record shared across all participating nodes of the automotive value chain.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Industry Pain Point (Pre-Blockchain) | Blockchain-Enabled Solution |
|---|---|
| Counterfeit parts entering the BOM | Cryptographic component fingerprints on-chain |
| Multi-week recall campaigns | Sub-second batch-level traceability |
| Disputes between OEM and Tier-1 suppliers | Self-executing smart-contract SLAs |
| Loss of quality data between shifts | Immutable shift-handover ledger |
| Lack of regulator visibility | Permissioned chain with read access for RTO/ISI |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Questions on this topic frequently test the student's ability to *map a real manufacturing process to a blockchain workflow* — not to write code. Memorize the **mapping of physical factory events to on-chain transactions**.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Reference Values (Bolded)

- **Industry Standard Reference:** The **ISO 23247** (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing) and **ISO/SAE 21434** (Automotive Cybersecurity) are the two governing standards students must cite.
- **Typical Permissioned Blockchain Throughput in Assembly Lines:** **1,000 – 5,000 TPS** (Hyperledger Fabric benchmarks).
- **Average Block Finality on Assembly Floor:** **\< 2 seconds** for permissioned chains (Quorum / Hyperledger Besu).
- **Recall Traceability Time Reduction:** From **~6 weeks (paper-based)** to **\< 2 minutes (blockchain)** — cited in MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative) pilots.
- **Reference Consortium:** **MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative)** — founded by BMW, Bosch, Ford, GM, and Renault.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Architectural Model for Blockchain in Manufacturing

The integration of blockchain with vehicle manufacturing is best understood as a **five-layer stack**. Each layer has a specific responsibility, and a KTU 2024 answer is considered incomplete without identifying the layer being discussed.

### Layer 1 — Physical / Device Layer (The Factory Floor)
- Industrial **IoT (IIoT) sensors**, **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**, **Robotic Arms (e.g., KUKA, FANUC)**, **Computer Vision Systems**, and **RFID / NFC tag readers** capture raw event data.
- Each device has a **unique device identity (DID)** registered on-chain.
- Events: *bolt torqued to 120 Nm*, *paint coat thickness = 110 µm*, *ECU firmware version flashed = v3.2.1*.

### Layer 2 — Edge / Gateway Layer
- **Edge gateways (e.g., Siemens Industrial Edge, AWS Greengrass)** aggregate sensor data, perform local validation, sign it cryptographically, and forward it upward.
- Implements the **"Sign at the Edge"** principle to prevent data tampering between sensor and ledger.

### Layer 3 — Blockchain / DLT Layer
- A **permissioned blockchain** (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum, or Corda) maintains the **immutable ledger** of all manufacturing events.
- **Consensus mechanism:** Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) or Raft — *not* Proof of Work, because latency on an assembly line must be \< 2 s.
- **Channels** segregate confidential data between competing suppliers sharing the same network.

### Layer 4 — Smart Contract / Business Logic Layer
- **Chaincode** (in Hyperledger) or **Solidity contracts** enforce business rules:
  - *Auto-reject a sub-assembly if torque value is outside 115–125 Nm.*
  - *Release payment to Tier-1 supplier only when QC smart contract signs off.*
  - *Trigger recall event if defect hash matches a known faulty batch.*

### Layer 5 — Application / Visualization Layer
- **OEM dashboards, dealer portals, regulator viewers, customer-facing vehicle history apps.**
- Provides **read-only views** of the on-chain vehicle history (the digital passport concept from Section 1.2).

---

## 2.2 Core Operational Workflow — The "Digital Birth Certificate" of a Car

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies the following **six-stage canonical workflow** that students must reproduce in exam answers:

1. **Stage 1 — VIN Minting (Genesis Block per Vehicle):** When a vehicle is allocated a production slot, a **genesis transaction** is created on-chain containing the VIN, target model, plant ID, and production schedule.

2. **Stage 2 — Component Ingestion (Supplier Batches):** Each inbound component (engine block, ECU, airbag module) is scanned at the goods-inward bay. Its **manufacturer ID, batch number, lot code, and digital twin hash** are written to the chain as a new transaction linked to the VIN.

3. **Stage 3 — Sub-Assembly Events:** Torque events, weld-spot counts, and paint parameters are recorded as discrete transactions at each station. The chain grows linearly with the number of stations the vehicle passes through (typically **200–400 stations** in a modern passenger car plant).

4. **Stage 4 — Final Assembly & EOL Testing:** End-of-Line (EOL) test results (brake test, headlight alignment, OBD-II scan) are immutably stored. The **Vehicle Birth Certificate** smart contract executes and emits an **NFT** (or on-chain certificate) representing the finished car.

5. **Stage 5 — Logistics & Dealer Handover:** As the vehicle is loaded onto a truck, train, or ship, custody-transfer events are written. The dealer receives a **chain-verified provenance bundle** at delivery.

6. **Stage 6 — Recall & Service Loop:** Throughout the vehicle's life, every service event, software update, and recall is appended to the same chain, creating a **lifelong maintenance history**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept / Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Because Module 2 of PEAUT751 is conceptual (not mathematical), the "Formula Sheet" below summarizes the **canonical definitions, threshold values, and design equations** that examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim.

| # | Concept / Equation | Definition | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transaction Throughput** $T = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{t_{finality}}$ | Number of manufacturing events finalized per second | TPS (Transactions per second) |
| 2 | **Cryptographic Hash Linkage** $H_{n+1} = \text{SHA-256}(H_n \Vert \text{MerkleRoot}_n \Vert t_s \Vert \text{Nonce})$ | Each new manufacturing block references previous block hash | 256-bit digest |
| 3 | **Consensus Finality** $t_f \le 2\,\text{s}$ | Maximum acceptable block finality on moving assembly line | Seconds |
| 4 | **Recall Latency** $t_r^{blockchain} \ll t_r^{paper}$ | Paper-based recall: ~6 weeks; Blockchain: \< 2 min | Time |
| 5 | **Component Provenance Depth** $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | Sum of all upstream tiers (Tier-1, Tier-2, …, Tier-n) traced | Discrete levels |
| 6 | **Fault Tolerance** $f \le \dfrac{n-1}{3}$ | Maximum Byzantine faulty nodes tolerated (PBFT) | Integer count |
| 7 | **Block Size for Assembly Event** $B \approx 2\,\text{kB}$ (avg) | Typical payload for a single station event | kilobytes |
| 8 | **Network Topology** | Permissioned, consortium-governed | Categorical |
| 9 | **Identity Standard** | **W3C DID (Decentralized Identifier)** | Standard |
| 10 | **Data Format Standard** | **GS1 EPCIS** for supply-chain events | ISO/IEC standard |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How Blockchain Is Used |
|---|---|
| **OEM (e.g., BMW, Mercedes-Benz)** | "PartChain" tracks every component from raw material to dealership; Renault's "XCEED" verifies compliance of 1,000+ parts per vehicle. |
| **EV Battery Provenance** | Cobalt, lithium, and nickel sourcing is logged immutably to satisfy **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** and to prove ethical mining. |
| **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV)** | Over-the-air (OTA) firmware updates are signed and versioned on-chain, preventing rollback attacks. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Indian **AIS-155** and European **UNECE WP.29** cybersecurity regulations can be auto-validated via smart contracts reading on-chain data. |
| **Insurance & Usage-Based Pricing** | Insurers read on-chain odometer and driving-style data directly, eliminating odometer fraud. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Whenever a PartChain-style question appears, always mention the **GS1 EPCIS standard** for event capture and **Hyperledger Fabric** as the reference platform.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of a Manufacturing Blockchain Workflow

## 3.1 Scenario: Tracking a Single Axle Assembly from Press Shop to Final Fitment

We will now walk through — in **9 explicit, exhaustive steps** — how a single front-axle beam is registered, processed, and ultimately fitted to a vehicle, with each event becoming an immutable blockchain transaction. This is the type of detailed trace a 14-mark KTU question requires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** OEM "AutoKTU Motors" operates a plant in Chennai. The Tier-1 supplier "ForgingsIndia Pvt Ltd" ships axle beams. The blockchain network is **Hyperledger Fabric v2.5** with three organizations: **AutoKTU (OEM)**, **ForgingsIndia (Supplier)**, and **AQA-Cert (Quality Auditor)**.

---

### Step 1 — Supplier Side: Generating the Component Digital Identity

The forging press at ForgingsIndia stamps a **2D Data Matrix code** onto the axle beam containing:
- **Part Number (P/N):** AX-FRT-LH-7740
- **Batch ID:** B-2025-Q3-1187
- **Manufacturing Date:** 2025-09-12
- **Forging Die ID:** DIE-44A

The supplier's edge gateway computes a **SHA-256 hash** of this metadata and stores the result locally before transmission:

$$
H_{component} = \text{SHA-256}\left( \text{P/N} \Vert \text{BatchID} \Vert \text{Date} \Vert \text{DieID} \right)
$$

The hash is **256 bits (64 hex characters)**. The raw data stays off-chain; only the hash and a **pointer URI (e.g., IPFS QmXyZ...)** are written to the chain.

---

### Step 2 — Chain Submission: Creating the Genesis Transaction for the Component

The supplier's chaincode (smart contract `ComponentRegistry`) is invoked:

```
Invoke: RegisterComponent(P/N, BatchID, Date, DieID, H_component, IPFS_URI)
```

The transaction is **proposed** to the ordering service. A block is created containing this single transaction. After **PBFT consensus** across the 3 organizations, the block is committed to every peer's ledger.

**Time to finality:** $t_f \approx 1.2\,\text{s}$ (typical for Fabric with Raft consensus).

---

### Step 3 — Logistics Transit: Custody Transfer

The axle beam is loaded onto a truck. A **GPS-enabled IoT seal** on the container periodically signs and broadcasts location events. Each event is a separate small transaction:

$$
\text{Tx}_{\text{custody}}^{i} = \text{ECDSA-Sign}_{\text{seal}} \left( \text{Latitude} \Vert \text{Longitude} \Vert t_i \Vert \text{TamperFlag}_i \right)
$$

If the tamper flag is set at any point, a **smart contract `CustodyMonitor`** automatically emits an event notification to the OEM and the supplier, both of whom can act on it via pre-agreed SLAs.

---

### Step 4 — Goods-Inward Inspection at OEM Plant

On arrival at AutoKTU's goods-inward bay, the axle beam's 2D code is scanned. The receiving station **re-computes** the hash and compares it with the on-chain value:

$$
H_{computed} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{on\text{-}chain}
$$

| Comparison Outcome | Smart Contract Action |
|---|---|
| Hash matches | Receipt transaction written; payment to supplier released via chaincode `AutoPay` |
| Hash mismatch | Component flagged "suspect counterfeit"; supplier's quality score downgraded; entire batch quarantined for forensic review |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Counterfeit axles are a leading cause of fatal front-axle failures in mid-segment vehicles. On-chain hash verification reduces this risk from "trust the paperwork" to "trust the math."

---

### Step 5 — Sub-Assembly Station: Front Axle Sub-Assembly Build

The axle beam is mated with the knuckle, hub, and brake rotor at station **FAS-07**. The robotic torque tool applies **120 Nm ± 5 Nm** at 4 bolt locations. Each torque event is signed by the tool's hardware security module (HSM):

$$
\text{Tx}_{torque}^{j} = \text{HSM-Sign}\left( j \Vert 120.3\,\text{Nm} \Vert t_j \Vert \text{OperatorDID}_j \right), \quad j \in \{1,2,3,4\}
$$

The `QualityGate` smart contract evaluates the 4 transactions:

$$
\text{QualityResult} = \begin{cases}
\text{PASS} & \text{if } 115 \le T_j \le 125 \text{ Nm } \forall j \\
\text{FAIL} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

If PASS, a **sub-assembly completion event** is emitted and linked to the parent VIN. If FAIL, the sub-assembly is **routed to rework bay**, and a non-conformance (NCR) record is anchored on-chain.

---

### Step 6 — Body Shop Integration: Linking Component to Vehicle VIN

When the front axle sub-assembly is married to the body-in-white at the **marriage station**, a single composite transaction is written:

$$
\text{Tx}_{marriage} = \text{HashLink}\left( \text{VIN}_{\text{vehicle}}, \text{H}_{axle}, \text{H}_{body}, t_{marriage} \right)
$$

From this point onward, **every event in the vehicle's life** is queryable by traversing the linked hashes backward — the **blockchain equivalent of a doubly linked list** of provenance.

---

### Step 7 — End-of-Line (EOL) Verification: Final Vehicle Birth

After the EOL test (brake dynamometer, headlight aim, OBD-II fault scan, ADAS calibration), the `VehicleBirthCert` smart contract executes. It verifies that:

1. **All mandatory BOM components have on-chain provenance records.**
2. **All quality gates have PASS status.**
3. **No outstanding NCRs exist for this VIN.**

If all three conditions are satisfied:

$$
\text{Output: NFT}_{\text{vehicle}} = \text{Mint}\left( \text{VIN}, \text{H}_{birth}, \text{Timestamp}, \text{PlantID} \right)
$$

A non-fungible token (or equivalent on-chain certificate) is minted. The vehicle is now a **"blockchain-native digital asset"** that can be transferred, financed, insured, or recalled via on-chain logic.

---

### Step 8 — Dealer Handover and Ownership Transfer

The dealer invokes a `TransferOwnership` smart contract that:
- Verifies the buyer's KYC (off-chain attestation referenced on-chain via a verifiable credential).
- Transfers the NFT to the buyer's wallet.
- Emits a `HandoverComplete` event to the OEM's CRM and the RTO's registration system.

This eliminates **title fraud** — historically one of the largest used-car market problems in India.

---

### Step 9 — Recall Scenario: The "Moment of Truth" for Blockchain

> [!NOTE]
> **Recall Event:** Six months later, ForgingsIndia's Die-44A is found to produce axles with subsurface cracks. The defect hash is $H_{defect}$.

The OEM invokes the `BatchRecall` smart contract:

$$
\text{Matched VINs} = \text{Query}\left( \text{VIN} : H_{component} \in \text{Batch}(B\text{-}2025\text{-}Q3\text{-}1187) \right)
$$

Every VIN married to a component from this batch is identified in **\< 2 minutes**. The system automatically:
1. Sends recall notifications to each vehicle owner's registered contact.
2. Blocks OTA firmware updates until the recall service is acknowledged.
3. Books a service appointment at the nearest dealer.
4. Releases warranty reserve to the dealer on completion of the fix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare with the legacy process:** A paper-based recall would require **5–6 weeks** of dealer outreach, with typically only 60–70% compliance. Blockchain-based recall achieves **near 100% compliance** because non-compliant vehicles are flagged at the next service visit and even via OTA checks.

---

## 3.2 Summary of the Analytical Matrix

| Step | Physical Event | On-Chain Artifact | Smart Contract Invoked | Finality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forging press stamps part | Component hash + IPFS pointer | `ComponentRegistry` | 1.2 s |
| 2 | Submit to ledger | Genesis block (component) | `ComponentRegistry` | 1.2 s |
| 3 | Truck transit | Custody events | `CustodyMonitor` | 1.0 s |
| 4 | Goods inward scan | Receipt + auto-payment | `AutoPay`, `VerifyHash` | 1.5 s |
| 5 | Torque at FAS-07 | 4× torque Txs + PASS/FAIL | `QualityGate` | 1.2 s |
| 6 | Marriage to body | Composite hash link | `VehicleAssembly` | 1.0 s |
| 7 | EOL test | NFT minted | `VehicleBirthCert` | 2.0 s |
| 8 | Dealer delivery | NFT transfer | `TransferOwnership` | 1.5 s |
| 9 | Recall event | Affected VIN list | `BatchRecall` | \< 2 min total |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
