# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**Explain with an example how blockchain can be used to detect counterfeit components in an automotive assembly line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Each component entering the assembly line is tagged with a **unique identifier (e.g., 2D Data Matrix code)** containing its part number, batch ID, and manufacturing date. The supplier's edge gateway computes a **SHA-256 hash** of this metadata and writes it to a permissioned blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric) before shipment. **Example:** When a forged axle beam arrives at the OEM's goods-inward bay, the receiving station re-computes the hash and compares it with the on-chain value. If the hashes match, the component is genuine and payment is auto-released via smart contract; if they do not match, the component is flagged as counterfeit, the supplier's quality score is downgraded, and the entire batch is quarantined. This eliminates the need to *trust the paper certificate of origin* and replaces it with *mathematical trust*.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**List any three benefits of using smart contracts in the vehicle assembly process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Automated Quality Gate Enforcement:** A smart contract can reject a sub-assembly if a torque value falls outside the tolerance window (e.g., 115–125 Nm), removing human discretion from quality decisions.

2. **Self-Executing Supplier SLAs:** Payment to a Tier-1 supplier is released automatically the moment a QC smart contract signs off, eliminating invoice disputes and reducing payment cycle from ~45 days to ~1 day.

3. **Instant Recall Traceability:** When a defect batch is identified, a `BatchRecall` smart contract can query the entire on-chain history in under two minutes and notify every affected vehicle owner — a task that traditionally takes 5–6 weeks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> The most common reason students lose 4–5 marks on this topic is **describing blockchain features in general (e.g., "it is decentralized, immutable, secure") without mapping them specifically to a manufacturing process**. Always tie every general feature to a *concrete factory-floor event*. A 14-mark answer should reference at least **two smart contracts, three on-chain transaction types, and one real OEM case study (BMW PartChain / Renault XCEED / MOBI)** to score full marks.

---

### Question A. [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option A]
**(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the **five-layer reference model** for integrating blockchain with a vehicle manufacturing plant. Discuss the role of each layer with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **end-to-end workflow of tracking a single component** (e.g., an axle beam) from supplier forging to final vehicle fitment, highlighting the smart contracts invoked at each stage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The five-layer reference model is illustrated in the diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student is expected to reproduce the following structured explanation:

**Layer 1 — Physical / Device Layer (1 mark):**
This layer comprises the IIoT sensors, PLCs, robotic arms, RFID readers, and computer-vision cameras that generate raw manufacturing data. Example: a KUKA robotic arm reports its torque-application events to the edge gateway.

**Layer 2 — Edge / Gateway Layer (1 mark):**
Edge gateways (e.g., Siemens Industrial Edge) perform local validation, sign the data cryptographically, and forward only the signed events to the blockchain layer. This prevents tampering during transit.

**Layer 3 — Blockchain / DLT Layer (2 marks):**
A **permissioned blockchain** (Hyperledger Fabric or Quorum) maintains the immutable ledger. Consensus is achieved via PBFT or Raft, achieving sub-2-second finality. **Channels** are used to segregate confidential data between competing suppliers sharing the same network. The ordering service sequences transactions into blocks.

**Layer 4 — Smart Contract Layer (1.5 marks):**
Chaincode enforces business rules — auto-payment, quality gate evaluation, recall matching, and ownership transfer. Each smart contract is a deterministic program executed on every peer.

**Layer 5 — Application / Visualization Layer (1.5 marks):**
Read-only dashboards for OEM quality teams, dealer portals, regulators (RTO / ARAI), and customer-facing "vehicle passport" mobile apps. These do not write to the chain; they only query it.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Stage | Physical Event | Smart Contract | On-Chain Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forging at supplier | `ComponentRegistry.Register` | Component hash + IPFS URI |
| 2 | Loading onto truck | `CustodyMonitor.Log` | Custody transfer event |
| 3 | Goods-inward scan | `VerifyHash.Match` | Receipt or counterfeit flag |
| 4 | Sub-assembly torque | `QualityGate.Evaluate` | PASS / FAIL decision |
| 5 | Marriage to body | `VehicleAssembly.Link` | Composite hash linking component to VIN |
| 6 | End-of-line test | `VehicleBirthCert.Mint` | Vehicle NFT |
| 7 | Dealer handover | `TransferOwnership.Execute` | NFT transferred to buyer |

**Real-world anchor (1 mark):** BMW's *PartChain* initiative uses this exact flow for over 10 million parts annually; Renault's *XCEED* project extends it to 1,000+ parts per vehicle across 4 plants.

---

### Question B. [14 Marks — Internal Choice Option B]
**(a)** Discuss in detail how blockchain can be used to **manage a vehicle recall campaign** more efficiently than traditional paper-based systems. Include a comparison of recall latency and compliance rates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **digital twin of a vehicle on blockchain**. How does the immutability of the ledger support the trustworthiness of the digital twin? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A recall is triggered when a defect is identified in a specific component batch (e.g., airbag inflators from lot B-2024-08-998). In a **traditional paper-based system (2 marks):** the OEM must manually trace the lot to every vehicle it was fitted to by consulting shipment records, dealer invoices, and registration databases — typically a 5–6 week process. Compliance is 60–70% because notifications depend on dealer outreach and customer response.

In a **blockchain-based system (4 marks):**
1. The OEM invokes the `BatchRecall` smart contract with the defective component's batch hash.
2. The contract queries the on-chain ledger for all VINs whose marriage transaction references this batch.
3. Affected VINs are identified in **\< 2 minutes**.
4. The contract automatically:
   - Sends SMS / email / app notifications to each owner.
   - Blocks OTA firmware updates until the recall is serviced (forcing compliance).
   - Books a service appointment at the nearest dealer.
   - Releases warranty reserve to the dealer upon service completion.
   - Updates the vehicle's NFT service history with the recall fix.
5. Compliance approaches **100%** because non-serviced vehicles are flagged at every subsequent service and even at the next registration renewal.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Metric | Paper-Based Recall | Blockchain-Based Recall |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | 5–6 weeks | \< 2 minutes |
| Compliance Rate | 60–70% | \~100% |
| Cost per Vehicle Recalled | High (manual outreach) | Low (automated) |
| Audit Trail | Fragmented | Fully immutable |

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Digital Twin (2 marks):**
A **digital twin** of a vehicle is a real-time virtual replica of the physical vehicle, including its component configuration, software versions, sensor data, and maintenance history. When anchored to a blockchain, the digital twin becomes **tamper-evident**: every state-change of the physical car is mirrored as an on-chain transaction, so the twin can be cryptographically proven to be authentic.

**How Immutability Supports Trust (3 marks):**
- **Provenance:** A buyer of a used car can verify that the digital twin's mileage, service history, and accident record have not been rolled back. Odometer fraud is eliminated.
- **Software Versioning:** Every OTA firmware update is signed and versioned on-chain, preventing rollback attacks and unauthorized modifications.
- **Insurance:** Insurers read the on-chain digital twin to verify the vehicle's condition, enabling usage-based and behavior-based premium calculation.
- **Resale Value:** A vehicle with a clean, blockchain-verified digital twin commands a higher resale price (an emerging "vehicle history premium" in markets like the US and Germany).

**Reference Architecture (2 marks):**
The digital twin is stored as a **composite Merkle tree** on-chain, with leaves representing individual sub-systems (engine, brakes, ADAS, infotainment). The root hash is linked to the vehicle's NFT. Any tampering with a leaf breaks the Merkle root, making the change immediately detectable. This is the same cryptographic structure used in MOBI's Vehicle Identity Standard.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam Day):**

- **Blockchain ≠ Cryptocurrency:** Manufacturing use cases are **permissioned** (Hyperledger Fabric / Quorum), not public Bitcoin/Ethereum.
- **Consensus on the factory floor is PBFT or Raft** — never Proof of Work, because latency must be \< 2 seconds.
- **The 5-layer model** is mandatory: Device → Edge → Blockchain → Smart Contract → Application.
- **6-stage vehicle lifecycle on chain:** VIN Minting → Component Ingestion → Sub-Assembly → Final Assembly + EOL → Logistics/Dealer → Recall/Service.
- **Smart contracts are not optional** — they are the *active enforcers* of quality, payment, and recall logic. The most commonly tested contracts are: `ComponentRegistry`, `QualityGate`, `VehicleBirthCert`, `BatchRecall`, `TransferOwnership`.
- **GS1 EPCIS** is the standard for capturing supply-chain events; **W3C DID** is the standard for digital identity.
- **Merkle root anchoring** is what makes the digital twin trustworthy — any leaf change breaks the root hash.
- **Real-world anchors to cite in every answer:** BMW PartChain, Renault XCEED, MOBI consortium, EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542, UNECE WP.29.
- **Recall latency: 5–6 weeks → \< 2 minutes** — this is the single highest-impact statistic for 14-mark answers.
- **Counterfeit prevention** is achieved via re-computation and on-chain comparison of SHA-256 hashes at the goods-inward bay.
- **NFTs for vehicles** are not "fun trinkets" — they are functional digital certificates of authenticity enabling transfer of ownership, insurance, and recall management.
- **Cyber-physical integration:** Every on-chain transaction begins with a **physically signed event from an HSM-equipped tool** — this is the "sign at the edge" principle.
- **Standards triad to remember:** **ISO 23247** (Digital Twin) + **ISO/SAE 21434** (Cybersecurity) + **GS1 EPCIS** (Supply-chain events).
- **Indian regulatory context:** ARAI's AIS-155, RTO registration, and the upcoming AIS for EV battery passport are all blockchain-friendly use cases.
- **Throughput benchmark:** Permissioned blockchains in automotive plants achieve **1,000–5,000 TPS** — far higher than public chains.
- **Fault tolerance formula:** $f \le (n-1)/3$ for PBFT — examiners love testing this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enhancing Transparency and Traceability in Automotive Logistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Enhancing Transparency and Traceability in Automotive Logistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transparency** in automotive logistics refers to the **real-time, immutable, and permissioned visibility** of transactional, operational, and provenance data across all tiers of the automotive supply chain — from raw material suppliers (Tier-3/Tier-4) to Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), dealers, and end-customers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Traceability** is the **cryptographically verifiable ability** to reconstruct the complete lifecycle history of a part, sub-assembly, or finished vehicle — including its origin, custody chain, environmental conditions, quality certifications, and service events — using blockchain-anchored digital ledgers and decentralized identifiers (DIDs).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic is mapped to:
- **CO2:** *Analyze blockchain-based architectures for automotive supply chain management.*
- **Cognitive Level:** *Apply / Analyze (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy — Level 3/4).*

### 1.2 The "Why" — Problem Context

The modern automotive supply chain is a **multi-tier, globally distributed, and deeply fragmented** ecosystem. A single passenger vehicle contains **~30,000 parts** sourced from **1,500–2,500 tiered suppliers** across multiple continents. This complexity creates four critical pain points:

| Pain Point | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Counterfeit Parts** | Estimated **$3–5 billion** annual loss in automotive aftermarket |
| **Recall Ambiguity** | Inability to pinpoint exact affected sub-batch leads to over-recall |
| **Documentation Fraud** | Bill of Lading, certificates of origin, and customs docs are paper-based |
| **Lack of Cold-Chain Visibility** | EV battery cells lose performance if temperature/humidity is unmonitored |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tamper-Proof Passport"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine every car part is born with a **digital passport**. Every time the part moves — from a Korean steel mill to a German stamping plant, to a Pune assembly line, to a Kerala dealership — a **new visa stamp** is added to its passport. These stamps are **cryptographically signed** and **linked** to all previous stamps. If anyone tries to alter any past stamp, the entire passport becomes invalid instantly. This is exactly what blockchain-based traceability provides to a physical automotive component.

### 1.4 Engineering Standards & Quantitative Anchors

The following **industry-recognized metrics** are used throughout this module:

- **Block Time:** Average time to produce a new block on the chain (**~10 seconds** for Hyperledger Fabric, **~13 seconds** for practical PoW Ethereum).
- **GDPR / ISO 21434 Compliance:** Mandatory for in-vehicle and supply chain data.
- **IATF 16949:** The global quality management standard for automotive suppliers — blockchain provides **non-repudiable audit trails** aligned with this standard.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Transparency Architecture

The blockchain-enabled automotive logistics system is built on three synergistic layers:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Sensors & RFID/IoT)**
- **RFID tags**, **GPS trackers**, **temperature/humidity sensors**, and **accelerometers** generate raw event data.
- These devices are typically attached to containers, pallets, or individual high-value parts (e.g., EV battery cells, ECUs).
- The data is signed at the edge using the device's private key (Hardware Security Module — HSM).

**Layer 2 — Blockchain Layer (Distributed Ledger)**
- The signed events are bundled into **transactions**, hashed via a **Merkle Tree**, and committed to a block.
- Each block header contains: `Previous Block Hash`, `Merkle Root`, `Timestamp`, `Nonce`, and `Validator Signature`.
- Permissioned ledgers (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum) are preferred over public chains due to **GDPR compliance** and **throughput requirements** (**~3,000 TPS** for Fabric).

**Layer 3 — Application Layer (Smart Contracts & DApps)**
- **Smart contracts** (typically written in **Solidity**, **Go**, or **Chaincode**) encode business rules:
  - Auto-validate customs documents.
  - Trigger payment upon delivery confirmation (Letter of Credit on-chain).
  - Initiate recall workflows when anomaly thresholds are breached.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Cryptographic Linking

> [!NOTE]
> Each block's hash depends on the hash of the previous block. This creates a **one-way cryptographic chain** — any retroactive tampering with a historical block would require recomputing all subsequent blocks AND gaining consensus from the majority of validators. This is **computationally infeasible** in a properly decentralized network.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles the high-yield formulas and metrics for solving numerical or analytical questions on this topic. **Use `\vert` for absolute value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cryptographic Hash Function** | $H(m) = \text{SHA-256}(m)$ | Output: **256 bits** | Anchors block integrity |
| 2 | **Merkle Root Computation** | $R = H(H(A \vert\vert B) \vert\vert H(C \vert\vert D))$ | Logarithmic in $n$ leaves: $O(\log_2 n)$ | Efficient transaction verification |
| 3 | **Block Hash** | $B_n = H(B_{n-1} \vert\vert \text{MerkleRoot} \vert\vert t \vert\vert \text{Nonce})$ | Fixed 256-bit digest | Chains blocks cryptographically |
| 4 | **Consensus Latency** | $T_{\text{conf}} = k \cdot T_{\text{block}}$ | $k \approx 6$ confirmations (PoW) | Time to finality for trade finance |
| 5 | **Throughput (TPS)** | $\text{TPS} = \dfrac{N_{\text{tx}}}{T_{\text{block}}}$ | Fabric: **~3,000 TPS** | Capacity for OEM-scale logistics |
| 6 | **Recall Precision (improved)** | $P_{\text{recall}} = \dfrac{\text{Defective units identified}}{\text{Defective units produced}}$ | Blockchain uplift: **+15–25%** | Reduces over-recall costs |
| 7 | **Chain Growth Rate** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{\text{Blocks added}}{\Delta t}$ | Hyperledger: ~6 blocks/min | Data availability cadence |
| 8 | **Storage per Block** | $S_{\text{block}} = S_{\text{header}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{\text{tx}_i}$ | Header: **~80 bytes**; Tx: **~250 bytes** | Scalability planning |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the field, these formulas drive concrete engineering decisions:
- A **Tier-1 battery supplier** must size their edge gateway storage as $S_{\text{gateway}} = N_{\text{parts}} \times S_{\text{block}}$ to handle local buffering during network partitions.
- An **OEM's recall dashboard** uses $P_{\text{recall}}$ to compute **warranty cost savings** (each avoided over-recall saves **~₹45,000 per vehicle** in logistics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Physical Event to Immutable Blockchain Record

The complete transformation pipeline from a physical logistics event to a cryptographically anchored blockchain record is derived below.

**Step 1 — Event Capture at the Edge**
A GPS-equipped container leaves a Tier-2 supplier's warehouse. The IoT sensor records an event tuple:

$$
E = \{ \text{part\_id},\ \text{location},\ \text{timestamp},\ \text{env\_temp},\ \text{env\_hum} \}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Each event is a structured tuple that uniquely identifies a discrete logistics milestone (e.g., departure, transit checkpoint, customs clearance, arrival).

**Step 2 — Local Hashing & Digital Signature**
The IoT device computes a SHA-256 digest of $E$ and signs it using its private key $K_{\text{priv}}$:

$$
h_E = \text{SHA-256}(E)
$$

$$
\sigma_E = \text{ECDSA.Sign}(K_{\text{priv}},\ h_E)
$$

**Textual Logic:** Hashing compresses the event into a fixed-length fingerprint; ECDSA signing provides **authenticity** and **non-repudiation** — only the device holding $K_{\text{priv}}$ could have produced $\sigma_E$.

**Step 3 — Transaction Construction**
The signed event is wrapped into a blockchain transaction:

$$
T = \{ h_E,\ \sigma_E,\ \text{part\_id},\ \text{new\_custodian\_pub\_key} \}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The transaction now contains the cryptographic proof of the event plus the public identity of the next custodian, enabling seamless ownership transfer.

**Step 4 — Merkle Tree Aggregation**
Multiple transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ are aggregated into a Merkle tree. For four transactions, the Merkle root is:

$$
R = H\bigl( H(T_1 \vert\vert T_2) \ \vert\vert\ H(T_3 \vert\vert T_4) \bigr)
$$

**Textual Logic:** The Merkle root $R$ is a single 256-bit digest that cryptographically summarizes all transactions in the block. Any single-bit change in any $T_i$ will propagate up and change $R$ entirely.

**Step 5 — Block Header Assembly**
The block header is constructed with all required fields:

$$
\text{Header}_n = \{\ \text{PrevHash}_{n-1},\ R,\ t_n,\ \text{Nonce},\ \text{ValidatorSig} \ \}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The header binds the new block to its predecessor via $\text{PrevHash}_{n-1}$, ensuring continuity of the chain.

**Step 6 — Consensus & Block Finalization**
Validators execute the consensus protocol (e.g., **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance — PBFT** in Hyperledger). Upon agreement, the block is appended:

$$
B_n = \text{Header}_n \cup \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}
$$

$$
\text{Chain}_{n} = \{\text{Chain}_{n-1},\ B_n\}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The chain is now extended by one cryptographically validated block. Any future audit can traverse the chain backwards to reconstruct the **entire custody history** of the part.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Transparency vs. Traceability Use Cases

| Use Case | Primary Requirement | Blockchain Mechanism | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold-Chain Monitoring (EV Batteries)** | Traceability | IoT + smart contract auto-alerts | Real-time temperature breach detection |
| **Anti-Counterfeit Spare Parts** | Transparency | Public hash registry on chain | Customer scans QR → instant verification |
| **Cross-Border Customs Clearance** | Transparency | Pre-validated smart contracts | Clearance time reduced from **~7 days to <2 hours** |
| **Warranty Claim Verification** | Traceability | On-chain service log | Eliminates fraudulent warranty claims |
| **Ethical Sourcing (Cobalt, Lithium)** | Transparency | Signed CoO on chain | ESG compliance verifiable by auditors |
| **Recall Precision** | Traceability | Lot-level batch hash lookup | Surgical recall of exact sub-batches only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Blockchain Logistics Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[sup_t4 : Raw Material Supplier] --> B[sup_t3 : Component Manufacturer]
    B --> C[sup_t2 : Sub-Assembly Plant]
    C --> D[sup_t1 : Tier-1 Supplier]
    D --> E[oem : Vehicle Assembly Line]
    E --> F[dealer : Regional Dealership]
    F --> G[customer : End User]
    H[iot_sensors : GPS / Temp / RFID] --> C
    H --> D
    H --> E
    H --> F
    I[bc_ledger : Permissioned Blockchain] --> C
    I --> D
    I --> E
    I --> F
    J[reg_auth : IATF 16949 Auditor] --> I
    K[customs : Smart Customs Contract] --> I
    I --> L[recall_dash : OEM Recall Dashboard]
    L --> G
```

**Textual Logic:** The diagram maps the multi-tier supplier hierarchy, the IoT data ingress points, the central blockchain ledger, and the consumer-facing recall dashboard. Each tier writes to the same immutable ledger, giving every participant a synchronized view.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Lifecycle for a Logistics Transaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[event : IoT Event Captured] --> P2[hash_step : SHA-256 Hashing]
    P2 --> P3[sign_step : ECDSA Digital Signature]
    P3 --> P4[tx_pool : Transaction Pool]
    P4 --> P5[merkle : Merkle Tree Aggregation]
    P5 --> P6[header : Block Header Assembly]
    P6 --> P7[consensus : PBFT Consensus]
    P7 --> P8[commit : Block Committed to Chain]
    P8 --> P9[audit : Auditor / Smart Contract Read]
    P9 --> P10[recall : Recall Trigger if Anomaly]
```

**Textual Logic:** This pipeline shows the cryptographic and consensus operations that transform a raw sensor reading into a fully validated, immutable, and auditable blockchain record. The final stage connects directly to automated recall workflows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** *Define transparency and traceability in the context of blockchain-enabled automotive logistics. How does blockchain ensure both properties simultaneously?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Transparency (1 mark):** Real-time, permissioned visibility of all supply chain transactions to authorized participants. Blockchain ensures transparency by replicating the ledger across all nodes, so every participant holds an identical, up-to-date view.
- **Traceability (1 mark):** Ability to reconstruct the full lifecycle history of a part. Blockchain ensures traceability by cryptographically linking each event to the previous one via hash chains and timestamps.
- **Simultaneous Guarantee (1 mark):** Smart contracts enforce the same business rules (e.g., ownership transfer, quality checks) on the same shared ledger, ensuring that what is visible (**transparency**) is also provable historically (**traceability**).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** *List any three key benefits of using blockchain for tracking automotive spare parts in the aftermarket. Mention one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Benefit 1 (1 mark):** Anti-counterfeiting — Each genuine part is registered on-chain with a unique hash; customers can verify authenticity via QR code.
- **Benefit 2 (1 mark):** Recall precision — Lot-level traceability allows surgical recall of defective units, reducing logistics cost and brand damage.
- **Benefit 3 (1 mark):** Warranty fraud prevention — Service history is immutable, eliminating false claims.
- **Limification (Bonus):** Scalability challenges when handling billions of micro-parts and IoT data streams.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *ESE Model Pattern*

**(a) Explain the architecture of a blockchain-based automotive logistics system in detail, with reference to the physical, blockchain, and application layers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture is organized into **three tightly integrated layers**:

**1. Physical Layer (Sensors & Edge Devices) (2 marks)**
This layer comprises IoT devices — **RFID readers, GPS modules, temperature/humidity sensors, accelerometers, and tamper-evident seals**. They are mounted on containers, pallets, and high-value components (e.g., EV battery cells, ECUs, ADAS sensors). Each device uses a **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** to store private keys and sign events at the edge. Data captured includes part ID, geolocation, timestamp, and environmental parameters.

**2. Blockchain Layer (Distributed Ledger) (3 marks)**
This layer is typically a **permissioned blockchain** (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum, or Corda) because:
- It must comply with **GDPR** and OEM data privacy policies.
- It needs **high throughput (~3,000 TPS)** to handle daily logistics volume.
- It requires **known validator identities** for regulatory accountability.

Key operations:
- **Transactions** are constructed from signed IoT events.
- **Merkle trees** aggregate transactions into a single root hash.
- **Block headers** chain each new block to its predecessor.
- **PBFT or Raft consensus** finalizes the block in **sub-second latency**.

**3. Application Layer (Smart Contracts & DApps) (2 marks)**
Smart contracts (Chaincode in Fabric, Solidity in Ethereum) encode:
- **Customs clearance logic** — auto-validates HS codes and CoO.
- **Payment triggers** — Letter of Credit released on delivery confirmation.
- **Recall orchestration** — emits alerts when a defective batch hash is queried.
- **ESG reporting** — generates audit-ready provenance reports.

The three layers are **loosely coupled but cryptographically anchored**, ensuring data integrity from sensor to dashboard.

---

**(b) Discuss how blockchain enables traceability in the EV battery supply chain, from lithium mining to vehicle integration. Include the role of smart contracts. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mining & Smelting Tier (1.5 marks)**
Lithium is mined in Australia/Chile and processed in China. At each handoff, the **bill of lading, CoO (Certificate of Origin), and ESG compliance certificate** are hashed and committed to the blockchain. The smart contract validates that the CoO matches the customs declaration.

**2. Cell Manufacturing Tier (1.5 marks)**
Battery cell manufacturers (e.g., CATL, LG) attach a **digital twin** to each cell batch. The cell's **chemistry, capacity rating, and QC test results** are written on-chain. Any deviation (e.g., sub-threshold voltage) triggers an automatic alert.

**3. Module & Pack Assembly Tier (1.5 marks)**
At the pack level, multiple cells are aggregated. A **parent-child hash linkage** is created — the pack hash is computed as:

$$
H_{\text{pack}} = H(H_{\text{cell}_1} \vert\vert H_{\text{cell}_2} \vert\vert \ldots \vert\vert H_{\text{cell}_n})
$$

This allows traceability of any defective cell back to its raw material origin.

**4. Vehicle Integration Tier (1 mark)**
At OEM assembly, the battery pack is linked to the **VIN (Vehicle Identification Number)** on-chain. The vehicle now has a complete digital lineage.

**5. Smart Contract Role (1.5 marks)**
Smart contracts automate:
- **Cold-chain compliance** — alerts if temperature exceeds $40^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Warranty activation** — triggers upon first charging event recorded on-chain.
- **Recall coordination** — if a defect is identified, the contract queries all vehicles linked to the defective pack hash and emits recall notifications to the OEM dashboard and the customer app simultaneously.

The result is a **single source of truth** spanning continents, accessible to miners, manufacturers, regulators, OEMs, and end-users — all without intermediaries.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative ESE Choice*

**(a) Compare Hyperledger Fabric and public Ethereum for automotive supply chain deployment. Which is more suitable and why? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Hyperledger Fabric | Public Ethereum |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Control** | Permissioned; known identities | Permissionless; pseudonymous |
| **Throughput** | ~3,000 TPS | ~15–30 TPS (post-Merge) |
| **Finality** | Immediate (PBFT) | Probabilistic (~13 min for 99.9%) |
| **Data Privacy** | Private channels & collections | Public by default |
| **Consensus** | Pluggable (Raft, PBFT) | PoS (Casper) |
| **Governance** | Linux Foundation | Ethereum Foundation |
| **Cost Model** | Subscription / infrastructure | Gas fees (variable) |
| **GDPR Compliance** | Easier (data can be pruned) | Difficult (immutability conflict) |

**Justification (1.5 marks):**
Hyperledger Fabric is **more suitable** because:
- **Automotive supply chains** involve B2B contracts requiring **data confidentiality** (pricing, supplier lists are trade secrets).
- **GDPR/ISO 21434** mandate data deletion rights, which is incompatible with public chain immutability — Fabric's **private data collections** elegantly solve this.
- **Throughput** is critical for OEM-scale operations (millions of events/day).
- **Regulatory compliance** is easier with **permissioned validators** of known legal identity.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** For automotive logistics, **permissioned enterprise blockchains** are the industry consensus.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Merkle tree construction for a block containing 4 logistics transactions. Why is Merkle root essential for traceability? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Consider four logistics transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$. The Merkle tree is constructed as:

- **Level 0 (Leaves):** $H_1 = H(T_1)$, $H_2 = H(T_2)$, $H_3 = H(T_3)$, $H_4 = H(T_4)$.
- **Level 1 (Branches):** $H_{12} = H(H_1 \vert\vert H_2)$, $H_{34} = H(H_3 \vert\vert H_4)$.
- **Level 2 (Root):** $R = H(H_{12} \vert\vert H_{34})$.

This forms a **binary tree** with 7 total hashes for 4 transactions. The diagram should show all three levels with labeled nodes and the root $R$ included in the block header.

**Mathematical Expression (2 marks):**

$$
R = H\bigl( H( H(T_1) \vert\vert H(T_2) ) \ \vert\vert\ H( H(T_3) \vert\vert H(T_4) ) \bigr)
$$

**Why Merkle Root is Essential for Traceability (2 marks):**
1. **Efficient Verification (SPV):** A light client can prove a transaction $T_i$ is included in a block by presenting only $O(\log_2 n)$ sibling hashes, without downloading the full block. This is critical for **mobile dealer and customer apps** with limited bandwidth.
2. **Tamper Detection:** Any modification to $T_i$ changes $H_i$, which propagates to $H_{ij}$, which changes $R$. Thus, **a single hash comparison** at the block header level verifies the integrity of all transactions — enabling fast, efficient audit trails across the supply chain.
3. **Granular Traceability:** Auditors can request proof of inclusion for a specific shipment event without exposing unrelated transactions, supporting **confidentiality in a shared ledger**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse transparency with traceability.** Transparency = real-time *visibility*; Traceability = *historical reconstruction*. Examiners frequently award zero to answers that conflate the two.
> - **Avoid generic statements** like "blockchain is secure and immutable." Always specify the *mechanism* — hash chains, Merkle roots, PBFT consensus.
> - **For diagrams, label every node.** A Mermaid diagram without labels will lose **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> - **Mention specific technologies** (Hyperledger Fabric, ECDSA, SHA-256, IATF 16949) — vague answers receive partial credit only.
> - **Always tie the answer to automotive context.** Generic blockchain answers score poorly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transparency** = real-time visibility; **Traceability** = historical reconstruction — **never interchange them**.
- The **three-layer architecture** is: Physical (IoT) → Blockchain (Ledger) → Application (Smart Contracts).
- **Permissioned blockchains** (Hyperledger Fabric) dominate automotive use cases due to GDPR, throughput, and privacy.
- The **block hash** $B_n = H(B_{n-1} \vert\vert \text{MerkleRoot} \vert\vert t \vert\vert \text{Nonce})$ is the cryptographic anchor of the chain.
- **Merkle trees** enable $O(\log_2 n)$ verification — essential for lightweight recall dashboards.
- **Smart contracts** automate customs, payments, cold-chain alerts, and recall workflows.
- **Key standards:** IATF 16949 (quality), ISO 21434 (cybersecurity), GDPR (data privacy).
- **High-yield metrics to memorize:** ~3,000 TPS (Fabric), ~13 sec block time (Ethereum), 256-bit SHA-256 hash, $O(\log_2 n)$ Merkle verification.
- **Real-world adoption examples** include the MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative) consortium, IBM–Ford collaboration, and Renault's digital twin program.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always answer with **mechanism + automotive context + quantitative anchor**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain for Quality Assurance and Compliance in Automotive Manufacturing
Case Studies of Blockchain Implementation in Leading Automotive Companies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Blockchain Implementation in Leading Automotive Companies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study (Academic Definition):** A case study is an empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary phenomenon (here, blockchain deployment) in its real-life context, especially when the boundaries between phenomenon and context are not clearly evident. In the automotive blockchain domain, a case study documents the **problem statement**, the **architectural solution**, the **consortium partners**, the **consensus mechanism adopted**, the **transaction throughput achieved**, and the **measurable business outcome** of a specific blockchain project executed by an automotive Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) or Tier-1 supplier.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the case study of a blockchain deployment in an automotive firm must address the following canonical pillars:

1. **Business Trigger** – the inefficiency, fraud, recall risk, or compliance gap that justified the move from a centralized database to a distributed ledger.
2. **Platform Selection** – whether the firm used a public chain (Ethereum, Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda, IOTA Tangle, or a permissioned variant).
3. **Consortium Topology** – who runs the nodes, what the trust model is, and whether consumers are direct participants.
4. **Smart Contract Logic** – the on-chain rules that govern data immutability, dispute resolution, and access control.
5. **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** – measurable outcomes such as recall time reduction, counterfeit detection rate, or CO₂ reporting accuracy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Tamper-Evident Logbook" Analogy:** Imagine every car in the world carrying a *notarized, time-stamped logbook* that can only be written by the workshop that did the repair, only read by a permitted party (insurer, owner, regulator), and can **never** have a page torn out without the entire network noticing. The logbook is not stored in the workshop's drawer — it is replicated in thousands of drawers, all over the world, and every entry is mathematically chained to the previous one. This is exactly what blockchain offers an automotive enterprise. Each **case study** we will examine is simply a *real factory that built such a logbook and put it to commercial use.*

## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Module 2

The KTU 2024 scheme pedagogically follows the **Kolb Experiential Learning Cycle**: concepts introduced in Module 1 (fundamentals) and Module 2 (architecture) must be crystallized through real-world instantiation in Module 2's case-study section. Examiners deliberately test whether the student can map an *abstract* blockchain feature (e.g., immutability, zero-knowledge proof, Merkle root) to a *concrete* business process (e.g., spare-parts provenance, EV battery passport, odometer fraud).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Heuristic:** If the question says "Explain with a case study" or "Illustrate with an example from the automotive industry," the examiner expects **at least one named OEM**, the **problem**, the **blockchain-based solution**, and the **quantified benefit**. Memorize at least three flagship case studies (BMW PartChain, MOBI Consortium, Porsche–Xain, Renault–Circulor, Hyundai HDCO).

## 1.4 Boundaries of the Module

This module is **descriptive-analytical**, not mathematical. The KTU 2024 learning outcome for this module is:

> **CO₂:** *Illustrate the deployment of blockchain technology in real-world automotive scenarios by analysing at least three production-grade case studies and inferring the architectural choices made.*

Therefore, the notes emphasize **architectural patterns, governance models, and business KPIs** rather than cryptographic derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Analytical Framework (KTU 2024 Evaluation Lens)

To uniformly analyse any automotive blockchain case study, the KTU examiner expects a student to dissect each project under these five orthogonal layers. Memorize this table — it is the *de facto* answer skeleton for 14-mark case-study questions.

| # | Analytical Layer | Sub-questions the Examiner Asks | Engineering Insight |
|---|------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------|
| **L1** | **Trigger / Business Pain** | Why did the firm need blockchain? Could a regular database have solved it? | Highlights the *unique* value proposition of DLT (multi-party trust, immutability, auditability). |
| **L2** | **Platform / Ledger Choice** | Public, consortium, or private? Ethereum, Hyperledger Fabric, Corda, IOTA, Quorum? | Reveals the trade-off between openness, throughput, and confidentiality. |
| **L3** | **Consensus & Governance** | PoW, PoS, PBFT, Raft, PoA? Who validates? | Determines finality speed and node-trust model. |
| **L4** | **Smart Contract / Data Model** | What assets are tokenized? What metadata is on-chain vs off-chain? | Distinguishes the cryptographic anchor from bulk data (e.g., IPFS). |
| **L5** | **KPI / Outcome** | Recall time, counterfeit rate, CO₂ saved, customer NPS, transaction latency. | Validates ROI and justifies the architectural cost. |

## 2.2 High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Comparative Matrix of Marquee Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Every KTU Module 2 question is essentially a row from this matrix.** Use `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| OEM / Project | Year | Pain Point | Platform | Consensus | Key KPI Reported |
|---------------|------|------------|----------|-----------|------------------|
| **BMW PartChain** | 2019–22 | Counterfeit parts, recall traceability | Hyperledger Fabric (permissioned) | PBFT variant (Kafka-ordered) | Up to **10 tiers** of supplier visibility; recall tracing in minutes vs weeks. |
| **BMW VerifyCar** | 2017–19 | Odometer fraud in used cars | Permissioned DLT (private) | Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance family | Vehicle mileage registered on blockchain at every service. |
| **Porsche – Xain** | 2018 | Secure car access, key sharing, data monetisation | Xain (custom DAG + AI layer) | Custom proof-of-work variant | Sub-second transaction finality for in-car use cases. |
| **Renault – Circulor** | 2019–21 | Cobalt ethical sourcing for EV batteries | Hyperledger Besu (Ethereum-compatible) | PoA (QBFT / Clique) | End-to-end provenance of battery minerals. |
| **Hyundai HDCO / Hdac** | 2018–20 | In-vehicle payments, mobility rewards | Hdac (IoT-forked chain) | PBFT (transaction-finality first) | Demonstration of car-to-car micro-payment. |
| **MOBI Consortium** | 2018–present | Fragmented mobility identity, V2X data silos | Multi-DLT (Hyperledger, IOTA, Ethereum) | Heterogeneous, project-by-project | Industry-wide standards for *Vehicle Identity* and *Mobility Data Marketplace*. |
| **VW – Energy Web** | 2018–22 | EV charging settlement, grid balancing | Energy Web Chain (Ethereum fork) | PoA (Authority-round) | Peer-to-peer EV charging pilots in Germany. |
| **Mercedes – Icertis** | 2019 | Procurement contract disputes | Hyperledger Fabric | Raft | Reduction in contract cycle time. |
| **Continental – Trusted Used Car** | 2018 | Data trust in used-car resale | Permissioned DLT | BFT-class | 1,000+ data points verifiable per vehicle. |
| **Ford – MOBI partnership** | 2018–20 | Urban mobility, parking, vehicle identity | Hyperledger / IOTA | Project-specific | Pilot in *Driving Data Marketplace*. |

## 2.3 The "Why Not a Database?" Argument (Frequently Asked)

A common 3-mark question is: *"Why couldn't BMW have used an Excel sheet or a normal SQL database to track parts?"* The 4-pillar answer is:

1. **Multi-party write access** — Suppliers, OEMs, and regulators must all write; a normal DB has one admin.
2. **No single point of trust** — Suppliers don't trust the OEM's private database.
3. **Tamper-evidence** — A row in SQL can be silently edited; a Merkle-rooted block cannot.
4. **Shared ledger semantics** — All parties see the *same* cryptographic truth in real time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recall Management:** Regulators (NHTSA, UNECE) can in principle query the chain directly.
- **Insurance Telematics:** Claims adjudicated against immutable driving data, reducing disputes.
- **EV Battery Passport:** The EU Battery Regulation 2023 mandates a *digital product passport* — blockchain is the leading candidate.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Data Market:** Cars generate **~4 TB/day**; selling this to insurers/cities is a tokenized data economy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of Four Flagship Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Instruction:** Although this section is descriptive, the KTU 14-mark question expects the **same five-layer dissection** for *every* case study. Use the structured walkthrough below as a *template* that can be re-applied to any OEM project.

## 3.1 Case Study I — BMW PartChain (2019–Present)

### Step 1: Business Trigger (L1)

BMW sources components from a globally distributed supplier network with **10+ tiers of sub-suppliers**. Counterfeit and sub-standard parts (e.g., brake calipers, airbags) had caused reputational damage and expensive recalls. The existing ERP system stopped visibility at **Tier 1**.

### Step 2: Platform Choice (L2)

BMW selected **Hyperledger Fabric** under the Linux Foundation. Justifications:

- **Permissioned network** — Suppliers are KYC-verified, not anonymous.
- **Confidential channels** — Pricing data shared only between transacting parties.
- **Pluggable consensus** — Throughput tunable for industrial B2B traffic.

### Step 3: Consensus & Governance (L3)

The ordering service uses **Kafka-based crash-fault-tolerant consensus**; endorsers run a deterministic endorsement policy. The governance is a **predefined consortium** of BMW, its Tier-1s, and the standards body (Verband der Automobilindustrie — VDA).

### Step 4: Smart Contract & Data Model (L4)

The chaincode (Fabric's term for smart contract) implements:

- A **Digital Part Twin** — for every physical part, an NFT-like asset on the ledger.
- A **Material Passport** containing batch number, country of origin, recyclability, weight, and supplier signature.
- Bulk documents (test certificates, 3D models) anchored on **IPFS** with only the hash stored on-chain.

### Step 5: KPI & Outcome (L5)

- **Recall tracing time:** reduced from weeks to minutes.
- **Supplier transparency:** visibility from **Tier 1 to Tier 10**.
- **Use case extension:** tracking **battery cell provenance** for BMW's EV ramp-up.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution Cue (14-mark):** L1 + L2 + L3 + L4 + L5 = roughly 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 marks. If a student misses *KPI*, expect a -2 deduction.

## 3.2 Case Study II — MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative)

### Step 1: Business Trigger (L1)

Founded in **2018** by BMW, Bosch, Ford, GM, and Renault, MOBI addresses the fact that vehicles, mobility services, and smart-city infrastructure have *no common digital identity*. Result: data silos, no cross-OEM portability of wallet, parking, toll, and identity.

### Step 2: Platform Choice (L2)

MOBI is **platform-agnostic** — it issues *specifications* rather than code. Reference implementations use **Hyperledger Fabric, IOTA, and Ethereum**. The federation model allows OEMs to pick the DLT that suits their regulatory environment.

### Step 3: Consensus & Governance (L3)

Governance is a **multi-stakeholder consortium** with a steering committee, working groups, and a public charter. Consensus is delegated to each project deployment.

### Step 4: Smart Contract & Data Model (L4)

MOBI has published the following flagship specifications:

- **VID — Vehicle Identity** (a Self-Sovereign Identity — SSI — for the car).
- **MOBI Mobility Data Marketplace (MDM)** — tokenized exchange of trip, sensor, and V2X data.
- **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) Communication Standard** — DLT-anchored message integrity.

### Step 5: KPI & Outcome (L5)

- Adoption by **30+ OEMs and Tier-1s** globally.
- The first cross-OEM **Vehicle Identity** standard enabling, in theory, a single digital wallet usable across brands.

## 3.3 Case Study III — Porsche & Xain AG (2018)

### Step 1: Business Trigger (L1)

Porsche sought to (a) enable **temporary key sharing** for valet/delivery scenarios, (b) record driving data immutably for **insurance and resale**, and (c) explore in-car **cryptocurrency payments** at charging stations.

### Step 2: Platform Choice (L2)

Porsche partnered with **Xain AG** (a Berlin-based start-up) which operates a *custom DAG-based* blockchain optimised for **low-power, in-vehicle execution** and AI integration.

### Step 3: Consensus & Governance (L3)

A **permissioned variant** with a delegated PoW consensus that achieves sub-second finality — essential for in-car UX.

### Step 4: Smart Contract & Data Model (L4)

- **Lock & Unlock Contracts** — temporary cryptographic access delegation.
- **Driving Style Tokenisation** — on-chain record of accelerations, hard-braking, and charging cycles.
- **Off-chain AI** triggers smart-contract execution when policy conditions are met.

### Step 5: KPI & Outcome (L5)

- Sub-second **transaction finality**, demonstrated live in Panamera prototypes.
- Pilot in **stuttgart charging** micro-payments using native XAIN tokens.

## 3.4 Case Study IV — Renault & Circulor (Cobalt Tracing, 2019)

### Step 1: Business Trigger (L1)

EV batteries require **cobalt**, often mined under unethical conditions (DRC). Renault, under pressure from **OECD Due Diligence Guidance**, needed a tamper-proof audit trail of cobalt from mine → smelter → cathode → cell → pack.

### Step 2: Platform Choice (L2)

**Hyperledger Besu** — chosen for its Ethereum compatibility (smart contracts in Solidity) and its permissioned PoA consensus, which gives **transaction privacy** with **public verifiability** when required.

### Step 3: Consensus & Governance (L3)

**Proof of Authority (PoA)** with vetted validators: the auditor, the smelter, and the OEM form the validating set. Block finality is immediate.

### Step 4: Smart Contract & Data Model (L4)

- Each batch of cobalt gets a **digital twin NFT** that moves through custody events.
- **Chain-of-custody events** (e.g., "loaded at mine X", "weighed at port Y") are signed by the responsible party.
- ESG metadata (carbon footprint, child-labour audit) is attached immutably.

### Step 5: KPI & Outcome (L5)

- Renault's pilot traced **3,000+ tonnes** of cobalt with full provenance.
- Became a **template for the EU Battery Passport Regulation 2023/1542**, which will mandate similar DLT-based passports for all EV batteries sold in the EU.

## 3.5 Cross-Case Synthesis (Often Asked as a 14-Mark "Compare and Contrast")

| Comparative Dimension | BMW PartChain | MOBI | Porsche–Xain | Renault–Circulor |
|-----------------------|---------------|------|--------------|------------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Traceability | Standardisation | UX + Security | Ethical Sourcing |
| **DLT Type** | Permissioned | Heterogeneous | Custom DAG | Permissioned PoA |
| **Tokenization** | Part-twin NFT | Vehicle SSI | Access & data tokens | Material NFT |
| **Finality** | Sub-second | Project-specific | Sub-second | Instant (PoA) |
| **Public-good angle** | No (B2B) | Yes (industry) | No (Porsche-only) | Yes (ESG) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing *"blockchain = Bitcoin"* in a case-study answer. Always say *"Hyperledger Fabric is an enterprise-grade, permissioned, Ethereum-compatible (or non-Ethereum) DLT that is **not** a cryptocurrency chain."* Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BMW PartChain — Multi-Tier Supplier Network Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[OEM BMW Ordering Service] --> nodeB[Tier 1 Supplier Node]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tier 2 Supplier Node]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Tier 3 Supplier Node]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Tier N Supplier Node]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Hyperledger Fabric Channel]
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[IPFS Off-Chain Document Store]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Chaincode Smart Contract]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Digital Part Twin Ledger]
    nodeJ[Regulator NHTSA Query] --> nodeF
    nodeK[Recall Authority] --> nodeF
    nodeL[Insurance Provider Read Access] --> nodeF
```

> The diagram illustrates how a **single Hyperledger Fabric channel** acts as the cryptographic spine connecting BMW to all sub-suppliers, while bulk data is shunted to **IPFS** for cost-efficient storage. Regulators and insurers are **read-only** members of the channel.

## 4.2 MOBI Consortium — Federated Working-Group Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[MOBI Steering Committee] --> nodeB[Working Group Vehicle Identity]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Working Group Data Marketplace]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Working Group V2X Standards]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Working Group Token Economy]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Reference Implementation Hyperledger]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Reference Implementation IOTA]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Ethereum Based MDM]
    nodeD --> nodeI[DLT Anchored V2X]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Utility Token Specs]
    nodeF --> nodeK[OEM Member A]
    nodeF --> nodeL[OEM Member B]
    nodeG --> nodeM[OEM Member C]
    nodeH --> nodeN[Tier 1 Supplier]
    nodeI --> nodeO[Smart City Authority]
```

> MOBI's architecture is a **federation of working groups**, each producing open specifications. This structure explains why MOBI itself does not deploy a single chain — it coordinates many.

## 4.3 Renault Circulor — Cobalt Custody Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[DRC Cobalt Mine] -->|Custody Event 1| nodeB[Local Trader]
    nodeB -->|Custody Event 2| nodeC[Smelter]
    nodeC -->|Custody Event 3| nodeD[Refinery]
    nodeD -->|Custody Event 4| nodeE[Cathode Manufacturer]
    nodeE -->|Custody Event 5| nodeF[Cell Maker]
    nodeF -->|Custody Event 6| nodeG[Battery Pack Assembler]
    nodeG -->|Custody Event 7| nodeH[Renault EV Plant]
    nodeH -->|Custody Event 8| nodeI[End Customer Vehicle]
    nodeJ[Hyperledger Besu PoA Network] -.anchors.-> nodeA
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeB
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeC
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeD
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeE
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeF
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeG
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeH
    nodeJ -.anchors.-> nodeI
    nodeK[ESG Auditor Read Access] --> nodeJ
    nodeL[Regulator EU Battery Passport] --> nodeJ
```

> The dotted lines indicate that the *physical* custody chain is mirrored on the *digital* ledger. Each arrow crossing is a **signed, immutable, hash-anchored transaction** — making it impossible to launder conflict-mineral batches into legitimate supply.

## 4.4 In-Vehicle Blockchain Use Case (Porsche–Xain) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Driver Mobile Wallet] -->|Auth Token| nodeB[Porsche In-Vehicle Edge Node]
    nodeB -->|Verify on Xain DAG| nodeC[Temporary Unlock Smart Contract]
    nodeC -->|Authorize| nodeD[Vehicle Door Lock Actuator]
    nodeE[Telematics Sensor Array] -->|Hashed Driving Data| nodeF[In-Vehicle Local Chain]
    nodeF -->|Periodic Anchor| nodeG[Xain Mainnet DAG]
    nodeH[Insurance Smart Contract] -->|Query| nodeG
    nodeI[Charging Station Smart Contract] -->|Query| nodeG
    nodeG -->|Trigger Payout| nodeA
```

> The diagram shows how a **local in-vehicle chain** (fast, offline) periodically anchors summaries to the **mainnet DAG** — a typical **hierarchical-blockchain** design used to overcome latency and bandwidth constraints in moving vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students most commonly lose marks by (a) writing generic blockchain benefits instead of *automotive-specific* ones, (b) confusing Hyperledger Fabric with Ethereum, (c) omitting the *KPI / Outcome* layer, and (d) forgetting to name the **consortium partners**. Memorize at least **three OEM names** with their **platforms** and **one KPI each**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024]**
*"State any three business pain points in the automotive industry that justify the use of blockchain technology."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Counterfeit Spare Parts & Recall Traceability** — The global auto-parts market is plagued by counterfeits (estimated at **$45 billion annually**). A distributed ledger allows every part to carry a *digital twin* whose provenance is verifiable across all tiers of the supply chain. *(1 mark)*
2. **Odometer Fraud in Used Cars** — In Europe alone, odometer fraud costs consumers approximately **€6 billion/year**. An immutable service log on a blockchain prevents mileage rollback. *(1 mark)*
3. **Ethical Sourcing of EV Battery Minerals** — Cobalt and lithium supply chains are multi-jurisdictional; blockchain provides tamper-proof *chain-of-custody* records aligned with **OECD Due Diligence Guidance**. *(1 mark)*

*(Alternative acceptable points: Insurance telematics disputes, fragmented V2X data silos, lack of cross-OEM vehicle identity.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
*"Differentiate between permissioned and permissionless blockchains. Identify which category BMW PartChain falls into and justify."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Permissionless (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum) | Permissioned (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) |
|---------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Node Access** | Anyone can join | KYC-verified members only |
| **Consensus** | PoW / PoS — energy intensive | PBFT / Raft / PoA — energy efficient |
| **Throughput** | Low (e.g., 7–15 TPS) | High (1,000+ TPS) |
| **Confidentiality** | Pseudonymous public ledger | Private channels, data partitioning |

**BMW PartChain** is a **permissioned** Hyperledger Fabric deployment. *(1 mark)* Justification: The supplier network is a known consortium of pre-verified entities, and pricing data must remain confidential to transacting parties. *(1 mark)* A permissionless network would expose proprietary supplier information and would not provide the **sub-second finality** required for industrial B2B traffic. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **BMW PartChain** project. Discuss the business problem it addresses, the choice of blockchain platform, and the consensus mechanism used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Analyse the role of the **MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative)** consortium in standardising blockchain use across the automotive industry. List any four flagship specifications published by MOBI. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

1. **Business Problem (2 marks):**
   - Counterfeit and sub-standard spare parts in a global supply chain of 10+ tiers.
   - Existing ERP visibility capped at Tier 1 → **inability to trace defects** to their origin.
   - Need for a **multi-party trusted ledger** that no single OEM could control unilaterally.

2. **Platform Choice (2 marks):**
   - **Hyperledger Fabric** (Linux Foundation).
   - Reasons: permissioned, confidential channels, pluggable consensus, deterministic finality, no cryptocurrency volatility, modular smart contracts (chaincode).

3. **Consensus Mechanism (2 marks):**
   - **Kafka-based crash-fault-tolerant ordering** for the ordering service.
   - **Endorsement policy** executed by selected peers before commit.
   - Finality is **deterministic** (no probabilistic forks as in PoW).

4. **Additional Architectural Notes (1 mark):**
   - Off-chain storage of bulky documents on **IPFS**; only hashes anchored on-chain.
   - Digital Part Twin NFT model; Read-only access for regulators.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

1. **MOBI Genesis (2 marks):**
   - Founded in **2018** by BMW, Bosch, Ford, GM, Renault.
   - Mission: prevent fragmentation of vehicle/mobility digital infrastructure by creating *open, cross-OEM standards*.

2. **Standardisation Role (2 marks):**
   - Issues **specifications, not chains** → platform-agnostic.
   - Enables **interoperability** of vehicle identity, mobility wallets, V2X messages across brands.
   - Provides reference implementations on **Hyperledger, IOTA, Ethereum**.

3. **Four Flagship Specifications (2 marks):**
   - **VID – Vehicle Identity** (Self-Sovereign Identity for cars).
   - **MDM – Mobility Data Marketplace** (tokenized data exchange).
   - **V2X Communication Standards** (DLT-anchored message integrity).
   - **Token Economy Framework** (utility tokens for mobility services).

4. **Industry Impact (1 mark):**
   - 30+ OEM and Tier-1 members globally; first cross-OEM digital vehicle identity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *"With a neat block-level architecture diagram, explain how Renault and Circulor use blockchain to trace cobalt from mine to EV battery."* *(7 marks)*

**(b)** *"Discuss the architecture of the Porsche–Xain project. How does it achieve sub-second transaction finality inside a moving vehicle?"* *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

1. **Business Trigger (2 marks):**
   - EV battery cobalt is often mined under unethical conditions (DRC).
   - Renault's compliance with **OECD Due Diligence Guidance** and **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** requires verifiable chain-of-custody.
   - Need to prove *conflict-free* provenance to end customers and regulators.

2. **Platform Choice (2 marks):**
   - **Hyperledger Besu** (Ethereum-compatible, permissioned).
   - Combines Solidity smart contracts with **Proof-of-Authority (PoA)** consensus.

3. **Architecture (2 marks):**
   - Each batch of cobalt is tokenized as a **Digital Material Twin NFT**.
   - Custody events (mine → trader → smelter → refinery → cathode → cell → pack) are **signed and time-stamped** on-chain.
   - **Off-chain documents** (audit reports, weighbridge tickets) anchored via IPFS-style hashing.
   - **Validators:** Renault, smelter, ESG auditor (PoA set).

4. **Outcome (1 mark):**
   - Traced **3,000+ tonnes** of cobalt in pilot.
   - Directly influenced the **EU Battery Passport** standard.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

1. **Business Need (1 mark):**
   - Porsche wanted *cryptographic, temporary vehicle access* and *data monetisation* for owners.

2. **Platform — Xain AG (2 marks):**
   - Custom **DAG-based** blockchain (not a linear chain) — supports parallel micro-transactions.
   - **In-vehicle edge node** that runs a local, lightweight consensus.

3. **Sub-Second Finality Mechanism (2 marks):**
   - **Delegated Proof-of-Work variant** with AI-assisted validator selection.
   - **Hierarchical design** — a *local in-vehicle chain* processes fast events (door unlock); a *mainnet DAG* anchors summaries periodically.
   - Eliminates block-time bottleneck of Ethereum (~12 s) and Bitcoin (~600 s).

4. **Use Cases Demonstrated (2 marks):**
   - **Temporary key sharing** (valet, delivery, fleet handover) via smart contract.
   - **Driving data tokenisation** for insurance pricing and resale proof.
   - **Charging micro-payments** using native XAIN tokens.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Recall time is the most-cited KPI** in automotive blockchain case studies — memorize "from weeks to minutes."
- 🏷 **BMW PartChain + Hyperledger Fabric + PBFT/Kafka + Tier-1 to Tier-10 visibility** is a *must-remember cluster*.
- 🏷 **MOBI = consortium = standards = Vehicle Identity + MDM + V2X** — *not a single chain*.
- 🏷 **Renault–Circulor = Hyperledger Besu + PoA + cobalt traceability + EU Battery Passport precursor**.
- 🏷 **Porsche–Xain = DAG + sub-second finality + in-vehicle edge node + temporary key sharing**.
- 🏷 **Hyundai HDCO** demonstrates **in-car payments** and **IoT-friendly consensus**.
- 🏷 **VW–Energy Web** is the **EV charging and grid balancing** case study — PoA on Energy Web Chain.
- 🏷 **Mercedes–Icertis** is the **procurement contract** case study.
- 🏷 **Continental** is the **used-car data** case study.
- 🏷 Always end a case-study answer with a **KPI line** — examiners reward quantified outcomes.
- 🏷 Always **name the consensus** — never say "uses blockchain" alone.
- 🏷 Always distinguish **on-chain vs off-chain** data (bulk files on IPFS, hashes on chain).
- 🏷 The **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** mandates a *digital product passport* — a strong *future-trend* pointer for KTU 2024 questions.

> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Structure every case-study answer using the **5-Layer Framework** (L1 Trigger → L2 Platform → L3 Consensus → L4 Smart Contract → L5 KPI). This single structure is worth 3–5 marks in any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Vehicle Identities and Smart Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT751 - BLOCK CHAIN IN AUTOMOTIVE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Vehicle Identities and Smart Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Vehicle Identity (DVI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Digital Vehicle Identity (DVI)** is a cryptographically verifiable, tamper-proof, and immutable digital representation of a vehicle on a distributed ledger (blockchain), encapsulating its unique identification parameters, ownership history, technical specifications, telemetry, and lifecycle events. It acts as the vehicle's "digital passport" within a decentralized automotive ecosystem.

A DVI typically binds a real-world vehicle (identified by its **Vehicle Identification Number — VIN**, a **17-character** alphanumeric code standardized under **ISO 3779**) to an on-chain cryptographic identity. The on-chain identity is usually represented by a public key hash or a smart contract address, while the private key may be held in the vehicle's **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** or **TPM (Trusted Platform Module)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAUT751 — Module 3):**
> Digital Vehicle Identities form the foundational layer upon which all subsequent blockchain automotive services — such as usage-based insurance, automated toll collection, vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) payments, and supply-chain provenance — are constructed.

### 1.2 Smart Contracts in the Automotive Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Smart Contract** is a self-executing computer program stored on a blockchain that automatically enforces, executes, or documents the terms of an agreement between parties without requiring a trusted intermediary. In the automotive industry, smart contracts are deployed on permissioned or permissionless ledgers to automate vehicle-related transactions such as insurance claims, ownership transfers, and toll payments.

Smart contracts were conceptualized by cryptographer **Nick Szabo in 1994**, well before the invention of Bitcoin, but became practically viable only with the launch of **Ethereum in 2015** and later enterprise platforms like **Hyperledger Fabric** and **Hyperledger Indy**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Digital Vehicle Identity = Aadhaar for Cars:**
Think of a DVI like the **Aadhaar card** issued to Indian citizens by UIDAI. Just as Aadhaar provides a unique, tamper-resistant digital identity linked to biometrics and demographic data, a DVI provides a unique on-chain identity linked to a vehicle's VIN, ownership records, service history, and real-time telemetry. No two vehicles share the same DVI, and any modification (e.g., change of owner, accident report) is permanently recorded — similar to how Aadhaar authentication logs every verification event.

**Analogy 2 — Smart Contracts = A Vending Machine for Vehicles:**
A smart contract works exactly like a **vending machine**. You insert the right input (cryptocurrency payment, valid sensor data, or a digital signature) and the machine automatically dispenses the output (a service activation, an insurance payout, or a transferred ownership record). There is no human cashier, no negotiation, and no possibility of the machine "forgetting" the rules. The contract's code is the law — once deployed, it executes deterministically as written.

**Analogy 3 — Combined Workflow = DMV on Blockchain:**
Imagine if the Regional Transport Office (RTO) were replaced by an autonomous digital system. When you buy a used car, instead of standing in queues at the RTO for an **NOC (No Objection Certificate)** and re-registration, a smart contract would automatically:
1. Verify the seller's ownership via the DVI.
2. Transfer payment atomically (escrow).
3. Update the on-chain ownership record.
4. Issue a new digital registration certificate.

This is the future state envisioned by blockchain automotive consortia such as the **MOBI (Mobility Open Blockchain Initiative)**.

### 1.4 Key Standards, Constants & Identifiers

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Automotive Identity Standards:**
> - **VIN Length:** **17 characters** (since 1981, ISO 3780).
> - **VIN Structure:** WMI (3 chars) + VDS (6 chars) + VIS (8 chars).
> - **Cryptographic Hash Output:** Typically **256 bits** (SHA-256) or **160 bits** (RIPEMD-160).
> - **Public-Key Cryptography:** **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) over the **secp256k1** curve is the industry standard for blockchain-based vehicle identities.
> - **Block Time:** Ethereum ≈ **12–15 seconds**; Hyperledger Fabric ≈ **sub-second** with Raft consensus.

### 1.5 Importance in the Automotive Industry

Digital Vehicle Identities and Smart Contracts together address several pain points of the traditional automotive sector:

| Pain Point | DVI + Smart Contract Solution |
|---|---|
| Odometer fraud (affects ~10% of used cars globally) | Immutable mileage records on-chain |
| Inefficient cross-border vehicle registration | Automated, code-enforced transfer logic |
| Lack of trust in used-car history | Cryptographically verifiable provenance |
| Slow insurance claim processing (avg. 30+ days) | Smart-contract-triggered parametric payouts |
| Vehicle data silos (OEMs, insurers, repair shops) | Shared, permissioned ledger access |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Digital Vehicle Identity

A DVI is not a single record — it is a **multi-layered cryptographic construct**. Let us break it down step by step:

**Step 1 — Vehicle Onboarding (Off-Chain Anchor):**
The vehicle's physical identifiers are captured:
- **VIN:** 17-character ISO 3780 code (e.g., `1HGCM82633A123456`).
- **Engine Number, Chassis Number, ECU firmware hash.**
- **Initial odometer reading.**
- **Manufacturer digital certificate (OEM-signed X.509).**

**Step 2 — Cryptographic Key Pair Generation:**
An asymmetric key pair is generated, typically using **ECDSA over secp256k1**:
- **Private Key (sk):** Stored in the vehicle's **HSM/TPM** or in a **Secure Enclave** of the in-vehicle infotainment (IVI) system.
- **Public Key (pk):** Mathematically derived from sk; published on-chain.

**Step 3 — On-Chain Registration:**
A transaction is broadcast to the automotive blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric channel, or a public chain like Ethereum) that creates a smart contract instance representing the vehicle. The contract stores:
- `vehicleAddress` → the contract's own address (acts as the DVI).
- `vin` → the 17-character VIN (often hashed to save gas/space).
- `currentOwner` → the public key hash of the present owner.
- `metadataURI` → a pointer (e.g., IPFS CID) to the off-chain detailed vehicle document.

**Step 4 — Event Logging:**
Every lifecycle event (sale, accident, service, insurance claim) is recorded as an **immutable event log** on the ledger, indexed by the DVI.

### 2.2 Anatomy of an Automotive Smart Contract

**Step 1 — Code Authoring:**
Smart contracts for automotive use-cases are typically written in:
- **Solidity** (Ethereum, Polygon, MOBI Chain).
- **Go, Java, or JavaScript** (Hyperledger Fabric chaincode).

**Step 2 — Compilation & Bytecode Deployment:**
The source code is compiled to **bytecode** and deployed via a `CREATE` or `CREATE2` opcode, returning a deterministic **20-byte address** (on EVM chains).

**Step 3 — State Variables (Persistent Storage):**
On EVM chains, smart contract storage is a **key-value store** of **2²⁵⁶ slots**, each holding a **256-bit (32-byte) word**. For an automotive contract, state variables may include:

| State Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `bytes17 vinHash` | Hashed VIN for privacy-preserving identification |
| `address payable owner` | Current owner's wallet address |
| `uint256 mileage` | Latest odometer reading (in km, scaled by 100) |
| `uint256 lastServiceTimestamp` | Unix timestamp of last maintenance |
| `bytes32 insurancePolicyId` | Reference to active insurance policy |
| `bool isStolen` | Flag indicating theft status |
| `mapping(address => uint256) maintenanceLog` | Per-mechanic service history |

**Step 4 — Function Execution (Transaction-Triggered):**
Functions are invoked by **Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs)** or by other contracts. Each invocation requires:
- A signed transaction containing `nonce`, `gasPrice`, `gasLimit`, `to`, `value`, `data`, `v`, `r`, `s`.
- The EVM executes the bytecode, consumes gas, and updates the **Merkle Patricia Trie** storage root.

**Step 5 — Event Emission:**
Critical state changes emit **EVM events (logs)** that are stored in the **transaction receipt's bloom filter** for efficient off-chain querying.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Cheat Sheet — Memorise Before Exam Day.**
> All formulas below are commonly tested in KTU ESE questions on Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cryptographic Hash | $H(M) = h$ | $M$ = message, $h$ = fixed-length digest (256 bits for SHA-256) | Fingerprinting vehicle data (VIN, telemetry) |
| 2 | Digital Signature Generation | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(M))$ | $sk$ = private key, $\sigma$ = signature | OEM signing a vehicle birth certificate |
| 3 | Digital Signature Verification | $\text{Verify}(pk, H(M), \sigma) \in \{0,1\}$ | Returns **1** if valid, **0** if invalid | Verifying a service record's authenticity |
| 4 | Public Key Derivation (ECDSA) | $pk = sk \cdot G$ | $G$ = generator point on secp256k1 | Deriving a vehicle's on-chain address |
| 5 | Ethereum Address Computation | $A = \text{Keccak256}(pk_{x} \Vert pk_{y})[12:32]$ | Trims to last 20 bytes of the hash | Generating the DVI smart contract address |
| 6 | Transaction Hash | $T_{hash} = \text{Keccak256}(RLP(nonce, gasPrice, ...))$ | $RLP$ = Recursive Length Prefix encoding | Unique identifier of an on-chain vehicle event |
| 7 | Merkle Root | $R = H(H(A \Vert B) \Vert H(C \Vert D))$ | Pairwise hash of leaves, recursive to root | Block header integrity for vehicle data batches |
| 8 | Patricia Trie Root (State) | $R_{state} = \text{TrieRoot}(account_{1}, account_{2}, ...)$ | Maintains global state of all DVI contracts | EVM world state verification |
| 9 | Gas Consumption | $\text{TxFee} = gasUsed \times gasPrice$ | $gasPrice$ in **Gwei (10⁹ wei)** | Cost of registering a vehicle on Ethereum |
| 10 | Wei to Ether Conversion | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ wei}$ | $1$ Ether = $1{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ wei | Pricing smart contract deployments |
| 11 | Block Gas Limit (Ethereum) | $\text{Total Gas} \leq 30{,}000{,}000$ | Per-block cap (post-EIP-1559) | Maximum vehicle events per block |
| 12 | Timestamp Validity | $\text{parent.timestamp} < t_{new} \leq \text{parent.timestamp} + 15$ | Block timestamp drift tolerance | Verifying service timestamps |
| 13 | Storage Slot Cost (EVM) | $1 \text{ slot} = 20{,}000 \text{ gas (SSTORE)}$ | First-time write to a 256-bit slot | Cost of initializing a DVI contract |
| 14 | VIN Check Digit (ISO 3780) | $C = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{17} w_i \cdot v_i\right) \bmod 11$ | $w_i$ = positional weights, $v_i$ = VIN digit value | VIN validation in smart contract constructor |

> [!WARNING]

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The combination of DVI and Smart Contracts enables four major engineering applications:

1. **Supply Chain Provenance:** From raw aluminium to the assembled vehicle, every component's certificate of origin is anchored to the DVI.
2. **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI):** Telematics data (speed, mileage, G-force events) is signed by the vehicle's HSM and submitted to an on-chain smart contract that auto-adjusts premiums and triggers parametric claims (e.g., crash detection → instant payout).
3. **Automated Tolling & EV Charging:** Smart contracts enable **machine-to-machine (M2M)** micropayments without human intervention.
4. **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Energy Trading:** Electric vehicles can autonomously sell excess stored energy back to the grid via smart contracts, with the DVI as the trading identity.
